description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Freie Schule Charlottenburg is a reform-pedagogical primary and secondary school emphasizing respect for children's developmental processes, inspired by Jesper Juul, Montessori, Piaget, and Wild. Its core philosophy centers on **self-determined learning**, freedom without compulsion, no grades or classwork, and holistic development fostering inner strength, self-respect, humanity, self-motivated action, creativity, social competence, and subject competence. The school prioritizes non-directive education, individuality, sensory-motor learning through movement, and ethical/philosophical exploration without affiliation to any religion or worldview.[1][6][9]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 2002 as a parent and educator initiative, the school became independently owned in August 2010 after transitioning from the Freie Schule am Mauerpark. It serves about 25 students with 4 core learning companions (Lernbegleiter) plus additional adults, volunteers, and parents in a community-oriented environment promoting democracy and self-determination. Facilities include 600 m² of open spaces (no closed classrooms), outdoor areas, and specialized rooms: Foyer (eating, games, reading, assemblies); Werkstatt & Kreativraum (crafting, building, experiments); Musikraum; plus areas for sciences/experiments, movement/sports/role-play, arts/crafts, math/Montessori materials/languages, reading/writing/math, music, and garden/courtyard. Children freely choose activities across fluid themes.[3][6][9]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school offers grades 1-10 (currently up to grade 7) as a state-approved Ersatzschule (integrated primary and secondary, approved to class 10), with open all-day programs, year-crossing groups in early years, and English as the first foreign language. Learning is child-led via prepared environments, offer plans, projects, and daily activities like movement, experimenting, crafting, building, role-play, theater, cooking, math, writing, reading, discussions, and life skills—no fixed schedules, grades, or tests. It emphasizes sensomotoric learning, self-accessible Montessori-oriented materials, and fächerübergreifend (cross-disciplinary) pursuits based on children's interests.[2][3][5][7][8][9]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in self-chosen, interest-driven activities across open spaces: movement/turnen/sports, exploring/experimenting, crafting/pottery/building, role-play/theater, cooking, math/writing/reading, discussions, music, games, arts/malerei/basteln, sciences, languages, and garden work. Extracurriculars integrate into the day with projects, team meetings, outings, and volunteer-supported pursuits; after-school care (Hort) extends to 15:00-16:00. No structured sports/arts/exchanges mentioned, but emphasis on holistic, body-mind-spirit involvement and social learning.[3][9]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, certifications, or specific outcomes (e.g., Abitur/MSA/BBR rates) are mentioned in the content.[1-10]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Enrollment process**: Parents must engage deeply with the pedagogical concept and apply non-directive principles at home. Steps include: attending info events, submitting the Anmeldebogen (form), observation (Hospitation), and team parent interview. Contact Christine Pichel at 017663749499 or christine.pichel@freie-schule-charlottenburg.de for terms; primarily accepts first-graders, exceptions to grade 3, limited lateral entrants to grade 2 (secondary full). description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Freie Schule Pankow is a state-recognized alternative school emphasizing **freiwilligkeit (voluntariness)**, **selbstbestimmung (self-determination)**, and learning outside conventional systems, providing a safe space free from right-wing or extremist ideologies.[1][4][5] It follows the tradition of Freie Alternativschulen, prioritizing a humanistic view that grants all individuals dignity, self-determination, and lifelong education through partnerships between children and adults, fostering social relationships, identity, and creative conflict resolution.[4][6] Core principles include self-managed learning, community responsibility, and rejecting deficit-based views of children.[6][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in the early 1990s as a self-managed alternative school, it received provisional primary school approval in 1996, full recognition as an Ersatzschule in 2000, and Secondary Level 1 approval in 2015, enabling Berufsbildungsreife (BBR), erweiterte BBR (eBBR), and Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA) exams.[5] Owned by the nonprofit Freie Schule Pankow eG (cooperative of staff and parents), it serves ~120 students from Berlin districts and surroundings, with a multidisciplinary team of educators, social pedagogues, craftsmen, interns, and FSJ volunteers.[1][4][5] The new building at Grabbeallee 66, Berlin-Pankow (13156), supports freies Lernen; hours are 8:30-16:15.[1][4][7] Previously in Weissensee.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** and Grund- und Sek1-Schule (primary and secondary levels 1), it covers grades from primary (Einschulung) through 7-10, focusing on freies Lernen where students decide learning timing and topics, with adults providing frameworks.[1][2][8] Offers state-equivalent BBR, eBBR, and MSA; Abitur requires transfer (partners with Anna-Freud-Oberschule).[8] Multilingual website (German, English, Spanish, French); no specific curriculum details beyond self-directed, community-developed daily routines.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Emphasizes hands-on, self-directed activities including **ausflüge (excursions)**, nature, music (musisches), sports, crafts (handwerkliches), workshops, and project weeks.[4] Features Lernräume (learning spaces), Werkstätten, Schulregeln co-developed with students, Elternmitarbeit (parent involvement), and events like Winterfest, ball court construction, "Grünes Klassenzimmer." [4] Daily communal encounters build trust and independence.[8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
State-recognized Ersatzschule since 2000 (primary) and 2015 (Sek1), member of BFAS and daks, aligning with their 2021 #bildungistpolitisch statement.[4][5] No awards, rankings, or specific outcomes mentioned; focuses on equivalent state exams (BBR, eBBR, MSA).[8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Process**: Three steps—(1) 1-page application letter from parents answering 3 questions (wishes for child, why this school, expected learning processes) plus child's learning reflection, sent by post/email; prefer post-Infoabend/Tag der offenen Tür attendance; deadlines: Erstklässler by Nov 15 (prior year), 7th grade SEK1 by Jan 24, 2025 (for 2025/26, extended contextually); Quereinsteiger anytime (currently no spots).[9] (2) Kennlerngespräch (get-to-know interview).[9] (3) Kennenlerntage (trial days, e.g., Jan 20-22 or Feb 17-19 for 2024/25 starters), followed by mutual decision.[9] Capacity ~120; no current free places.[1][10]

**Fees**: No specific tuition, registration, material, meal, or after-school costs mentioned on pages; "Finanzen" section listed but empty in scrape. School seeks donations (e.g., GLS Bank DE16 4306 0967 1161 8869 00, betterplace.org), implying needs-based funding.[4] No scholarship or income-based details; one PDF noted but unrelated to fees.[4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Friedensburg-Oberschule prioritizes joint learning in stable groups, supporting all students to achieve their full potential through internal differentiation by performance levels and offering tasks with varying difficulty.[1] It emphasizes personality development, all-day schooling from 8:00 to 16:00 four days a week with support, advising, projects, and professional-oriented group work.[1] As the largest public bilingual European school in Berlin, it focuses on extending international partnerships to address digitalization, inclusion, environmental protection, and project-based teaching across subjects.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Goethestr. 8-9, 10623 Berlin in the Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district, this public tuition-free **Integrated Secondary School (ISS)** serves as the site for the **European State School of Berlin (SESB)** German-Spanish program.[1] Facilities include an Aula for art exhibitions, Schließfächer (lockers) via Mietra with online rental discounts, and a Bücherfond (book fund) system.[Main Page] It operates as an all-day school and holds **ERASMUS+ accreditation** for international projects.[3][7] No founding date is specified.

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This **ISS** covers grades 7-13, with enrollment info for 7th and 11th grades.[Main Page][1] From 9th grade, core subjects (German, Maths, English/Spanish in SESB) divide into ability-based groups (AoK) for realistic goals.[1] Bilingual German-Spanish SESB offers continuous instruction; students can pursue the best possible diploma, including **Abitur** for university qualification.[1][5] All-day structure includes project-oriented learning; digital tools like itslearning platform since 2020 enhance media skills.[3] Unterrichtszeiten (class schedule) available as PDF.[Curriculum Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars feature arts (ARTE annual art exhibition in Aula, Wintershow 2025, Sommerkonzert, Kunstausstellung 2026), music (Musikfahrt), sports (annual Crosslauf with CleanUp), and international trips (2nd Sri Lanka journey for grades 12-13 to build school partnerships).[Main Page] **ERASMUS+ projects** like WEBS promote language/ICT skills, intercultural awareness, and European heritage via exchanges with seven bilingual schools.[7] Competitions include 1st prize in "IST DAS KUNST ODER KANN DAS KI?" (myDigitalWorld).[Main Page]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Nominated as one of **15 best schools for Deutscher Schulpreis 2020**.[Main Page] **ERASMUS+ accreditation** supports global partnerships as Berlin's largest public bilingual European school.[3] 1st prize in national youth digital safety competition (2024).[Main Page]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public school with no tuition; admissions for 7th/11th grades via primary school certificate marks (70% allocation) and lottery (30%).[1][Main Page] Open Day on **10.01.2026 (10:00-13:00)** includes talks by principal Hr. Zimmerschied on school info/admissions and SESB by Hr. Edroso; virtual option and flyer available.[Main Page]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Friedrich-Bergius-Schule emphasizes a supportive "school family" environment where students are encouraged to contribute their unique abilities in class and extracurriculars, with new concepts under recent leadership fostering resilience to challenges.[1][2] Its pedagogical motto is **"Leistung fordern – Sozialverhalten fördern – Berufsfähigkeit erreichen"** (Demand performance – Promote social behavior – Achieve vocational competence).[2] Recent reforms include clear rules, welcome classes, smaller groups, value dialogues for a student-developed code of conduct, and media-reported changes addressing past issues like violence and mobbing to build respect and cohesion.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as Gymnasium Friedenau with groundbreaking on April 20, 1901, and opened April 18, 1903, in a Neorenaissance-style building by architects Paul Engelmann, Erich Blunck, and Johannes Duntz; it closed post-WWII, reopened as Friedrich-Bergius-Oberschule (Realschule) in 1958, and now operates as an integrated secondary school named after chemist Friedrich Bergius.[2][6] Located in Berlin-Friedenau (Tempelhof-Schöneberg district) at Perelsplatz 6-9, 12159 Berlin, the historic listed monument (Objektnummer 09066263) houses the Stadtteilmuseum Friedenau and features a green courtyard, large sports field, 1960 turnhalle, and 2010/2011 multi-purpose building with turnhalle, cafeteria, and classrooms.[1][2][6] The community includes ~400 students, staff, and parents collaborating on events like the Open Day.[1][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
An **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** for grades 7-10, offering core subjects like Deutsch (focus on reading/writing), Mathematik (critical thinking, problem-solving), Englisch (global language emphasis), Kunst (creative/experimental), and Sport (gender-separated, fitness/social skills).[1][4][7] Additional offerings: Wahlpflichtunterricht, mathematisch-naturwissenschaftliches Profil, theaterbetontes Profil, 2nd foreign language Französisch, offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB), inner differentiation in Deutsch/Mathe/Englisch (grade 7), duales Lernen (internships, job coaching), and berufsorientierung coaching.[1][4] Students earn **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** after grade 10, qualifying for gymnasiale Oberstufe (Abitur pathway) with good grades; no BBR mentioned.[2][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include AGs (e.g., Künstlerisches Gestalten, Theater, Chor), projekt-/sprachenfahrten, stipendien-beratung for study abroad, lesetraining, and schülerprojekte like "Pfand-Raising".[1][4][5] Sports via dedicated program and facilities; arts through creative Kunst and theater profile.[1][4] Online absence reporting, parent evenings, and Bücherfonds participation enhance support; past reforms added Willkommensklassen and value dialogues.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent turnaround from 2024 issues (violence, mobbing via Brandbrief) under new leadership (Engin Çatık) praised in media as a Berlin model, with overregional press on positive changes.[1][3][5] No specific awards, rankings, or exam results detailed in content.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Öffentlich school**, enrollment targets grade 6 transitions from primary school, promoted via Open Day (Dec 13, 2025, 10-13 Uhr) showcasing programs, school family, and motto "Come in and find out!"[1] No fees or selective requirements specified; automatic notifications for absences via website.[1]

description:
# Fritz-Kühn-Schule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Fritz-Kühn-Schule's mission centers on **"innovative, digital, praxisnah" (practice-oriented) learning pathways leading to the Abitur**[5]. The school emphasizes that learning extends beyond traditional classroom instruction, focusing on developing students' independence, motivation, and lifelong learning capabilities[2].

The school operates a **hybrid learning model** that combines structured classroom instruction with flexible, project-based learning[2][4]. This approach integrates **Präsenzunterricht** (in-person instruction), flexible learning days for excursions and workshops, and **self-directed learning (svL)**[2]. Core subjects—German, Mathematics, and English—are taught in smaller class groups to support individual learning needs[2].

The educational philosophy emphasizes **social learning, career orientation, and media competency**[4]. Students engage in regular project days focused on teamwork, communication, and conflict resolution, alongside workshops featuring professionals from various industries[4]. The school positions itself as a place "where all find their place" in a supportive, anxiety-free atmosphere[2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Contact:**
The school is located in **Berlin-Bohnsdorf**, Treptow-Köpenick district[1][3]. It operates across two buildings:
- Main building: Dahmestraße 45, 12526 Berlin-Bohnsdorf
- Branch location: Dahmestraße 36, 12526 Berlin-Bohnsdorf[5]

**Leadership:**
- Headmaster: Frau Obst
- Deputy Headmaster: Herr Wabnitz[5]

**Current Enrollment:**
The school currently serves approximately **500 students in grades 7-10**[2][4].

**Expansion and New Facilities:**
Beginning in school year 2026/27, the Fritz-Kühn-Schule will expand significantly with a new building at Kalker Str. 7, 12524 Berlin, constructed using sustainable wood-compartment building methods[1]. This expansion adds **625 new school places**, including **225 positions for a new upper secondary level (gymnasiale Oberstufe)** and **400 positions for secondary level I**[1].

The new building features:
- Open learning areas and modern classroom/team spaces
- Outdoor sports facilities and "green classrooms"
- Landscaped break areas
- Media workshop with recording studio[1]
- Three-field sports hall with spectator gallery[1]

The building holds the **"Nachhaltiges Gebäude" (Sustainable Building) quality seal and BNB Silver certification**[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Type and Grades:**
The Fritz-Kühn-Schule is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** serving grades 7-10, with expansion to include upper secondary (grades 11-13) beginning 2026/27[1][3][5].

**Languages Offered:**
Students study **English, French, and Spanish**[3].

**Educational Profiles:**
The school emphasizes:
- **Career-oriented profile** with practical learning groups (Praxislerngruppen)[6]
- **Environmental profile**[3][6]
- **Dual learning opportunities** through partnerships with businesses[6]

**Special Programs:**
- **BE.hybrid school experiment**: Participation in Berlin's statewide hybrid learning initiative[4]
- **Digital learning**: All students from grade 7 onward receive iPad training and use digital learning materials[2]
- **Elective courses (Wahlpflichtkurse)**: Offered in grades 7-10[5]

**Graduation Pathways:**
Upon expansion in 2026/27, students will be able to pursue the **Abitur (university entrance qualification)** through the new upper secondary level[5]. The school currently prepares students for secondary-level certifications.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Hybrid Learning Structure:**
Students experience a combination of:
- **Fixed-schedule in-person instruction** with core subjects taught in reduced class sizes[2]
- **Flexible learning days** for field trips, subject-specific workshops, art, music, and project-based work[2]
- **Self-directed learning (svL)** opportunities[2]

**Extracurricular and Social Learning:**
- Regular project days targeting teamwork, communication, and conflict resolution[4]
- Professional workshops and career information sessions with industry experts[4]
- Various student clubs and activities (AGs)[4]
- Student field trips and excursions to deepen knowledge in authentic learning environments[2]

**Digital and Media Education:**
The school provides **media pedagogy support**, fostering understanding of media functionality, critical analysis skills, and responsible digital citizenship[4]. A media workshop with recording studio supports this focus[1].

**Career Orientation:**
The school maintains numerous partnerships with businesses to support career exploration and vocational orientation[6].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not provide specific information about standardized test scores, graduation rates, or formal rankings. However, the school has been recognized for:

- **Sustainable building certification**: The new expansion building received the "Nachhaltiges Gebäude" quality seal and BNB Silver certification[1]
- **Innovation in education**: The school is featured in the learninglab podcast series discussing innovative learning approaches and self-directed learning across multiple learning environments[5]
- **Participation in Berlin's hybrid learning initiative**: Selection as a BE.hybrid school experiment participant demonstrates recognition of the school's innovative pedagogical approach[4]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment for School Year 2026/27:**

The Fritz-Kühn-Schule will open **eight 7th-grade classes**, accepting **208 new students**[5].

**Admission Timeline:**
- Online registration booking opens: December 17, 2025
- Registration period: March 5-12, 2026
- Admission decisions: Expected June 10, 2026[5]

**Admission Criteria:**
When demand exceeds capacity, admission follows Berlin's Sek I-VO (Secondary Level I Regulation):
- **60% (125 places)**: Based on teacher recommendation (Förderprognose)
- **30% (62 places)**: Allocated by lottery
- **10% (21 places)**: Reserved for students with documented hardship cases[5]

**Required Documentation:**
- Completed Hologrammbogen (school recommendation form) signed by both guardians
- Teacher recommendation (Förderprognose)
- Completed application form (Anmeldeformular_26-27)
- Photo data permission form
- Data protection declaration for photos
- Consent form for school experiment participation
- Current passport photo
- Copy of swimming certificate
- If applicable: documentation of learning disabilities (LRS, dyscalculia), special education status, or Berlin-Pass

**Information Sessions:**
- Grundschultag (school visit day): January 15, 2026, 8:30 AM–1:00 PM
- Parent information evening: January 16, 2026, 5:00 PM–8:00 PM[5]

Registration requires advance email notification to sekretariat@fritz-kuehn.schule.berlin.de[5].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Fritz-Reuter-Oberschule (FRO) positions itself as a modern Berlin public school emphasizing differentiated teaching forms supported by interactive tools like whiteboards, enabling diverse and engaging instruction.[1][6] It focuses on comprehensive secondary education through an integrated approach (Integrierte Sekundarschule, ISS) with gymnasial upper secondary options, promoting individual learning, inner and outer differentiation in key subjects, and special support like remedial classes.[4][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Neu-Hohenschönhausen) at Prendener Str. 29, 13059 Berlin, the school serves around 800-870 students in recent years (e.g., 839 students and 78 teachers in 2023/24) with over 200 in the gymnasial upper secondary.[1][2] Facilities include interactive whiteboards, generous sports and outdoor areas, architecturally interesting buildings, wheelchair-accessible features (e.g., elevators, ramps, parking), and a partially binding all-day program.[1][4] It fosters community through parent councils, student budgets, and events like alumni gatherings.[6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with gymnasial Oberstufe (school number 11K05), it covers Sekundarstufe I (grades 7-10, MSA qualification) and offers Abitur pathways, allowing all secondary qualifications.[2][3][5] Core languages are English (S1), with options for French, Latin, or Russian as third languages; bilingual tracks in German/English (enhanced English in grades 7-8, Geography/History in English in 9-10); sport-emphasized profile with extra sports hours (focus on girls' handball).[4][5][8] Advanced courses (Leistungskurse) in upper secondary include Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, History, Math, Physics, Political Education.[2][4] Additional features: career orientation (internships, job applications in grade 9), support for non-German speakers, and second educational pathway options.[4][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include ski trips, basketball tournaments, cross-country runs, science projects ("I love science," "Saving the Planet"), cultural exchanges ("Bonjour Paris"), and BSO events (Berufsschulorientierung).[6] Sports facilities support active breaks; arts via partnerships (e.g., Jugendkunstschule Lichtenberg); international partnerships with schools in Livry-Gargan (France) and Minneapolis/St. Paul (USA).[4] All-day offers mix mandatory and voluntary afternoon activities; events like open doors, alumni meets, and student-led tours enhance engagement.[1][4][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTcomes
The school's Abitur average was **2.52** in the past year, its best since 2001; MSA pass rate exceeds district average.[6] High gymnasial recommendation rate noted; low unexcused absences in Sek I; statistics enable comparisons (e.g., share of non-German origin students, learning aid-free pupils).[2][6] Enrollment grew from 733 (2013/14) to 879 (2022/23), indicating stability.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school**, enrollment follows Berlin's standard process for grade 7 transitions from primary school, with info events like Tag der offenen Tür (e.g., past in 2024, upcoming TdoT 2026) and central orientations (e.g., Nov 2025 for Hohenschönhausen primaries).[6][7] Profiles (bilingual or sport-emphasized) require specific registration; open to all eligible students in the district, with tours and parent consultations.[6][8]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is **"Freiheit, Verantwortung, Freundschaft"** (Freedom, Responsibility, Friendship), emphasizing values of independence, accountability, and community.[1] The educational approach focuses on international collaboration through Erasmus+ accreditation since 2021, fostering exploration of new school profiles and partnerships with European schools.[1] Students use a school-specific "Logbuch" (logbook) in every grade to promote self-organization, self-assessment, and goal-setting for independence.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The Gail S. Halvorsen Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) was formed in 2011 through the merger of the Alfred-Wegener-Oberschule and Beucke-Oberschule, previously known as the 9. ISS; it was renamed in 2013 after U.S. Air Force pilot Gail S. Halvorsen, famous for "Operation Little Vittles" during the Berlin Airlift, with Halvorsen personally unveiling the nameplate.[1][2][3][4][5] The main building dates to 1910, with origins in a 1909 Höhere Mädchenschule (higher girls' school) in Dahlem.[1][4][7] Located in Berlin-Dahlem (Steglitz-Zehlendorf district, Im Gehege 6, 14195 Berlin), it serves 493 students and 50 teachers (2023/24) with school hours Monday-Friday 07:30–18:00.[1][6] Community partnerships include Hermann-Ehlers-Gymnasium, Louise-Schroeder-Schule, Oberstufenzentrum Informations- und Medizintechnik, CARE, and Grips Theater.[1]

### 3. CURRICUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) with gymnasialer Oberstufe** (integrated secondary school with upper secondary gymnasiale level), it offers grades starting from 7th grade enrollment, providing pathways to school leaving certificates including Abitur (via gymnasiale Oberstufe).[1][6] Elective subjects (Wahlpflichtangebot) include French, Spanish, Economics-Work-Studies-Technology, Sports, and Art.[1] Dual learning projects connect academic learning with practical experiences in every grade, such as career and study orientation.[1] The school mentions "Schulabschlüsse" (school certificates), likely including MSA (Mittlerer Schulabschluss) and others typical for ISS, with BBR (Berufsbildungsreife) possible through practical programs.[1][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include Erasmus+ student exchanges (e.g., Hungary, Spain) and trips like to Iceland (visiting Skogafoss waterfall, geothermal plant, Alfred Wegener sites).[1][8] External projects encompass "Kunst gegen Mauern" (Art Against Walls), "denkmal aktiv" (active monument), "Stolpersteine – Gegen das Vergessen" (Stolpersteine – Against Forgetting), Inge-Deutschkron-Preis, YouKunst visits, Deutschlandfunk Kultur events at Humboldtforum, and film screenings like "Don’t call it Heimweh" on Margot Friedländer.[1][6] Arts activities feature the "umtriebige Kunstklasse" (active art class) excursions; sports and other electives are available.[1][6] The school provides a **Mensa** (cafeteria) with meals, including apples from its garden.[6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Erasmus+ accreditation** since 2021 supports internationalization and exchanges.[1] Named after Gail Halvorsen, who received the Lucius D. Clay Medal (2015) and CARE Deutschland-Luxemburg honorary membership (2016).[2] No specific awards, rankings, or outcome statistics (e.g., graduation rates) are detailed in available content.[1][2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich) tuition-free school**, enrollment follows Berlin public school processes, with a noted **Einschulung der 7. Klasse** (7th grade enrollment ceremony) on September 8, welcoming new students and parents.[6] No tuition, registration fees, or specific requirements beyond standard public ISS catchment are mentioned; contact via phone +49 (30) 841 08 184.[6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Georg-Klingenberg-Schule emphasizes **student-oriented, appreciative, and democratic learning** in a supportive environment, fostering curiosity, knowledge transmission, and responsibility. Core values include innovative and digital learning, community cohesion, rule adherence, sustainable thinking, career orientation, diversity, equal opportunities, team skills, reliability, trust, openness, co-determination, and co-responsibility.[1][2] The school promotes social learning, democracy education, and personal development through fair interactions and individualized support.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established over **40 years ago**, the school is located in the green settlement area of Biesdorf, serving the Marzahn-Hellersdorf district with more than **600 students** in 2022/23 taught by **50 teachers** and supported by four social pedagogues.[1][2][3] Facilities are fully digital: **kreidefrei** (chalk-free) classrooms equipped with Smartboards, media corners, broadband, and WLAN; two notebook rooms, three computer labs, 170 networked computers; total enrollment grew from 333 in 2011/12 to 612 in 2023/24.[1][3][4] Address: **Alberichstr. 24, 12683 Berlin**; phone: 030 514 35 37.[1][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** without gymnasial upper secondary, it serves **grades up to 10th class** with profiles in **information technology** (since 1990s) and **mathematics-natural sciences**.[1][3][4] Languages: **English** (1st), **French** (2nd).[3][4] Offers include dual learning (practice groups, internships, application training), internal differentiation in English/math (grade 7), teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb (part-time all-day), inclusion trial, and successful transitions to further education or vocational training by end of 10th grade.[2][4] No Abitur; focuses on MSA/BBR pathways via career orientation.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** like "Fit 4 Life," dancing, manga, creative design, creative writing, sports/games, digital photography; sports events (district cross-country, snow sports trip 2025 in South Tyrol); history trips (e.g., Martin Luther escape game 2025); English competitions ("The Big Challenge"); volunteer projects ("Mit Verantwortung" in grade 9); career info events.[1][2][4][5] Partnerships: Schlaufuchs Berlin, pad gGmbH, JFE "AM HULTSCHI," Freizeithaus Balzerplatz, Die Bandarbeiter e.V.[1] Meals via Sunshine Catering.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- **Exzellente Digitale Schule** seal awarded in 2018 and renewed multiple times.[1][5][7]
- **Exzellenzsiegel** for career orientation efforts ensuring successful transitions to further education/training.[1][2]
Student numbers and staff have steadily increased, reflecting growth and stability.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school**, enrollment is open; prospective students and families attend **Tag der offenen Tür** (open house) events, e.g., January 9/10, 2026 (16:00+), for insights into subjects, daily life, activities, and projects.[1][5][7] School start details provided for new grades (e.g., grade 7 at 8:20 on courtyard).[5]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Georg-von-Giesche-Schule operates as an **Integrierte Sekundar- und Europaschule**, emphasizing integrated secondary education with a European focus, including bilingual elements through its association with the Staatliche Europaschule Berlin (SESB).[1][2][3] It promotes flexibility, as shown in adaptive measures like virtual school tours during the Corona pandemic, and values international projects, arts, and community engagement, aligning with SESB's goals of continuous bilingual instruction, bicultural education, and equal access without fees to foster multilingualism and cross-cultural understanding.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The school is located at **Hohenstaufenstr. 47-48, 10779 Berlin**, in the **Tempelhof-Schöneberg** district, and previously housed the Werner-Siemens-Realgymnasium (WSRG), a progressive institution from 1903 known for innovative practices, student councils, and democratic principles, which was closed by Nazis in 1935 amid persecution of Jewish students and staff (from 212 Jewish students in 1931 to 72 by 1934).[1] Current facilities include a **Kunstatelier** (art studio), **Computerarbeitsplätze** (computer workstations), a school building with **Turnhalle** (gymnasium), and an **Aula** (auditorium) used for events.[1] It supports community through school psychology services, social work, and partnerships.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)**, it serves secondary levels starting from **7th grade** enrollments, with mandatory **Betriebspraktikum** (internship) in 9th grade.[1] It features a new **Profilklasse KUNST** (art profile class) and is part of the **Staatliche Europaschule Berlin (SESB)** network, offering bilingual instruction in languages like English, French, and Spanish alongside German, with subjects including Deutsch, Englisch, Französisch, Spanisch, Mathematik, Biologie, Chemie, Physik, Musik, Kunst, Sport, Informatik, and more, up to Gymnasiale Oberstufe (upper secondary).[1][2][3] Unterrichtszeiten (lesson times) are structured, with potential pathways like Abitur via SESB integration, though specific graduation details (Abitur, MSA, BBR) are not explicitly listed.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **extracurriculars** like Erasmus+ projects (e.g., "Geschichte in Bewegung" with Greece exchange), sports (football team won 1st place in Tempelhof-Schöneberg tournament), arts via Kunstatelier and new Kunst profile, cultural events (Stolpersteinverlegung ceremony), and infoveranstaltungen.[1] Additional offerings include Ganztag (all-day programs), AGs (interest groups), Hoffest, theater workshops, and projects like "Giesche im Bundestag" and "good@school."[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The Erasmus+ project **"Geschichte in Bewegung"** received a **bundesweite Auszeichnung** (nationwide award) on October 1, 2025, in Potsdam.[1] The school's football team achieved **1st place** in the Tempelhof-Schöneberg tournament.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public, tuition-free** school, enrollment is open with **Tag der offenen Tür** (open house) on **January 17, 2026, from 11:00 Uhr**, and online **Anmeldung for 7th classes/Terminbuchung**.[1] An **Infoabend for SESB** is scheduled for December 4, 2025, at 18:00 in the Aula; equal access via lottery if oversubscribed, per SESB model.[1][3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Georg-Weerth-Schule emphasizes **social learning** and **media education** as core focuses to ensure students are **fachlich kompetent** (subject-competent) and **selbstsicher** (self-confident) in their paths.[1] It positions itself as part of Berlin's **weltoffene** (open to the world) and **bunte** (colorful) urban community in Friedrichshain, offering **modernen Unterricht** (modern teaching) in **historical rooms** with tools like Smartboards, iPad classes, and digital platforms to foster **zukunftsorientierte Methoden** (future-oriented methods) and social forms.[1] The school develops 21st-century competencies including **communication**, **collaboration**, **creativity**, and **critical thinking**, supported by a media concept aligned with Berlin's digitalization strategy and school social work providing differentiated counseling and all-day programs.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in the heart of **Friedrichshain** (District: Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg) at **Eckertstr. 16A, 10249 Berlin**, the school serves a **diverse community** with around **400-450 students**, **45-50 teachers**, and **six school social workers**.[1][2] Facilities include **kreidefreie Klassenräume** (chalk-free classrooms) with **Smartboards**, **iPad-Klassen**, **digitale Lernplattformen** (digital learning platforms), a **basketball court**, three separate yards (for grades 7, 8-9, and 10), and lockers (though underused).[1][2] No specific founding history is detailed, but it operates historic rooms with modern upgrades and supports an **active school life** through partnerships like the parent association (Förderverein).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrated Secondary School (ISS)**, it serves grades **7-10** with a daily schedule starting at 8:00 AM and ending between 14:30-15:30, including **compulsory electives** and an **all-day program**.[1][2][3] The curriculum integrates **social learning**, **media education**, **Ethikunterricht** (ethics classes), and digital tools per Berlin's strategy, with no explicit details on languages, Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways provided in the content.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **vielfältiges Ganztagsangebot** (diverse all-day offerings), field trips (e.g., Theater Strahl for "Rasen" by grade 8 on 16.01.2026, House of One interreligious workshop for grades 9c/9e on 25.11.2025), school events (e.g., Christmas singing/theater "Hinter Türen" on 15.12.2025, studientag on new daily structure 03.12.2025), and informal sports like basketball on the court or after-class soccer (no formal teams).[1][2] Workshops involve group role-playing for conflict resolution, and videos showcase daily digital learning.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school pursues the **Qualitätssiegel "Exzellente Digitale Schule"** (quality seal for excellent digital schools) through sustained engagement in digital education, integration into teaching, and networking since 2018, per Berlin Senate strategy, though no confirmation of receipt is stated.[1] It continuously develops a media concept tailored to ISS needs in the district, with active parent and community involvement via Förderverein.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school (Öffentlich)**, enrollment targets grade 6 transitions via **Übergangsbegleitung** (transition support) including: Online Open Day (26.11.2025, 17:00-19:00), in-person Tag der offenen Tür (08.01.2026, 17:00-20:00 at Eckertstr. 16a), Schnuppertage (trial days for primary classes, max 30 students, 08:30-12:00, email sekretariat), and Oberstufenmesse (17.11.2025, 17:00-20:00 at OSZ Handel 1).[1] Invitations extend to school leadership, classes, parents, and students for guided tours and programs led by Schulleiterin Ulrike Stanicki.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Gustave-Eiffel-Schule emphasizes a welcoming, engaging school environment that showcases daily life, diverse offerings, and the dedicated team, fostering respectful interactions and preparation for professional and personal challenges.[Main Page][8] Its school program (Schulprogramm), continuously updated (last in 2025), outlines goals for teacher-student interactions, lesson design, and imparting skills beyond knowledge, such as work-world readiness; it is currently under revision.[Curriculum Page][8] The school positions itself as a place of encounter and respectful coexistence in a bound full-day structure (gebundener Ganztag).[7][8][9]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1991 as a combined Real- and Hauptschule, the school relocated in 2005 to Hanns-Eisler-Straße 78-80, 10409 Berlin (Prenzlauer Berg, Pankow district), and restructured to an Integrated Secondary School (Integrierte Sekundarschule, ISS) starting in 2010.[Curriculum Page][2][4] It serves a community near Volkspark Prenzlauer Berg, surrounded by high-rises, with ongoing expansion to a 6-track ISS including a new sports facility, cafeteria, and barrier-free design for school, club sports, and public use.[1][4] Facilities include specialized rooms for biology, chemistry, physics, art, music, French, metal/wood workshops, teaching kitchen, excellent sports areas, modern computer rooms, internet in all rooms, interactive whiteboards, school garden, fitness room, and student club.[Main Page][3][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a tuition-free public ISS (grades 7-10, without gymnasiale Oberstufe), it offers profiles in art (kunstbetontes Profil), economy-work-technology (WAT), theater, and career-oriented (berufsorientiertes Profil), with languages including English (1st), Spanish (2nd), and French (3rd).[2][3][Main Page] Programs feature bound full-day operations (GGB), dual learning (Produktives Lernen), career/study counseling from grade 7 (workshops, social internships, 14-day practicums in grades 9/10, WAT at partner OSZ, career choice passport), and pathways like Abitur (second educational path), MSA, and BBR.[3][4][6] Enrollment grew from 617 (2022/23) to 640 (2023/24) students across 60-67 classes; admission prioritizes average grade prognosis if oversubscribed.[2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in bound full-day activities with extracurriculars like school club, band, choir, climbing, school garden, drumming, soccer, ice skating/track cycling, photo/video editing, archery, strength training, painting, sculpture, hip-hop, dance, parcourt, and computer clubs.[3] Career prep includes workshops, social internships, practicums, and visits to upper schools/messe; community events feature winter markets, ski trips, parent cafes, open days, and projects like "Gustave hilft nach."[Main Page][4][6][10] A student newspaper ("Eiffel-News") and international exchanges (e.g., YFU) enhance experiences.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The student newspaper "Eiffel-News" won best in the ISS category at the 2024/2025 Berlin Student Newspaper Competition on February 18, 2025.[5] Inspections highlight strong career/study orientation, structured leadership, appreciative climate, and language support; the school operates at load level 4 with good facilities and evaluations informing program updates.[4][Curriculum Page]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free school, enrollment is open with information for new 7th graders via PDFs; open house events (e.g., January 16, 2026) and parent info sessions facilitate entry.[Main Page][3][10] Priority uses average grade prognosis if oversubscribed; contact school for questions.[3][Main Page]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
No explicit mission statement is provided in the scraped website content. The school emphasizes a **linguistic and musical focus**, with enhanced instruction in English and French, including DELF examinations in cooperation with Centre Francais, and musically oriented programs like band, choir, and various music AGs (working groups).[1][2][4] It promotes holistic, differentiated learning, including binnendifferenzierung in grades 7-8 and performance-based grouping in grades 9-10 for math and English, alongside support for diverse learners such as those with cognitive development needs and non-German speakers.[1][2][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1956** with official opening in **1958** (main building in 1957), the **Gustav-Freytag-Schule** is a protected architectural monument from the 1950s, located in southeast **Reinickendorf** near Breitkopfbecken and Bertha-von-Suttner-Gymnasium.[1][2] Facilities include two sports halls, a small sports field with table tennis and basketball, science labs, wood workshop, teaching kitchen, music and art rooms, library, and largely barrier-free access via elevator; recent reconstructions added a cafeteria, multipurpose room, kitchen, and free-time rooms.[1][2][7] It serves a diverse community with special classes for non-German speakers (including unaccompanied refugees since 2015) and cognitive support classes since 2016, in partnership with Modul e.V. for all-day programs.[1][2][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** (school number 12K05), it offers grades **7-10** without upper secondary gymnasium level, with a **linguistic profile** (English as first foreign language, French as second with extra profile hours and DELF1 exams) and **musical profile** (extra hours, differentiated courses).[2][3][4] Programs include berufsorientiertes (career-oriented) and gesundheitsbetontes (health-focused) profiles, hands-on science in small groups, internships (e.g., French school practicum in grade 9), and pathways supporting 39% gymnasial recommendation rate for Abitur-track schools; no specific MSA or BBR details mentioned.[3][4] Enrollment around 420-450 students with 41-49 teachers in recent years (2021/22-2023/24).[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars highlight **music and arts**: school band, choir, Tanz-AG, Gitarren-AG, Schlagzeug-AG, Klavier-AG, and BEGA Filmmusik project (13th phase ongoing, with 2025 concert success and 2026 challenge with prizes).[website][4][6] International exchanges with French schools, strong French ties via DELF and practicums.[1][2][4] Sports facilities support physical activities; all-day open program fosters community.[1][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Participation in BEGA Filmmusikprojekt with successful 2025 Abschlusskonzert (October 15) at Fontanehaus, drawing many attendees.[website] Around 39% of students receive gymnasial recommendations.[4] No awards, rankings, or certifications explicitly listed; recent digital upgrades via Digital Pact School.[1][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public, tuition-free** school, admission follows Berlin public secondary enrollment for grade 7 (Öffnungszeiten-z.-Anmeldung-des-7.-Jhg-2026 noted). **Open Day (Tag der offenen Tür)** scheduled for **January 15, 2026, 17-19 Uhr**, inviting all interested families.[website][6] Contact via email (kontakt@gustav-freytag-schule.berlin), phone (030 495 80 31), or form; supports diverse intakes including language learners.[website][1]

description:
# Gustav-Heinemann-Oberschule (GHO) School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Gustav-Heinemann-Oberschule** is guided by the motto **"gemeinsam – vielfältig – leistungsstark"** (together – diverse – high-performing)[1]. The school's pedagogical mission centers on preparing students for the demands of a networked, global society while fostering education, social competencies, and civic responsibility[1].

The school is named after Gustav Heinemann and draws inspiration from his values of **civil courage, morality, and solidarity with the disadvantaged**[1]. The institutional culture rests on two core principles:

- **Leistungsorientierung (Performance Orientation)**: The school identifies, supports, and promotes talented and gifted students through differentiated instruction and specialized profiles, rejecting any stigmatization of high-achieving students[1].
- **Werteorientierung (Values Orientation)**: Democratic values, mutual respect, and dignity form the foundation of school culture[1].

The school emphasizes that students develop **self-determination, co-determination, and solidarity with those facing disadvantages**[1].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Infrastructure:**
The school is located at **Tirschenreuther Ring 48, 12279 Berlin** in the Tempelhof-Schöneberg district[1]. The Gustav-Heinemann-Oberschule recently completed a major building renovation, with students ceremonially moving into the new facility after over 15 years of planning and construction[1].

**Building Design:**
The new building is a **six-form (6-zügige) integrated secondary school with upper-level gymnasium**[2]. Architecturally, it features a four-story steel-reinforced concrete skeleton structure with a footprint of approximately 86m × 65m[2]. The design creates a central zone with a two-story entrance hall serving as "the heart of the school," facilitating communication and community[2]. Two central courtyards provide spatial transparency and sightlines, while a multipurpose room accommodates theater performances and concerts[2]. The school courtyard includes a school garden and sports facilities, connecting to an existing sports hall[2].

**Community Support:**
The school is sustained by performance-oriented students, an engaged faculty, and an active parent support association (Förderverein)[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Entry Points:**
Students can begin at the school in **grade 5 or grade 7**[1]. The school offers **leistungsdifferenzierter Unterricht** (performance-differentiated instruction) beginning in grade 7[1].

**Graduation Pathways:**
The school provides flexibility in educational outcomes:
- **Mittlerer Schulabschluss** (Intermediate School Certificate) after grade 10
- **Abitur after 13 years** (standard pathway)
- **Abitur after 12 years** for high-performing students[1]

There is **no trial year**, and the decision regarding the final educational goal remains open until the end of grade 10[1].

**Specialized Profiles:**
The school offers four distinct profiles, each with specialized curricula:

- **Englisch Bilingual** (English Bilingual)
- **Mathematik-Informatik** (Mathematics-Computer Science)
- **Musik (Bläserklasse)** (Music with Wind Instrument Class)
- **Japanisch ab Klasse 5** (Japanese from Grade 5)[1]

**Advanced Learning Structures:**
Students with particular aptitude or motivation participate in **Fachleistungskurse** (specialized performance courses) in German, English, Mathematics, Biology, Physics, and Chemistry, working in **reduced group sizes**[1].

**Languages:**
The curriculum includes English (bilingual option available) and Japanese as foreign language options[1].

**Support Services:**
- **Berufsberatung** (Career Counseling) is available to students[1]
- **Sozialpädagogischer Dienst** (Socio-Pedagogical Services) provides student support[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities:**
The school offers **over 40 working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** covering diverse interests[1]. Documented activities include:

- Volleyball training and trips (e.g., 2025 trip to Sachsen-Anhalt sports school)
- Model railway club with annual exhibitions
- Euroscola program providing insights into EU Parliament operations
- Erasmus+ international exchange programs[1]

**International Engagement:**
Students participate in **Erasmus+ programs**, with documented exchanges including Cyprus trips and multilingual, cross-cultural learning experiences[1].

**Arts and Culture:**
- School newspaper ("GHO Intern")
- Annual art calendar featuring student works (available for €10)
- Theater and concert performances in the multipurpose room[1]

**Class Teacher Model:**
Class teachers (Klassenlehrer) provide mentorship beyond subject instruction, including support with homework and personal development[1].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Student Composition and Academic Standing:**
Over **60% of enrolled students hold a Gymnasialempfehlung** (gymnasium recommendation from primary school), indicating strong academic performance[1]. In comparative enrollment rankings with other Tempelhof gymnasiums, the Gustav-Heinemann-Oberschule **regularly ranks 2nd or 3rd**, demonstrating significant parental preference and competitive standing[1].

The school positions itself as **"the alternative to the gymnasium"** for high-achieving students, offering a performance-oriented environment without the stigmatization sometimes associated with comprehensive schools[1].

**School Inspections:**
The school maintains documentation of **Schulinspektion** (school inspections) and **Evaluation** processes, indicating regular quality assurance[1].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Admission for Grade 7:**

The school uses a **multi-criteria admission process** combining merit-based and lottery-based selection:

- **Aufnahmetest (Entrance Exam)**: Students must take a test for one of three profiles (English Bilingual, Mathematics/Computer Science, or Music/Wind Instrument Class)[1]
- **Förderprognose (School Recommendation)**: Based on the primary school's recommendation, which reflects grade point average[1]
- **Losverfahren (Lottery)**: **30% of available places are allocated through lottery**, ensuring access regardless of test performance[1]
- **Geschwisterregelung (Sibling Priority)**: Siblings of current GHO students receive priority admission[1]

**Admission for Upper Level (Grades 11-12):**
Separate admission procedures apply for students entering the gymnasium upper level[1].

**Information Sessions:**
The school hosts regular **Infoabende** (information evenings) for prospective families, with profile-specific sessions covering curriculum, teaching approach, and student life[1].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Gustav-Langenscheidt-Schule emphasizes **gemeinsames Lernen** (joint learning) and integrates education with afternoon care in a supportive environment that fosters self-confidence and provides second chances for success.[1][4] It promotes a relaxed community atmosphere as outlined in its Leitbild (school mission statement), with no grade repetition—instead, students advance automatically to the next class.[1] The school focuses on career preparation from grade 7, including company visits, self-assessment projects, and 2-3 week internships from grade 8, supported by school social work in its all-day program.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as Hohenzollern-Schule with a building constructed 1897-1900 (a listed monument designed by Paul Egeling), it was renamed multiple times: Scharnhorstschule (1938), Riesengebirgs-Oberschule (1956), and Gustav-Langenscheidt-Schule (2010) after the language teacher and publisher founder.[5] Heavily damaged in WWII (1943) and repaired by 1955, it became a model connected Hauptrealschule in 1999 and an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with all-day operations in 2010/2011 amid Berlin's school reform.[5] Located in **Tempelhof-Schöneberg district** near Heinrich-Lassen-Park and St. Norbert Church at **Belziger Str. 43-51, 10823 Berlin** (school number 07K09), it serves a diverse community with events like school festivals, Christmas markets, and safety info on fireworks.[1][2][3][5] Facilities include a cafeteria, computer rooms, teaching kitchen.[3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **ISS (Integrierte Sekundarschule)**, it covers secondary education (typically grades 7-10), offering **BBR (Berufsbildungsreife)** after 10 years, **eBBR (erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife)**, or **MSA (Mittlerer Schulabschluss)** after grades 9/10, with qualified students able to transfer to gymnasiale Oberstufe for **Abitur** after 3 more years.[1][3] Languages: **English** (1st foreign), **Spanish** or **French** (2nd foreign).[3][6] Features career-oriented profile, dual learning (Praxislerngruppen), school internships, internal/external differentiation in math/English (grades 9/10), doubled staffing in math/German/English (grade 7), and subjects like informatics, economics/work/technology, natural sciences, arts, sports.[1][3][6] All-day operation (teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb) with school social work.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include **basketball**, langlauf (cross-country skiing) AG, swimming, theater/performing arts, French, deepened language/science instruction, service learning, conflict mediators training.[3] Community events: school festival (Schulfete), Christmas market with tree sales/food/actions, Halloween wishes, democracy center visits, basketball tournaments.[3][scraped content] Sports and cultural trips, such as to Zentrum für Demenz (democracy center) and ALBA Berlin tournament.[scraped content] Prevention, political/cultural education, and company visits/praktika enhance experiences.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are detailed in available content; outcomes focus on inclusive success paths like MSA preparation and Abitur access without repetition.[1][3] Participates in urban FAB gGmbH programs since 2006 and model initiatives historically.[2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (öffentlich), enrollment follows Berlin public criteria: average grade from primary school forecast note and others if oversubscribed.[3] Open house: January 21, 2026, 16:00–19:00 for parents/prospective students.[scraped content]

description:
# Gutenberg-Schule Berlin-Lichtenberg: School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Gutenberg-Schule positions itself as an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) with a gymnasium upper level** (gymnasiale Oberstufe), offering a comprehensive educational pathway from secondary through upper secondary education. The school emphasizes **inclusive, community-oriented learning** with a focus on diverse student experiences and engagement. The website highlights welcoming communication ("Wir freuen uns darauf" — We look forward to it) and transparency through regular updates on school activities, suggesting a philosophy centered on **open dialogue with families and the broader school community**.

The school's approach integrates **academic rigor with experiential learning**, as evidenced by structured field trips, cultural exchanges, and skill-building activities that connect classroom content to real-world contexts.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Access:**
The Gutenberg-Schule is located in **Berlin-Lichtenberg** at Sandinostraße 10, 13055 Berlin, positioning it within a residential district. As a public school (Öffentlich), it serves the local community without tuition barriers.

**Facilities:**
The school operates multiple functional spaces including:
- A dining hall (Speiseraum) used for concerts and community gatherings
- Classroom buildings with designated spaces for arts, music, and specialized instruction
- Administrative offices and counseling rooms (e.g., Room B12 for the school psychologist)
- Outdoor spaces for community events (participation in the Lichtermarkt)

**Community Integration:**
The school actively participates in local cultural events, including the annual Lichtermarkt in Lichtenberg, where student-led baking groups sell homemade goods. This demonstrates **strong community embedding and student responsibility**.

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Structure:**
The school serves students from **Jahrgang 7 (Grade 7) through Jahrgang 13 (Grade 13/Abitur)**, reflecting the standard German secondary and upper secondary pathway. As an ISS with gymnasiale Oberstufe, students can pursue the **Abitur (university entrance qualification)**.

**Language Programs:**
- **Latin** is offered as a subject, with documented field trips (e.g., to Park Sanssouci for cultural-historical context)
- **French** exchange programs are available, with students spending a week in France to develop language skills and cultural competency
- The school supports **multilingual development**, as evidenced by participation in the "Mehr Sprachen – mehr WIR" (More Languages – More Us) national competition

**Subject Areas and Specializations:**
The curriculum encompasses:
- **Humanities and Social Sciences** (history, languages, social studies)
- **Arts and Music** (documented through theater courses, school band, piano instruction, and art competitions)
- **Sciences** (implied through standard ISS structure)
- **Physical Education** (skiing trips to South Tyrol)

**Graduation Pathways:**
Students can pursue the **Abitur** through the gymnasium upper level, with dedicated admissions processes and support structures.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities and Clubs:**
The school operates numerous **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** including:
- **Music and Performance:** School band, theater courses (Theaterkurs 10), piano instruction, Christmas concerts
- **Baking and Culinary Arts:** Multiple back-AGs (Backen-AGs) that produce goods for community markets
- **Reading and Literacy:** "LeseProfis" (Reading Professionals) program where students visit primary schools to share stories

**Field Trips and Educational Exchanges:**
- **Cultural excursions:** Opera visits (Berliner Staatsoper), historical sites (Park Sanssouci)
- **International exchanges:** France exchange program (approximately 14-15 students, cost-effective model)
- **Skiing trips:** Annual winter sports trip to South Tyrol (Ahrntal) combining athletic challenge with community building
- **Historical study tours:** Grade 13 trip to Krakow for Holocaust education and historical understanding

**Support Services:**
- **School psychologist** (Schulpsychologe Herr Timm) available for student counseling and support
- **Gifted student support:** Documented through participation in national language competitions and specialized programming

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results provided do not contain specific information about the Gutenberg-Schule's awards, rankings, or standardized outcome data. However, the school demonstrates **achievement through student participation** in:
- National competitions (e.g., "Mehr Sprachen – mehr WIR" language competition)
- Cultural performances and community contributions
- Structured academic pathways leading to Abitur qualification

The school's active engagement in community events and documented student success in extracurricular domains suggests a **well-rounded educational environment**, though formal achievement metrics are not available in the provided content.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process:**
- **Anmeldung Jahrgang 7** (Registration for Grade 7): The school accepts applications for entry into Grade 7, the standard entry point for secondary education in Berlin
- **Anmeldung Abitur** (Abitur Registration): Separate admissions process for upper secondary level
- **Open House Events:** "Tage der offenen Tür" (Days of Open Doors) and virtual school portraits available for prospective families

**Public School Status:**
As a **public school (Öffentlich)**, the Gutenberg-Schule is **tuition-free**. There are no enrollment fees, tuition charges, or material fees required for attendance. This aligns with Berlin's public education system, which provides free access to all students regardless of socioeconomic background.

**Contact for Admissions:**
Gutenberg-Schule
Sandinostraße 10
D-13055 Berlin
Telefon: (030) 971 15 65
Mail: sekretariat@gutenberg.schule.berlin.de

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Hagenbeck-Schule's mission centers on **biological diversity** as its unique profile, taking responsibility for current and future generations by promoting sustainability, global thinking, and local action in an evolving **Umweltschule** (environmental school).[1][2][4] It emphasizes individualized learning in a warm, appreciative atmosphere to foster self-determined personalities, supported by hands-on natural science themes and practical implementation of sustainability.[4][5] Core values include quality education, environmental responsibility, and an international commitment to biodiversity, with a nature-oriented leitmotif enhanced by career-preparatory, sports, artistic, and project-based offerings.[4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Weißensee (Pankow district) at Gustav-Adolf-Str. 60, 13086 Berlin, the school is a public **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** without gymnasiale Oberstufe, managed by Bezirksamt Pankow under principal Kerstin Körner.[1][3][5] No specific founding date is provided, but it has grown to around 479 students and 46 teachers as of 2023/24, up from 426 in 2011/12.[3] Facilities include a **school farm (Bauernhof)**, **vivarium**, **farm garden (Bauerngarten)**, school zoo with ~700 animals, school pond, eco-area, outdoor classrooms, and two computer labs.[4][5] Community support comes from a parents' and staff **Förderverein** (foerderverein@hagenbeck-schule.de) and partners/sponsors.[4]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **ISS** (integrated secondary school, grades 7-10), it offers a **teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb** (partially bound all-day program) with profiles in **mathematical-natural sciences**, **sports**, and **career orientation**.[1][3][5] Languages include **English** (1st), **French** or **Spanish** (2nd).[1][5] Programs emphasize natural sciences/biodiversity, berufsvorbereitende (career-preparatory) elements like internships, WETEK potential analysis, Girls/Boys Day, company visits, application training, and Berufswahlpass (career choice passport).[1][5][6] Graduation pathways support **MSA** (Mittlerer Schulabschluss), **BBR** (vocational preparation), and transitions to OSZ (vocational schools) or further education; no Abitur or gymnasiale Oberstufe.[3][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on learning at the farm, vivarium, and garden, alongside **extracurriculars (AGs)** like theater, soccer, volleyball, table tennis, animal/plant care, cooking/baking, French, application techniques, art, and computer training.[4][5] Project-oriented offerings include service-learning, MINT projects, mini-internships, school firm, and career fairs (Vocatum, Ausbildungstag Pankow).[5][6] Sports, arts, and berufsorientierte activities promote social competencies, with youth club, midday meals, and school social work.[1][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as an **Umwelt- und Nachhaltigkeitsschule** for the 8th time, highlighting ongoing environmental commitment.[4] Positive inspection (2017) noted strong berufs/studienorientierung, learning conditions, and biodiversity profile attracting students, with developments needed in media concepts and individualized learning.[7] Around 470 students served by 45 teachers, with Belastungsstufe 4 (moderate workload).[3][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public, tuition-free** school, enrollment follows Berlin public guidelines; at over-demand, priority by average grade in Förderprognose (promotion prognosis) and other criteria.[5] Contact sekretariat@hagenbeck-schule.de or +49 30 96275050 for inquiries.[10] No tuition, registration, or material fees specified.[School Information]

description:
# Hans-Grade-Schule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Hans-Grade-Schule is **an Integrated Secondary School (Integrierte Sekundarschule) serving Berlin-Johannisthal** with a sport-focused emphasis[1][2]. The school's educational philosophy centers on developing students as whole individuals through a combination of conventional instruction and experiential learning.

The school prioritizes three core competency areas: **methodological skills** ("How do I learn to learn?"), **communication abilities** ("How do I present effectively?"), and **teamwork** ("How do I work in a team?")[4]. These competencies are taught as a continuous principle across grades 7-9[2]. The school emphasizes creating a **friendly and respectful school environment** where conflicts are resolved through peer and teacher mediation rather than punitive measures[4].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Basic Information:**
Hans-Grade-Schule is located at **Heubergerweg 37, 12487 Berlin** in the Johannisthal district of Treptow-Köpenick[5][6]. The school is a **public institution** with no tuition fees.

**School Leadership:**
- Headmaster: Dr. Schulze
- Deputy Headmaster: Frau Seide
- Secretary: Frau Krummel
- Administrative Director: Frau Grüner[5]

**Student Population and Staff:**
The school serves approximately **420-430 students** across four classes per grade level (grades 7-10)[2][4]. The school employs approximately **34-40 teaching staff members**[2][3]. Class sizes range from **25-30 students**, with a typical range of 26-28 students per class[2][3].

**Facilities:**
The school operates from a main campus and has recently expanded with a **secondary location (Container facility) on Hoevelstraße** due to ongoing construction[5]. The school includes a **cafeteria** and has invested in campus improvements, including creating outdoor seating areas and green spaces for student use[5].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Structure:**
Hans-Grade-Schule serves **grades 7-10 only**; it does not offer a gymnasium (upper secondary) program[1][3]. Students seeking to pursue the Abitur (German university entrance qualification) must transition to cooperating schools[3][8].

**Languages:**
- **English** is mandatory as the first foreign language[6]
- **French** is offered as a second foreign language through a specialized French profile class[1][2]
- An **E+ advanced English course** has been available since grade 9 (introduced in 2016/2017) for students seeking advanced language qualification[4]

**Profile Classes and Specializations:**
Since 2013/2014, the school has organized students into **profile classes based on elective courses**[1][2]:

- **Sports Profile**: Requires passing an entrance examination; students receive intensive sports training[2][4]
- **French Profile**: Intensive French language instruction with emphasis on pronunciation and dialogue practice[3]
- **German-Musical-Artistic Profile**: Participates in a Berlin-wide theater project where students develop and perform original theatrical pieces[2]
- **Natural Sciences and Computer (NaWiC) Profile**: Offers computer science instruction beginning in grade 9[2][3]
- **Theater Profile**: Collaborative theatrical work, including recent partnerships with Zirkus Cabuwazi[5]
- **Economics-Work-Technology (WAT) Profile**: Introduced in 2022/2023, focusing on economics, home economics, and wood and textile workshops with project-based learning[2]

**Graduation Pathways:**
Students can earn one of five qualifications upon completing grade 10[2]:
- **Mittlerer Schulabschluss with gymnasium recommendation (MSA+)**: Qualifies students for upper secondary education
- **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)**: Standard secondary completion
- **Extended vocational education qualification (eBBR)**
- **Basic education qualification (BBR)**
- **Career-oriented completion (BOA)** or equivalent for students with special educational needs

Students achieving MSA+ can pursue the **Abitur, Fachabitur (technical Abitur), or Abitur with vocational training** through **cooperative agreements with multiple vocational training centers (OSZ) and other schools**[2][8]. The school reports that its students consistently achieve above-average rates of gymnasium recommendation[8].

**Grading and Differentiation:**
In grade 7, all students receive **differentiated instruction within mixed-ability classes**. Performance is assessed using a point system: **G-level courses award maximum 12 points, E-level courses award maximum 15 points**[4]. Students can move between course levels at semester and year-end transitions (advancing with 7 points in G-level, declining with 4 points in E-level)[4]. To achieve MSA, students must be enrolled in at least two E-level courses in grade 10[4].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Theater and Performing Arts:**
The school operates an active **school theater program (TuSch - Theater und Schule)** that emphasizes student expression, teamwork, and self-efficacy[5]. In 2024/2025, the program partnered with **Zirkus Cabuwazi** to create a black light theater production[5].

**Sports:**
The school's **sports program has operated for over 16 years** and has achieved significant recognition, including honors from the German Federal President for exceptional athletic performance[4]. The sports course is available as an elective beginning in grade 7[2].

**Extracurricular Activities:**
The school offers **working groups (AGs) in computer design, ceramics, and various sports** including football and volleyball[7]. A **school choir** is available[4].

**Field Experiences:**
School life includes **field trips, sports festivals, and school celebrations** alongside classroom instruction[2].

**Community Engagement:**
The school actively participates in community initiatives, including a **CleanUp-Day 2025** where students collected 648 kilograms of waste in the Johannisthal district[5].

**Support Services:**
The school provides **special education support** and **school social work services** to assist students facing challenges[4]. **Peer conflict mediators** (trained students and teachers) help resolve school conflicts[4]. **Trusted teachers** are available for student consultation[4].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Athletic Recognition:**
The sports program has achieved **multiple significant athletic successes across Berlin** and received **special recognition from the German Federal President** for outstanding performance[4].

**Gymnasium Transition Rates:**
The school reports **above-average annual rates** of students receiving gymnasium recommendations (MSA+), enabling successful transitions to upper secondary education[8].

**Theater Program Success:**
The school theater program has maintained **successful ongoing work** and continues to develop creative partnerships with cultural institutions[5].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process:**
As a **public school, Hans-Grade-Schule is open to all students**; enrollment is not selective based on academic performance[8]. However, the school notes that students should identify with the school's profiles[8].

**Entrance Requirements:**
- **Sports Profile**: Requires passing an entrance examination[2]
- **Other Profiles**: No specific entrance requirements mentioned

**Current Enrollment Status:**
As of January 2026, the school is **accepting applications for grades 7-8 only**. The school **cannot currently accept students for grades 9-10**[5].

**Admission Timeline (2026):**
- **Schnuppertag (Trial Day)**: January 27, 2026, 9:00-11:00 AM (registration through cooperating primary schools only)[5]
- **Tag der offenen Tür (Open House)**: January 29, 2026, 4:00-7:00 PM[5]
- Parent information evenings have already concluded[5]

**Transition Support:**
The school provides **cooperation agreements with multiple upper secondary schools** (including Anna-Seghers, OSZ Hermann Scheer, OSZ Lise-Meitner) that guarantee enrollment rights for qualifying students[8].

**Contact Information:**
- **Phone**: 030 636 09 61
- **Fax**: 030 632 23 629
- **Email**: 09k06[at]09k06[dot]schule.berlin.de[5]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Hector-Peterson-Schule emphasizes a **künstlerisch-kreatives Profil** (artistic-creative profile), where instruction often occurs through projects and unconventional presentations in close collaboration with artists and the neighboring Hebbel am Ufer theater.[1][6][7] Its Leitbild (school concept) is "Hector-Peterson-Schule - die Schule, für die ich mich bewusst entscheide!" (the school I consciously choose!), focusing on structural changes like morning circles, weekly planning (Wochenplanarbeit), and rituals to foster self-responsible, team-oriented learning.[6] Key values include creativity, flexibility, openness, interest, and adaptability, with initiatives in relationship-based learning, inclusion, performance differentiation, and quality improvement through collegial lesson observations.[1][4][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Named after Hector Peterson, a 12-year-old killed in 1976 during a demonstration against apartheid in South Africa, the school symbolizes conscious choice and transformation.[9] Originally a comprehensive school, it became an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** in 2012, selected in 2013 for the "School Turnaround" program by the Robert-Bosch-Stiftung and Berlin Senate to address challenges in a diverse Kreuzberg neighborhood near Kottbusser Tor.[3][6] It serves a multicultural community, previously labeled a "Brennpunktschule" (hotspot school), now more peaceful with enrollment growing from ~300 in 2016/17 to 513 students and 59 teachers in 2023/24.[1][3] Facilities include an Aula for events, schoolyard for activities like Spieletag, and spaces for creative projects; an audiowalk project explored the grounds.[1][2] Located at Tempelhofer Ufer 15, 10963 Berlin (Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg, Schulnummer 02K03).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **ISS with gymnasiale Oberstufe** (integrated secondary school with upper secondary level), it offers grades 7-10 on-site and, since 2024/25, grade 11 (Einführungsphase) in partnership with Albrecht von Graefe-Schule, with Qualifikationsphase temporarily there and plans for a joint site.[1][2] Foreign languages: **Englisch, Französisch, Türkisch**.[1] Curriculum features student-team-oriented learning, Wochenplanarbeit in subjects like Deutsch, Math, English (grades 7-9), Leistungsdifferenzierung, Inklusion, and strong Berufsorientierung (career guidance) including 11th compulsory year, OSZ pathways, Abitur access, and Agentur für Arbeit support.[1][2][6] Graduation pathways include MSA (Mittlere Reife), berufsbildende Abschlüsse, and Abitur via the new Oberstufe; ~90% graduate with qualifications.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **künstlerisch-kreative projects** (e.g., theater with Hebbel am Ufer, audiowalks, Spieletag 2025), Projektwoche, and Kulturagenten initiatives since 2011, increasing cultural project diversity and school identification.[2][4][5][6] Extracurriculars include Ganztag (all-day program) with AGs (working groups) needing leaders for creative youth activities, events like Zeugnisfeier, and cooperative Berufsberatung.[2][5] Experiences emphasize self-discovery, social competence, and interdisciplinary work like interactive school tours by grade 11 students.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Selected for School Turnaround (2013) leading to reduced police visits, halved-then-stabilized enrollment, and 90% graduation rate.[3] Kulturagenten-Programmschule since 2011, developing a distinct artistic profile with more teacher participation in projects.[4] Implemented school program with five Leitsätze covering creative orientation to qualifications; positive inspection on management, participation, and developments like Morgenkreis and Wochenplanarbeit.[6] Enrollment growth to 513 students (2023/24); Belastungsstufe 5.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Öffentlich school**, enrollment is open; local priority for Kreuzberg residents, but accepts others (e.g., external grade 11 applicants).[1][2][3] Events include Informationsabend (15.01.2026, 17:00 Aula) for grade 10 transitions (Ausbildung, OSZ, Abitur, 11th year), Infos for new grade 11 (26.02.2026, 18:00 Aula, register by 23.02.2026 to schmidt@hps-berlin.schule), Tag der offenen Tür (open day), and Padlet resources.[2] No fees or selective requirements noted beyond standard public processes.
