description:
# Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule Berlin: Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule operates on a foundational belief that **every student is unique and capable of reaching their full potential**[6]. The school's pedagogical approach is characterized by cooperation, transparency, friendly engagement, and respect, creating a learning environment centered on mutual appreciation and acceptance[1].

**Core Educational Values:**

- **Individualized Learning**: Students are supported according to their individual prerequisites and performance levels, with emphasis on enabling self-directed learning processes[1]
- **Positive Learning Climate**: The school prioritizes joy in learning as a central value, utilizing both innovative and proven teaching methods with regular outcome evaluation[1]
- **Humanistic Development**: Educational goals focus on developing positive learning and life attitudes, performance-oriented and self-responsible personalities, and humanistic civic values[1]
- **Practical Career Preparation**: Through partnerships with businesses, universities, and external organizations, students gain practical experience preparing them for vocational and academic futures[1]
- **Violence-Free Environment**: Nonviolence and mutual consideration are prioritized, even in conflict situations[1]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development:**

The school was established on August 28, 1994, as the 2nd Comprehensive School in Marzahn, with the foundation stone laid on April 5, 1993, by Mayor Andreas Röhl and Education Councilor Wolfgang Unger at Glambecker Ring 90[1]. The school celebrated its 30-year anniversary in 2024[1].

**Location and Campus:**

The Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule is situated in the **Marzahn district of Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf borough**, located at the northeastern edge of Berlin near the Brandenburg state border and the Ahrensfelder Hills[1]. The school campus encompasses **48,000 square meters** of land[1].

**Facilities:**

The school features a two-story building with a central atrium, complemented by[3]:
- Two gymnasiums
- Sports facilities
- Spacious schoolyard
- School garden area with a "green classroom" created by students during Wirtschaft-Arbeit-Technik (WAT) instruction on a former brownfield site[3]
- In-house learning studio offering homework help and exam preparation support[1]

**Student Population and Staffing:**

Approximately **1,200 students** are enrolled, supported by roughly **110 teachers, educators, and social workers**[1]. The school is the **largest integrated secondary school in the Marzahn-Hellersdorf district**[5]. Grade levels are organized in **7-track classes**[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Structure:**

The Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule is an **Integrated Secondary School (Integrierte Sekundarschule) with a gymnasium upper level**, serving students from grade 7 through grade 13[1][3].

**Graduation Pathways:**

Students can pursue multiple qualification routes[6]:
- **Berufsbildungsreife (BBR)** – Basic vocational qualification
- **Erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife (eBBR)** – Extended vocational qualification
- **Berufsorientierter Abschluss (BOA)** – Career-oriented completion
- **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** – Middle school certificate
- **Schulischer Teil der Fachhochschulreife** – Technical college entrance qualification
- **Allgemeine Hochschulreife (Abitur)** – General university entrance qualification (G9 format)[4]

In 2025, the Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule was among schools with the best Abitur results in the Marzahn-Hellersdorf district[1].

**Languages:**

The school offers **English, French, and Russian** as foreign languages[2]. A distinctive feature is the **bilingual program** (German/English), where one class per grade receives intensive English instruction beginning in grade 7, expanding to additional subjects in grade 9[5]. Vietnamese is available through an extracurricular activity group[5].

**Subject Areas and Specializations:**

The school emphasizes a **natural sciences and career-oriented focus**[1]. Core subject areas include[6]:

| Subject Area | Specific Subjects |
|---|---|
| **Natural Sciences (NaWi)** | Biology, Chemistry, Physics |
| **Languages** | English, French, Russian, Bilingual instruction |
| **Humanities (GeWi)** | Ethics, Geography, History, Politics |
| **STEM** | Mathematics, Informatik (Computer Science) |
| **Arts & Culture** | Art, Music, German |
| **Practical Skills** | Wirtschaft-Arbeit-Technik (WAT), Digitalization |
| **Physical Education** | Sport |
| **Special Education** | Inklusion und Sonderpädagogik |

**Advanced Placement Courses (Leistungskurse):**

Students in the upper level can select from: Bildende Kunst/Kunst, Biologie, Deutsch, Englisch, Erdkunde, Geschichte, Informatik/Informationsverarbeitung, Mathematik, Physik, Politische Weltkunde, and Russisch[2].

**Career Orientation Program:**

The school participates in the **Berlin Deepened Career Orientation Program (BvBO)**, providing early and intensive preparation for vocational and academic choices, including support in securing internship placements[1]. In grade 9, students complete a **three-week internship program**[4].

**Special Programs:**

- **In-house Learning Studio**: Provides homework assistance, exam preparation, and presentation coaching[1]
- **Differentiated Instruction**: The school emphasizes problem-oriented learning with open-ended questions, self-directed and cooperative learning approaches, and individualized support[5]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities (Arbeitsgemeinschaften):**

The school offers diverse activity groups including[6]:
- AG Fische (Fish/Aquatics)
- AG Keyboard
- AG Schulband (School Band)
- AG Fahrrad (Bicycle)
- AG Boomwhacker (Percussion)
- AG Foto (Photography)
- AG RVO-Podcast

**Student Publications:**

Students regularly publish articles in the school's in-house newspaper (Schülerzeitung), with recent topics including billiards rules, criminal case projects, and photography projects[6].

**Educational Exchanges and Field Trips:**

The school organizes student exchanges and educational trips to[6]:
- England
- France
- Hamburg
- Latvia
- Weimar

**Arts and Cultural Activities:**

Notable projects and performances include[6]:
- Jewelry design in advanced art courses
- Collage creation on dream worlds
- Memory game design
- Light fixture creation
- Christmas concerts (e.g., December 17, 2024)
- Theater visits in language programs
- Stop-motion video production
- Podcast creation on language topics

**Practical Workshops:**

Students engage with hands-on learning through[6]:
- Teaching kitchen (healthy nutrition, cooking, baking)
- Textile workshop (key chains)
- Technical drawing (dimensioning and three-view projection)
- Metal workshop (key chains)
- Wood workshop (table tennis paddles)

**Academic Competitions:**

Students participate in[6]:
- Känguru-Wettbewerb (Kangaroo Mathematics Competition)
- Diercke Wissen (Geography competition)
- The Big Challenge (English language competition)
- Language Olympiads

**Social and Pedagogical Support:**

The school's **socio-pedagogical department** provides support for students facing challenges, fostering a healthy learning and living environment with respectful coexistence[3]. The school offers **special education support** for students with learning disabilities and intellectual development needs[3].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Academic Recognition:**

- In 2025, the Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule was among schools with the **best Abitur results in the Marzahn-Hellersdorf district**[1]
- The school has received the **Marzahn-Hellersdorf School Prize** multiple times, both as an institution and for individual teachers[1]

**Inspection Findings:**

According to a 2014 school inspection report, the Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule demonstrated particular strengths in[5]:
- Individual accompaniment of all students' educational pathways
- Implementation of individualized learning approaches through problem-oriented tasks
- Student-activating and individualizing instruction
- Differentiation and self-directed, cooperative learning—areas that exceeded average Berlin secondary school performance, particularly in problem-oriented learning[5]

**Inclusive Education:**

The school is an **integration school for students with physical disabilities** and provides special education support for students with learning and intellectual development needs[4].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process:**

The school accepts applications for both middle school (grade 7) and upper school (grade 11) entry[6]. Specific enrollment information for the 2026/27 school year is currently being processed on the school website[6].

**Public School Status:**

As a **public school (Öffentlich)**, the Rudolf-Virchow-Oberschule is **tuition-free**. There are no enrollment fees or tuition charges for students[2].

**Contact Information for Admissions:**

- **Address**: Glambecker Ring 90, 12679 Berlin
- **Phone**: +49 30 – 93 029 818 0
- **Email**: kontakt[at]rvo-berlin.de
- **Website**: https://rvo-berlin.de

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schilling-Schule operates as a **Ganztagsschule** (all-day school) with special pedagogical focuses on **"Sprache" (language)**, **"Körperliche und motorische Entwicklung" (physical and motor development)**, and **"Geistige Entwicklung" (intellectual development)**, emphasizing creative, versatile support for students with special needs through diverse professional teams including teachers, therapists, and educators.[1][2][6][9] The school's philosophy centers on holistic development, integrating academic instruction, therapeutic interventions (e.g., speech therapy, occupational therapy, physiotherapy), and individualized care in a supportive environment described as "Eine Schule Viele Bereiche" (One School, Many Areas), promoting creativity and multifaceted growth.[6][8][9]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Originally founded as a language therapy school (Sprachheilschule) in Neukölln's Briesestraße, the school has expanded, including a Modularer Erweiterungsbau (MEB) for intellectual development opened on November 11, 2022, as highlighted by principal Andreas Seefeld.[9] Located in Berlin-Neukölln (Britz district) at **Paster-Behrens-Str. 81, 12359 Berlin**, it serves approximately 260-410 students and is publicly owned by Bezirksamt Neukölln (Abteilung Bildung, Schule, Kultur und Sport).[1][2][3][5][10] Facilities include a **therapy/swimming pool**, teaching kitchen, cafeteria, schoolyard ("Elfenwald" and "Unser wunderschöner Schulhof"), after-school areas (Hort), and recent expansions; community support comes via a Förderverein for materials, trips, projects, and events, plus partnerships like Humboldt-Universität cooperation and school social work.[1][2][5][6][9]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school covers **Grundschule (grades 1-6)** in language focus and **Sekundarstufe I (grades 1-10)** in physical/motor and intellectual development, following Rahmenlehrpläne for primary and integrated secondary school (ISS), with English as the first foreign language.[3][5][6] Special programs include speech, occupational, and physiotherapeutic support, swimming instruction, individualized learning, project weeks, and possible qualifications like **BBR, eBBR, MSA** (Mittlerer Schulabschluss).[4][5][6] As a tuition-free public ISS, it offers all-day operations (7:30-16:00) with Hort care (6:00-7:30 and 16:00-18:00, year-round).[2][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in extracurriculars like **school choir, guitar lessons** (with music school), **breakdance AG**, computer club, swimming badges, chess, diverse sports, riding therapy, class trips, and student-oriented free-time activities with homework supervision.[5][6] Additional experiences include a 2017 school film project by students, cultural/political education, school library, religion classes, and close parent involvement via newsletters and events.[1][5][6][9]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are detailed in the content; outcomes focus on enabling **MSA, BBR, eBBR** qualifications and comprehensive special needs support, with around 260-410 enrolled students and ongoing expansions like the 2022 MEB building.[2][4][5][10] Inspection reports and school programs are referenced but not summarized with results.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public special needs school (Förderschule/ISS)**, enrollment follows Berlin public school processes via eGovernment/Schulanmeldung, targeted at students needing language, physical/motor, or intellectual development support; contact schulleitung@schillingschule.de for inquiries, including teacher applications.[1][3][5][9] No fees or specific requirements beyond district assignment are mentioned.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schul- und Leistungssportzentrum Berlin (SLZB) aims to create a learning environment where academic education succeeds alongside optimal elite sports training, driven by enthusiasm from all participants, including students, staff, and parents, through appreciative communication and active involvement.[10][3] It emphasizes a "moral school" with core values of diversity and inclusion in sports, believing every individual has potential for success regardless of physical or mental limitations, fostering respect for different cultures, backgrounds, and perspectives among talented athletes from various countries.[10] As an **Eliteschule des Sports** (Elite School of Sports) and Olympiastützpunkt, it integrates learning, training, and living to support dual careers in education and high-performance sports.[2][3][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Formed from the 2014 fusion of Werner-Seelenbinder-Schule and Coubertin-Gymnasium as an experimental integrated secondary school (Integrierte Sekundarschule, ISS) in Berlin-Alt-Hohenschönhausen, with the Sportforum site consolidated by 2015 including a new building and gym.[3][9] Located at Fritz-Lesch-Str. 35, 13053 Berlin (Lichtenberg district), it operates within the 45-hectare **Sportforum Berlin**, Germany's largest performance sports center after the Olympiapark, featuring 35 modern facilities like athletics tracks/halls, ball courts, swimming pools, football fields, dojos, strength rooms, and sport medical/physiotherapy services.[4][5][6][7] The community includes ~300 national squad and 800 state squad athletes, over 30 clubs, a dormitory ("Haus der Athleten" with ~200 places), and Humboldt University sports science students; it offers full-day care, small classes, and team teaching in core subjects.[1][4][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)**, it covers all secondary levels including Grundschulteil (primary), Schulanfangsphase, SEK I/II, gymnasiale Oberstufe, and Zweiter Bildungsweg (second educational path), enabling all Berlin school qualifications like Abitur, MSA, and BBR per the Berlin School Act.[3][9] Programs feature flexible scheduling for training, sport classes grouping athletes by discipline with trainer-led sessions, special support concepts per student, team teaching in German, Math, English, and full integration of school, training, and boarding.[1][5][9] Languages include German and English; no specific arts curriculum detailed.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience elite sports training in **profile sports** (from grade 5: figure skating, swimming, gymnastics male/female, diving) and others like athletics (throws team), judo, basketball, fencing, handball, volleyball, and more, with individual placements in Olympic sports.[1][3][5][8] Extracurriculars include Ganztagsbetreuung (full-day care), boarding with pedagogical support, annual sport medical check-ups, physiotherapy, and cultural diversity fostering mutual learning.[5][10] Sports occur Tuesday-Friday on-site facilities; school life emphasizes inclusive, value-driven daily routines.[1][10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Designated one of 43 German **Eliteschulen des Sports** by DOSB, awarded "Beste Eliteschule des Sports 2014" (previously 2007), and Olympiastützpunkt Berlin supporting top talents toward peak performances.[2][3][8] It prepares promising youth for elite achievements via coordinated dual-career model, with flexible structures yielding strong sports and academic outcomes, though specific graduate stats not detailed.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admission targets talented athletes meeting sport-specific criteria, with step-by-step guidance on the website; possible quick enrollment into grade 7 with support if suitable.[1][10] Process involves application via "Schüler*in werden" section, coordination with sports associations (e.g., athletics, judo), and evaluation of athletic potential; open to diverse backgrounds.[1][5][10] As a public tuition-free school, no fees apply; contact sekretariat@slzb.de or +49 30 983185205/4.[10]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission is encapsulated in its motto: "Wir entwickeln Fähigkeiten, finden Stärken, erzielen Erfolge, gestalten Zukunft" (We develop skills, find strengths, achieve successes, shape the future).[1][8] It emphasizes a supportive environment with a young, motivated faculty of nearly 40 teachers, plus 6 referendars and practice students across all subjects from physics to WAT (Wirtschaft, Arbeit, Technik).[1][5] The approach focuses on individualized support, including a **Lerninsel** for students needing calm spaces, conflict resolution, behavioral reflection, or targeted help for learning difficulties like dyslexia, dyscalculia, or deficits in German, math, and English.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at **Alt-Tempelhof 53-57, 12103 Berlin** in the Tempelhof-Schöneberg district (school number 07K13), this **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** serves around 372 students with 46 teachers as of 2023/24, showing steady growth from 236 students in 2013/14.[3] A new school building is under construction at Eisenacher Straße, featuring artwork "Random Conversations" by competition winner Birte Endrejat.[1][8] Community ties include partnerships like Nachbarschaftsheim Schöneberg e.V. for ganztag care and programs like Schulsozialarbeit & ProRespekt.[1][7] Facilities include smartboards, a Lerninsel room, and a ganztag pavilion with recreational spaces for games and relaxation.[1][4][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an ISS without gymnasiale Oberstufe, it offers comprehensive secondary education with subjects including Deutsch, Mathematik, Fremdsprachen (**Englisch** as first language, **Französisch** and **Spanisch** as second/third), Naturwissenschaften (Biologie, Chemie, Physik), Sport, Geographie, Geschichte, Ethik, Design, Kunst, WAT, and Musik.[1][4] Special features include intensive preparation from grade 7 for presentation exams, self-organized learning (SOL) in Deutsch and Ethik, MSA-intensive courses and colloquia, block lessons, and profiles like berufsorientiertes, gesundheitsbetontes, informationstechnik, kunstprofilierung, and mathematisch-naturwissenschaftlich.[1][3][4] Evening school (Abendschule) provides pathways to **Berufsbildungsreife (BBR)** and **mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** as a second educational route; no Abitur mentioned.[4][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb (TGB)** operates one full day weekly (per survey preference), extending to 16:00 daily via partner Nachbarschaftsheim, with AGs like Häkeln, Volleyball, Manga Kurs, Mädchen Club, Fußball, Fun Dog-Hundetraining, Fahrradwerkstatt, Basketball, Tischtennis, ultimate Frisbee, Kreativ AG, Kochen, Nähen, Spanisch, Homepage, and Nachhilfe.[1][4][7] Arts and projects include Lesewoche with author readings, Projektwoche 2023, Nachhaltigkeitsprojekt, Beauty-Tag, Sprachfahrten, and Kunst am Bau visits.[1][8] Sports and social events feature active pauses, PiF-Basar, and Familiencafé.[1][8] Vertrauenslehrkräfte (Herr and Frau Ciftci) offer counseling for academic, relational, and personal issues.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or exam outcomes are detailed in the content, though successful events like Lesewoche and artist visits highlight active engagement.[1][8] Enrollment stability (around 370-400 students recently) and comprehensive subject coverage indicate operational success; Belastungsstufe 5 per Berlin typology.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school**, admission follows Berlin public processes with open events: **Tag der offenen Tür** on January 28, 2026 (15-18 Uhr) and **Schnuppertage** February 16-27, 2026 (register by February 10, 2025 at schnuppertag@schule-am-berlinickeplatz.de).[1][8] Details on Aufnahme und Schulplätze are available on the site; contact kontakt@schule-am-berlinickeplatz.de or 030 902772641.[1][4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Königstor is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** committed to **inclusion** for over 20 years, serving as an inclusive focus school for **Autismus** and **Geistige Entwicklung**.[1][2][6] It emphasizes **individualized learning**, keeping "jeden einzelnen im Blick," practical workshops, successful career orientation, student-led governance (Schüler*innenhaushalt), high student satisfaction, and gifted education through the nationwide network **“Leistung macht Schule”** for over 10 years.[1][2][6] The philosophy promotes equity via **Nachteilsausgleich** (disadvantage compensation) in exams for students with special needs, ensuring they meet standard graduation conditions.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in **Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg** district at **Weinstr. 3, 10249 Berlin**, the school has a long tradition of integrating students with special needs, formalized as an inclusive focus school since at least 2014 (Geistige Entwicklung and Autismus) and involved in programs like SPI since 2006.[1][2][4][7][8] The modern, fully renovated building includes a **Fahrstuhl** for barrier-free access, interactive whiteboards and internet in all classrooms, specialized rooms (Physik, Chemie, Musik, Bildende Kunst, Lehrküche, Textil-/Holz-/Metallwerkstatt), two computer rooms (24 and 12 workstations), division rooms per floor, Mensa, and Aula.[1][3][6] Staffing includes **53 teachers**, 6 pedagogical assistants, 3 supervisors, and 6 social workers.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves **grades 7-10** with class sizes of **22 students** (4 classes in grade 7, 3 each in 8-10).[1][2][6] Offers **Englisch** (1st foreign language) and **Spanisch** (2nd), dual learning (Service Learning, internships, apprenticeships, student firms), practical vocational training, and gifted programs like “digitale Drehtür.” [3][4][6] Possible **graduation pathways** include: **MSAGo** (mittlerer Schulabschluss with Gymnasialempfehlung), **MSA** (mittlerer Schulabschluss), **eBBR** (erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife), **BBR** (Berufsbildungsreife), **BOA** (Berufsorientierender Abschluss); students with Geistige Entwicklung focus receive an Abgangszeugnis after grade 10.[1][2][3] A **gymnasiale Oberstufe** is in planning; part-time all-day operation (teilgebundener Ganztag, mandatory two days/week until 15:45) with meals.[1][3][6] Recent enrollment: ~319-341 students, ~51-59 teachers (2022-2024).[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Extracurriculars (AGs)** include Basketball, Fußball, Foto, Zeichnen, Kochen, Jugendclub, Englisch, Mode, Kampfsport, Social Media, and more Wahlpflichtangebote.[1][6] Practical focus via well-equipped workshops, career orientation (Betriebspraktika, Schnupperpraktika), and student council involvement promote high satisfaction and individualized support.[1][3][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Reports **überdurchschnittliche Ergebnisse** (above-average results), high student satisfaction, successful berufsorientierung, and membership in **“Leistung macht Schule”** for gifted promotion.[1][2][6] Part of SPI program since 2006 with additional staffing since 2016; no specific awards listed, but noted for strong dual learning concepts and partnerships in inspections.[1][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)**, it serves secondary students (typically grades 7-10 or equivalent, leading to qualifications like MSA, BBR, or Abitur pathways per Berlin ISS model).[1][2] Core programs include performance-differentiated instruction, E-courses in **Mathematics, English, German, and Chemistry**, individual and productive learning, expanded **WAT (career orientation)** from grade 7, team-planned lessons with evaluation, practical interest-led learning in Uniwoche (project week), and daily basics in reading/writing/math.[1] Special supports cover students with special needs, dyslexia, German as a second language, and all-day programs (open and fixed schedules).[1] Artistic focus integrates creativity across subjects.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Hands-on artistic activities form the core, including woodworking, sewing, stage singing, band performances, theater productions, painting, photography, and crafts.[1] Extracurriculars (AGs) and leisure offers exist outside regular hours, with social competence training, external partnerships, and all-day programs promoting practical, life-relevant learning.[1] Student-created FAQ and building tour videos highlight daily life; events like Jahresabschluss and Lichtermarkt (noted for 30.11.2025) suggest community engagement.[1] No sports or exchanges are specified.[1]

No awards, rankings, certifications, or specific outcomes (e.g., graduation rates) are mentioned in the content.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admission criteria are linked on the site (Aufnahmekriterien), with a student FAQ video addressing common questions for newcomers.[1] As a **public tuition-free school (Öffentlich)**, enrollment follows Berlin public processes; contact via phone (+49 30 5597668), fax (+49 30 55151000), or email (sekretariat@11k06.schule.berlin.de).[1] Term calendar and daily info available online.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Rosenhain is a special needs school (Förderschule) that accompanies students with focuses on **"Lernen" (learning disabilities)**, **"geistige Entwicklung" (intellectual development)**, and **"Autismus" (autism)** toward a career-oriented graduation, emphasizing individual strengths in a supportive community.[2][3] The school's leitbild promotes education in a climate of security, enabling each student to feel valued, acquire life competencies for independent living, and integrate into society.[3] Key priorities include successful learning, social and personal competencies, vocational preparation, mobility training, environmental and health education, and extended care.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding date is mentioned; the school operates as a public institution in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district at Klingenthaler Str. 32, 12627 Berlin, serving about **260-280 students** from classes 3 to 10.[1][3][4] It functions as a campus-like community ("Auf unserem Campus ist Platz für jeden!") with open all-day operations, including a large outdoor area featuring a playground, school garden, basketball and football fields, and gymnasium.[4] Additional facilities include **Schülerfirmen** (4 student-run firms for 9th/10th graders), **eFöB** (extensive play and activity materials for independent leisure), and **Schulstation Scholle** (support service).[2] Affiliated programs like "Rückenwind," "projus," and "BRUNO 2" provide therapeutic small classes.[4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school covers **Grundschule (primary, classes 3-6)** and **Sekundarstufe I (secondary level 1, up to class 10)** with small classes (6-7 students), tailored schedules, and support from teachers, aides, and educators.[1][3][8] Core subjects include German, math, general studies, sports, art, and music, plus life skills, logopedics, and psychomotorics; **WAT** (vocational preparation) integrates student firms for 9th/10th graders.[2][8] Graduation pathways target **berufsorientierter Abschluss** (career-oriented), **Berufsbildungsreife (BBR)**, or **MSA**, with cooperative programs for Hauptschulabschluss.[4][6][8] German is the language of instruction.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize practical and social development: **student firms** for vocational training, **eFöB** for leisure skills, **Schulstation Scholle** for support, social trainings (classes 8-10), de-escalation via sports, woodworking (restoring furniture), Elterncafé, and home visits.[2][4][9] Projects include circus (CABUWAZI), summer festivals, and events like Zirkusprojekt and Sommerfest.[2] Therapeutic and mobility training, plus all-day care (VHG), foster independence.[3][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are detailed in the content; outcomes focus on preparing students for independent life, vocational entry, or reintegration to mainstream schools via projects like Rückenwind.[3][4] Enrollment supports career-oriented closures and societal integration.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school** (Öffentlich), admissions target students with specified special needs ("Lernen," "geistige Entwicklung," "Autismus"), subject to waitlists and applications ("JETZT BEWERBEN").[2][8] Contact via phone (030 9989181) or email (sekretariat@rosenhain.schule.berlin.de); process involves year-group placement in small, supportive classes.[1][6][8]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's pedagogical concept is outlined in its **Schulinternes Curriculum (SchiC)**, which aligns with Berlin's new framework curriculum for grades 1-10 and the introductory phase of grade 11, emphasizing transparency, binding content, and orientation for parents, students, and staff.[3] It concretizes education and upbringing based on framework categories, including language education, media literacy, and cross-disciplinary themes, developed collaboratively by the faculty and updated as needed (last aligned in 2019).[3] The school promotes **inclusive education**, integrating students with learning support needs into regular classes, with targeted small-group support in English for those with learning or intellectual development focuses, supported by school assistants, pedagogical aides, and six social pedagogues.[2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Operating since September 2006 under carrier **casablanca gGmbH**, the school serves the Wedding community in Berlin-Mitte district.[1][4] Located at **Ofener Str. 6, 13349 Berlin**, with a branch at Edinburger Str. 53.[4][6] Facilities include 18 classrooms/special rooms with Smartboards, 4 computer rooms, and extended hours (Monday-Thursday 8-16 Uhr, Friday until 13:45).[4] It operates the "Café Klosterhof" in the neighboring Paul-Gerhard-Stift as a practical learning project for grades 8-10.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with gymnasiale Oberstufe, it covers secondary levels including a primary school section and second educational pathway, offering increased hours in core subjects: German (5 instead of 4), English (4 instead of 3), Math (5 instead of 4), and Russian from grade 9.[2][4] **Produktives Lernen** allows students to choose internships (3 days/week) based on interests across three trimesters, with 2 school days focused on core learning for qualifications.[5] Graduation pathways include: Grade 9 - **Berufsbildungsreife (BBR)**; Grade 10 - **Berufsorientierender Abschluss (BOA)**, BBR, **erweiterte BBR (eBBR)**, **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)**; Grade 11+ pathways to Abitur via Lessing-Gymnasium preparation and guaranteed placement.[2][4][8] Enrollment for grade 11 and open house events (e.g., 12.02.2026) are highlighted.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Extracurriculars** include working groups (AGs) in dancing, artistic design, choir, student band, mediators, nutrition, digital media, sports, social skills training, and Rollhockey.[2][4] Practical experiences feature "Café Klosterhof" operation, 3-week internships in grades 9-10, project weeks for career orientation (grades 8-10), and career support via BVBO, "Berlin braucht dich", "Rock your life", and job agency.[2] International elements include COMENIUS partnerships and guest visits (e.g., Prinz Edward).[4][7] Competitions like Kamilala multilingual stories and ballads are promoted.[7] Vocational program "Produktives Lernen" emphasizes student choice in practical placements.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Received **"Schule ohne Rassismus - Schule mit Courage"** title in 2017.[2] Participated in Berlin's first inclusive sports festival in 2017 with a grade 9 class.[2] Features a 2017 inspection report from Senatsverwaltung für Bildung.[10] Ongoing successes in competitions (e.g., Kamilala, ballads) and practical projects like Café Klosterhof.[2][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, admission follows Berlin public criteria; at overdemand, based on average grade from primary school promotion prognosis.[6] Processes include grade 11 enrollment, open house days (e.g., 12.02.2026, 2025 events), and Nichtschülerprüfung options.[4][7] Evening school available for second pathway.[6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's philosophy emphasizes **joy in learning**, a profile-based education, and values of being peaceful, fair, and friendly together ("Gemeinsam sind wir friedlich, fair und freundlich"). It promotes diverse, project-oriented learning that strengthens individual interests and abilities, with modern, digital facilities enabling varied methods. The approach is holistic and individualized, preparing students for global challenges through language-sensitive instruction, democracy education, media literacy, and inclusive practices.[1][2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1922 in a building designed by Hans Winterstein (1919-1922), incorporating Villa Oppenheim, the school was renamed **Schule am Schloss** in 2010/2011 through the merger of Oppenheim-Schule and Elisabeth-Schule (previously Schlesien-Oberschule and Sophie-Charlotte-Schule) as part of Berlin's school reform.[4][6] Located at Schloßstr. 55A, 14059 Berlin (Charlottenburg, Bezirk Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf), with a branch at Kamminer Str. 17, it serves ~500 students and 55 teachers (2023/24 data).[2][3][4] Facilities include modern classrooms, specialist rooms, three gymnasiums, a school pond, workshop, sports facilities, and cafeteria; it operates part-time all-day schooling (TGB).[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** without gymnasial upper secondary, it covers secondary levels (Sekundarstufe), offering all Berlin general education qualifications: **BOA, BBR, MSA, up to Abitur**. Profile classes in year 7 focus on music, sports, natural sciences, language, IT, art, health, career orientation, and math-science; dual learning includes internships and practice groups. Languages: English (1st), Spanish (2nd); special features include welcome classes for new arrivals without German, language-sensitive math (BiSS-Transfer project, 2020-2025), inner differentiation in German/English/math, and integration for students with special needs.[1][2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars feature all-day programs with 7-8 hour days, profile lessons, team projects, AGs (working groups) in arts, sports, sciences, IT, language, and exam prep. Sports include Bundesjugendspiele, 1st sports festival with Trixitt; arts via Christmas concert, music profile; community events like open day (16.01.2026), career orientation. Partnerships include Preußische Schlösser (student-led tours at Schloss Charlottenburg) and BiSS research network.[1][2][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Received **Auszeichnung Berufsorientierung** (career orientation award, 2022); participates in BiSS-Transfer research for language-sensitive math, led by Prof. Dr. Susanne Prediger (TU Dortmund). Student numbers stable at ~500; noted for structural development as ISS.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school**, enrollment is via online form (student details, parent contact, message) or in-person; upcoming events include open day on 16.01.2026 and enrollment info (19.01.2026). Supports Praxislerngruppen for newcomers without German and Zweiter Bildungsweg (second educational path).[1][2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is **"Vielfalt leben, ZUkunft gestalten"** (Live diversity, shape the future), emphasizing a vibrant learning environment that fosters community, education, opportunities, and future prospects as core values outlined in its logo and Leitbild.[1][5] It positions itself as **"ein Ort zum Lernen und zum Leben"** (a place for learning and living), promoting diverse, creative, and practical education through projects in media, digital design, culture, and school development to inspire students, build confidence, and develop competencies.[1][5] The approach integrates community strength via its kollegium (faculty team), school social work for a supportive atmosphere, and initiatives like language education and reading culture.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Spandau's Staaken-West district, the school serves a local community with over 85 staff and 700+ students (recent figures: 526-647 students).[1][5] The campus, originally part of a 1930s military airfield kasernenanlage and former hospital site, resulted from a 2011 fusion of the Louise-Schroeder-Schule (Hauptschule) and Gottfried-Kinkel-Schule (Realschule); it features 10+ buildings, 100+ rooms, modern fachräume (specialized classrooms), a spacious schulhof (schoolyard) for outdoor learning, and recent 2015-2020 renovations including new annexes and a wooden multi-purpose hall.[1][7][8][9] It includes a cafeteria with partial meal provision and partners with local Spandau institutions.[1][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with **gymnasiale Oberstufe im Verbund** (upper secondary in partnership with Schule an der Haveldüne and Schule an der Jungfernheide), it offers all qualifications from **BBR (Berufsbildende Realschule)** to **Abitur**, including MSA support, with a **naturwissenschaftliches Profil (STEM focus)** and MiNT emphasis.[1][2][5][6] Languages include **English (1st foreign language)** and **French (2nd)**; admission uses average Förderprognose grade.[1][2] It operates as a teilgebundene Ganztagsschule (partially mandatory all-day school) with post-regular-hours AGs (extracurriculars) and Hauptfach Lernförderung (core subject tutoring); BuT-Lernförderung and dual learning via practical cooperations are highlighted.[1][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** such as cooking, swimming, basketball, football, art, robotics, textiles, drums/percussion, hip hop, experimenting, martial arts, table tennis, and school medical service, plus projects in journalism (Kleeblatt News), media production, language/reading culture, school development, and exchanges like "Kleeblatt meets Linden-Grundschule."[1][5][6] All-day offers on two days include sport/juvenile social work support, Konfliktlotsenprogramm (conflict mediation), sport emphasis, and digital/medienbildung in modern facilities.[1][6][7] Events like Tag der offenen Tür (open house) and community news via photos/videos enhance dynamic experiences.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are detailed in the content; strengths noted in a 2013 inspection include dual learning implementation, practical cooperations, school social work, and Ganztagsbetrieb, though areas like management, professionalization, and differentiated instruction showed development needs.[7] Current profiles highlight ongoing projects and community engagement without quantified outcomes like exam results or rankings.[1][2][5][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich), tuition-free** school, enrollment is via **Anmeldung** (registration) highlighted repeatedly on the site, likely based on local catchment and Förderprognose average grade for upper levels; processes include open days and newsletter sign-ups.[1][5] Contact: Brunsbütteler Damm 431-437, 13591 Berlin; Tel: 030/3640330; email: Sekretariat@kleeblatt.schule-berlin.de.[1][4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Wildgarten is a public special needs school (Förderschule) focusing on individualized support for students with learning challenges and intellectual development needs, operating under special education principles in small classes.[1][2][3] The school emphasizes sonderpädagogische Prinzipien (special pedagogical principles), integrating life skills training, practical content, and vocational preparation starting from 8th grade through "dual learning" that combines classroom instruction with real-world practice.[3] Staff including teachers, educators, and social workers support holistic development for approximately 170-300 students.[3][5][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district (Baumschulenweg neighborhood) at Köpenicker Landstraße 185A, 12437 Berlin, the school serves the local community near Plänterwald forest and is accessible via S-Bahn Baumschulenweg (10-minute walk) or buses 166/167/165.[1][2][6] A major renovation from 2017-2021 improved the facade with colorful window bands and interior corridors using differentiated color schemes for class identification, creating an inviting environment (BGF: 4,150 m²).[7] Recent expansion includes a three-story new building with 12 classrooms, 8 divisible rooms (some with accessible kitchens), multi-purpose room, art room, cafeteria, therapy spaces, staff areas, and all-day facilities to enhance inclusion.[8] A branch campus exists at Sachsenstr. 22, 12524 Berlin.[6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school covers **Grundschule** (primary, grades 1-4) and **Sekundarstufe I** (secondary level I, grades 5-10), with dual focus areas: **Lernen** (learning) and **Geistige Entwicklung** (intellectual development).[1][2][3][4] Programs include small-class individualized learning, practical life skills, vocational orientation via "dual learning" (school-praxis integration starting in primary), internships from 8th grade, and career preparation; no mentions of Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways typical of mainstream schools.[3] All-day offers utilize flexible spaces.[8]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Practical vocational insights begin in primary school and continue through Sekundarstufe I via dual learning and internships.[3] School social work provides counseling and support for students, teachers, and parents across all grades.[3][5][9] New facilities support all-day programs, group meals in kitchens, art activities, therapy, and inclusive multi-purpose spaces.[8] No specific extracurriculars like sports, arts exchanges, or clubs are detailed.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, test results, certifications, or specific outcomes (e.g., graduation rates) are mentioned in available content.[1-10] The school is part of Berlin's school construction initiative for special needs inclusion.[8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (Öffentlich), enrollment follows Berlin's standard public special needs process: diagnosis of Förderbedarf in Lernen or Geistige Entwicklung, typically via local education authorities; contact sekretariat@wildgarten.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 5328067 for inquiries.[2][6][10] Open house days are listed in Berlin's school calendar; the school seeks staff applications, indicating growth.[6][8]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule am Zille-Park is a state-funded special education center (Förderzentrum) with a focus on **learning disabilities** (Förderschwerpunkt "Lernen"), aiming to enable students to experience learning successes, build confidence in their abilities, spark motivation, and achieve sustainable progress.[1][3] It prepares students for independent professional and private lives through individualized, hands-on, and visually oriented instruction that strengthens their overall personality, with support from special educators, school social workers, social pedagogues, homework supervisors, pedagogical assistants, and educators.[1][3] The philosophy emphasizes a welcoming environment where all students and adults feel comfortable, enjoy attending school, and have the right to peaceful learning and interaction.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Formerly known as Theodor-Plievier-Oberschule, the school is located in Berlin-Mitte district, in the Wedding neighborhood at Ravenéstr. 10-12, 13347 Berlin-Gesundbrunnen.[3][4][5][7] No specific founding date is provided, but it operates as a public integrated secondary school (Integrierte Sekundarschule, ISS) under school number 01S01, led by Christina Wagner.[7][9] It includes an open all-day program (Ganztagsschule) with care until 16:00, holiday supervision, warm meals, homework help, leisure activities, and playtime; the Haus- und Einzelunterricht (home and individual instruction) originated from the Charité hospital school and is available exclusively in Mitte district.[1][2][10] Community support involves collaboration with youth offices, school psychological services, inclusion centers, and parents.[10]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves **grades 3-10** (Grundschule/primary from year 3-6; Sekundarstufe I/lower secondary up to 10), targeting students with learning disabilities where regular schools cannot meet needs, including those with speech impairments or awaiting placement elsewhere.[1][2][3][5] Curriculum follows Berlin's framework plans for primary school and intellectual development, focusing on German and math in individual settings, with personalized assessment and interest-based customization; English is offered as the first foreign language.[1][7][9] Special programs include Haus- und Einzelunterricht for transitional individual schooling (grades 1-10), vocational preparation from grade 5 via projects and a student company from grade 9, and potential transition to mainstream schools.[1][3][10] No mentions of Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Small classes (max 12 students per class in primary, e.g., 3/4, 5, 6) with fixed teams; over 30 primary students currently.[1] All-day support includes social work, educators for development, and vocational projects with partners; Haus- und Einzelunterricht stabilizes students while catching up on material.[1][3][10] Recent news covers short Christmas holidays, staff council elections in the cafeteria, and personnel meetings, indicating community events but no specific extracurriculars, sports, arts, or exchanges detailed.[10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, certifications, test results, or specific outcomes mentioned in the content.[1-10]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, admission requires a claim to special education for learning disabilities unmet at regular schools, via application from the home school to Berlin Mitte school authority, in collaboration with youth offices, SIBUZ (psychological/inclusion center), and parents.[1][10] Targets students grades 1-10 needing transitional individual support, those with speech issues, or awaiting other placements; primary intake from grade 3.[1][2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule an der Dahme is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with a gymnasiale Oberstufe, emphasizing individualized learning, versatile teaching methods, and project-based instruction tailored to students' paces from grade 7 onward.[1][2] The school fosters engagement through a mix of experienced and young teachers, integrating arts and culture via programs like "Kulturagenten für kreative Schulen Berlin," and promotes broad future perspectives including all qualifications up to Abitur.[1][4] Core values include high practical relevance, inner differentiation, and support for diverse learners via special education, gifted programs, and school social work.[2][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The main building dates to the late 19th century, expanded around the millennium with a new structure and sports hall between historic red-brick buildings.[2] A branch at Lobitzweg 1 serves productive learning for grade 9+ students from district Oberschulen.[2] Located in **Treptow-Köpenick (Köpenick)** at Glienicker Str. 24-30, 12557 Berlin (school number 09K08), it serves the local community with good material conditions, including interactive whiteboards in most rooms, networked computer rooms, workshops for wood/metal/plastics, teaching kitchen, textile workshop, and pottery studio.[4][6] Enrollment grew from 413 students in 2011/12 to 615 in 2023/24, with 57 teachers.[5] Led by Director T. Vetter.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves **grades 7+ (Sekundarstufe I and II)**, offering all exits: MSA, vocational paths (BBR), and **Abitur** via gymnasiale Oberstufe (since 2016/17 for qualified MSA holders).[1][4] Focuses on **productive learning** (high practice from grade 9, with weekly praxis days, internships in grades 8-10), dual learning, career orientation (e.g., Girls' Day, job workshops, cooperation with Oberstufenzentrum Wirtschaft und Sozialversicherung).[1][6] Languages: **English (from grade 7), French, Spanish (facultative)**.[5][6] Subjects include Deutsch, Math, Naturwissenschaften, Gesellschaftswissenschaften, Musik/Bildende Kunst, Wirtschaft-Arbeit-Technik, Sport, Sonderpädagogik, Begabtenförderung.[2] Block scheduling; Sek I starts 7:50 AM (up to 4 PM), Sek II 8:00-4 PM with breaks.[Scraped Content] Profiles: Produktives Lernen, Bildende Kunst.[5][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Offers **open all-day program**, project days, sports competitions, extracurriculars like AG Schulimkerei (school beekeeping), Schulgarten (school garden), mandatory AG weekly in grades 7-8.[1][2][Scraped Content] Arts integration via Kulturagenten; berufsorientierte praktika, Ausbildungstag events.[1][6] Facilities support hands-on: workshops, kitchen, sports hall; digital tools like 11-20 interactive boards.[1][4] Parental involvement via Elternarbeit, school social work; events like Tag der offenen Tür (next: Jan 29, 2026).[Scraped Content]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications detailed; participates in eEducation Berlin Masterplan and Kulturagenten program.[1] Steady enrollment growth to 615 students (2023/24); offers guaranteed vocational backups and Abitur equivalence to Gymnasium.[4][5] Belastungsstufe 3 (moderate load).[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, open enrollment for grades 7+; new 7th classes based on 26 students/class.[4] Tag der offenen Tür for prospective families (tours, demos, teacher talks; next Jan 29, 2026, 4:30-7 PM).[Scraped Content] Aufnahmekriterien bei Übernachfrage apply if oversubscribed; contact via info@dahmeschule.de or director for inquiries.[6][Scraped Content]

description:
# Schule an der Haveldüne: Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Core Mission:** The school's mission centers on the principle that **"Learning and Living" come together**[1]. The institution positions itself as a community-based learning environment where students develop their full potential through engaged teaching and diverse programs.

**Educational Values:** The school emphasizes several key principles[1]:
- Creating an inclusive community where differences and differing opinions are understood as strengths
- Zero tolerance for violence, racism, and sexism
- Active intervention against bullying
- Fostering **independence and self-efficacy** as primary educational goals
- Meeting individual student needs through diverse learning offerings

**Pedagogical Approach:** The school prioritizes **individualized education that fosters talents and transmits values**[2], combining academic rigor with practical, hands-on learning experiences.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**School Type and Location:** Schule an der Haveldüne is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) with a gymnasium upper school (gymnasiale Oberstufe)**[1][3], located in the Spandau district of Berlin. The school is a **public institution** (öffentlich)[3].

**Address and Access:** Jaczostr. 53-67, 13595 Berlin (Wilhelmstadt)[3]. The school is accessible via multiple bus routes: 134, M49, 136, 236, X34, and X49[7].

**Facilities and Technology:** The school has invested significantly in modern infrastructure[1]:
- One of the first Berlin schools to transition completely to chalk-free operations
- Smartboards in nearly every classroom enabling interactive instruction
- Mobile devices (tablets) for designated learning activities
- Student Wi-Fi network providing fiber-optic internet access throughout the school

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Enrollment:** The school welcomes students from **grade 7 onwards**[1].

**Graduation Pathways:** Students in the middle school (Mittelstufe) can pursue multiple certification levels[1]:
- Berufsorientierter Abschluss (BOA) – career-oriented certificate
- Berufsbildungsreife (BBR) – basic vocational qualification
- Erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife (eBBR) – extended vocational qualification
- Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA) – intermediate school-leaving certificate

**Upper School (Gymnasium):** Beginning in grade 11, students can complete the **Abitur (German university entrance qualification) in three years**[1].

**Elective Profile System (Wahlpflichtunterricht):** Starting in grade 7, students select from specialized profiles[6]:
- **Creative Profile:** Emphasizes artistic expression and creative ideas
- **Practical Profile:** Focuses on hands-on, project-based learning
- **Language and Science Profiles:** Introduce upper-school methodologies with linguistic and natural science emphases

**Elective Subjects:** The school offers diverse elective courses including[6]:
- Visual arts, music, and media
- School garden
- Mathematics (with advanced coursework)
- Natural sciences
- Workshop-based practical work
- Communication skills ("Meister der Kommunikation")
- English language studies

**Special Programs:**

*Produktives Lernen (Productive Learning):* Since 2012/13, the school operates two classes in this alternative learning model for grades 9-10[4]. This program supports students in developing **joy in learning, achieving school completion, and building real vocational perspectives**[4]. Students combine classroom learning with practical work placements, with all assignments connected to their workplace activities. Assessment occurs three times yearly through comprehensive educational reports (Bildungsberichte) with certificate status[4].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Sports and Competitions:** The school participates in various athletic activities[9]:
- Football tournaments with other Spandau integrated secondary schools
- Teacher-versus-student sports competitions
- School-internal ball sports competitions
- Rowing competitions (part of the rowing profile)

**Cultural Events:** The school hosts regular cultural programming, including a winter concert (scheduled for January 27, 2026)[1].

**Career and Study Orientation:** The school emphasizes practical preparation for post-secondary pathways through structured career guidance and university/vocational school visits[6].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Quality Certifications:** The school has earned two significant recognitions[1]:

- **2023 – Berlin Quality Seal for Excellent Career Orientation:** This certification recognizes secondary schools demonstrating exceptional vocational preparation and career guidance[1].

- **2017 – "Starke Schule" (Strong School) Award:** Under the motto "Strong School: Germany's Best Schools Leading to Vocational Readiness," the school's comprehensive educational concept and student engagement convinced the jury[1].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process:** The school accepts applications for the upcoming school year 2026/27[1]. Interested students, parents, and educators are invited to submit applications.

**For Produktives Lernen (Productive Learning) Program:** Applicants must submit[4]:
- Written application letter explaining motivation for the program
- Curriculum vitae with photograph
- Most recent school report card

Applications can be submitted via email, post, or in person at the school secretariat. Following application review, selected candidates participate in a six-week orientation phase in grade 8, with final admission to the program occurring in grade 9[4].

**Contact Information:** 
- Email: sekretariat@havelduene.schule.berlin.de[1]
- Phone: 030 / 365 09 73[2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule an der Jungfernheide operates as an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** emphasizing inclusive education, particularly as an inclusive special focus school for "Geistige Entwicklung" (intellectual development) since 2018/19, integrating up to three students per class in two classes per grade with a maximum of 21 students total.[1][2][4][6] It promotes a naturwissenschaftliches Profil (natural sciences profile), digital excellence, MINT (STEM) focus, career orientation, and chess education, as evidenced by its status as a "Deutsche Schachschule" and "Schachschule in Gold."[4][7] The approach supports diverse learners through small class sizes, part-time all-day operations, and pathways to various qualifications, fostering an inclusive, STEM-oriented, and supportive community environment.[2][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Spandau (Siemensstadt district) at Lenther Steig 1-3, 13629 Berlin, the school serves approximately 450-479 students with a staff including Schulleiterin Frau Stolle, deputy Herr Katzer, and secretariat staff Frau Simpson and Frau Weber.[2][5] It features three school buildings on the southern plot undergoing separate renovation, with plans to expand to a Gemeinschaftsschule (GemS 2-6-2) across northern and southern plots, including sports facilities on a 19,246 m² northern plot in a water protection area.[1] Facilities include a canteen (Mensa) for daily meals, snacks, and drinks, barrier-free access, and part-time all-day operations until 15:35 on Tuesdays and Thursdays; it participates in community events like Clean-Up Day and has involvement from parents, student representatives, and local authorities in planning.[2][4][7][8] Enrollment spans 479 students as of recent data.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serving grades 7-13, the curriculum covers **Fachbereiche** including Deutsch, Mathematik, Fremdsprachen (1st: English; 2nd: Spanish; elective: French), Naturwissenschaften, Gesellschaftswissenschaften, Sport, musisch-künstlerische Fächer, WAT (likely Werkstatt/Arbeitslehre-Technik), Ganztag/Starke Hände, Wahlpflichtkurse, Inklusion, and Willkommensklasse.[2][4] Graduation pathways include Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA), MSA-GO (with access to gymnasiale Oberstufe), Abitur (via upper secondary in partnership with Schule an der Haveldüne and Schule am Staakener Kleeblatt), erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife (eBBR), Berufsbildungsreife (BBR), and berufsorientierender Abschluss for students with learning focus.[2][3][4] Admission criteria include average grade from Förderprognose; it holds profiles in naturwissenschaftlich, berufsorientiert, and MINT-digital.[1][2][3][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars feature a **Hip Hop Klasse** for 2026/27, Mädchenschachtag (girls' chess day) on 30.09.2025, JungFernFunk (school radio/podcast), Elternkreis Inklusion (inclusion parent circle), and events like Tag der offenen Tür (open day) and Clean-Up Day 2025.[4][7] Sports and arts include dedicated subjects, Arbeitsgemeinschaften (clubs), and chess programs; all-day offerings provide extended learning with meals.[2][4][7] The school supports inklusion via Willkommensklasse and special focus classes, with digital and career-oriented activities.[2][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Achievements include **Schachschule in Gold** status since 2022 as a Deutsche Schachschule, Digitale Exzellente Schule, Exzellente Berufsorientierung, and MINT-Schule/MINT-digitale Schule.[4][7] It is an inclusive special focus school since 2018 with additional program support.[1][6] Student numbers have stabilized around 400-460 with 45-63 teachers over the past decade.[3] Upper secondary partnerships enable Abitur access.[2][3][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, enrollment is based on Förderprognose average grade, with capacity for up to three students per inclusive class (Förderschwerpunkt Geistige Entwicklung) in two classes per grade.[1][2][4] Contact sekretariat (Fr. Simpson & Fr. Weber) at +49 30 38392612 or info@sadj.eu for advice, questions, or registration; open events like Tag der offenen Tür facilitate inquiries.[5][7]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule an der Windmühle is a **sonderpädagogisches Förderzentrum** (special education support center) with a focus on **emotional and social development** in Berlin-Neukölln.[1][2][3] It emphasizes **small classes**, **individual support planning**, **customized instruction**, **intensive parent involvement**, and holistic care including after-school programs and external consultations for challenging students.[2] The school's approach integrates teaching with ambulatory special education consulting, diagnostics, and support in regional primary and secondary schools, in cooperation with partners like SIBUZ-Neukölln since 2013/14 and EJF-gAG for social work and childcare.[2][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding date is mentioned; the school has participated in the **Berliner Schule 2.0** program since 2012, providing advanced IT equipment including Promethean active whiteboards in all classrooms, stationary and mobile computers for modern media and internet use.[2] Located at **Buckower Damm 176, 12349 Berlin (Buckow, Neukölln district)**, it neighbors Britzer Garten and is accessible via BVG bus M44 (Britzer-Garten stop).[1][2][3] The community includes ~**60 students** (grades 1-10 in 8 classes), **15 teachers**, 1 secretary, 2 social workers, 4 educators (noted variably as 2 in some sections), and 1 janitor; leadership by Schulleiter **Bernd Schrader**.[2][3][5] Facilities support after-school care (Hort) until 16:00, holiday care, a student-run kiosk (grades 7-10), and dog-assisted programs.[2][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves **grades 1-10** (primary and Sekundarstufe I) as an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** and special needs center with **emotional/social development focus**.[1][2][3] Core instruction by 15 teachers emphasizes individualized planning and design; additional offerings include ambulatory consulting in Neukölln schools, single instruction in special cases, reliable half-day primary (VHG), and after-school Hort with homework support.[2][4] No details on languages, Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways; curriculum integrates projects, IT tools, and social training.[2][4] Student teachers and Referendariat (special education traineeship phase 2) are hosted.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Extracurriculars** include intensive afternoon care (grades 3-6), Hort until 16:00 with meals, homework, projects, play, movement, and holidays; student-managed kiosk (grades 7-10); dog-assisted social work and reading projects; student trips; soccer tournaments (participation and hosting); diverse projects with external partners.[2][4][7] Social workers offer counseling, class projects, career orientation, job applications, trip support, and mediation; parents get support via conferences, cafes, and parenting advice.[4] Hort goals foster independence, self-confidence, tolerance, responsibility, and creativity.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, test results, or outcome metrics (e.g., graduation rates) are specified in the content.[3] Participation in **Berliner Schule 2.0** highlights advanced digital infrastructure as a key feature.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public, tuition-free** school (öffentlich), enrollment follows standard Berlin special education placement processes (not detailed); contact Schulleiter Bernd Schrader for internships, Referendariat, or inquiries (Tel: 030 609724013); secretary Emine Hasanpaşaoğlu (030 609724020).[2][3][5][9] Serves Neukölln region with focus on students needing emotional/social support.[1][2]

description:
# Schule am Tierpark – Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Core Mission:** The school's vision is to **accompany students successfully through school into life**, supporting each student to achieve their individually best school graduation—from BBR (Berufsreife) through Abitur[2][6].

**Educational Approach:** The school emphasizes **student-centered, competency-oriented learning** with a focus on diversity and student participation[2]. Key philosophical pillars include:

- **Deeper Learning:** The school aims to establish independent, research-based learning more broadly across the institution, particularly in the upper secondary level through project-based work[2]
- **Four Core Content Areas:** Nature, Technology, Responsibility, and Creativity form the foundational pillars of school identity, integrated throughout grades 7-13[6]
- **Language Development:** Language education is embedded across all subject instruction to develop both subject-specific and academic language competencies[6]
- **New Assessment Culture:** The school is developing assessment practices that allow for diversity and capture complex competencies, including social skills[2]

**School Identity:** Described by the school as "**innovative, heterogeneous, challenging, creative, and curious**"[8].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**School Type and Structure:** **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) with gymnasiale Oberstufe** (integrated secondary school with upper secondary level)[1][2]. The school was formerly known as George-Orwell-Schule until October 2022[2].

**Location and Accessibility:**
- **Primary Address:** Sewanstraße 223, 10319 Berlin (Friedrichsfelde district)[5]
- **Secondary Location:** Dolgenseestraße 60, Haus B, 10319 Berlin[3][7]
- **District:** Lichtenberg[1]
- **Public Transportation:** Located 5 minutes from U-Bahnhof Tierpark (subway station) with direct bus access[4]

**Enrollment:** Approximately **742 students** across grades 7-13[2].

**Key Facilities and Resources:**
- **Smartboards:** All classrooms equipped with interactive smartboards[4]
- **Makerspace:** A collaborative workspace with 3D printers and tools for creative project implementation, designed to foster collaboration and practical skills[2]
- **Modern Media Equipment:** State-of-the-art film and photography technology for media literacy development[4]
- **Bicycle Storage:** Secure bike parking available on school grounds[4]
- **Outdoor Spaces:** School courtyard with recreational facilities[4]

**Community Partnerships:**
- **Official Partner School of Tierpark Berlin** (Berlin Zoo)[9]
- Cooperation with EPIZ (education center)[7]
- Kulturagenten-Schule (Cultural Agents School) program participant since 2022[8]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels:** Grades 7-13 (ages 12-19)[2]

**Languages Offered:**
- **First Foreign Language:** English[5]
- **Second Foreign Language Options:** French, Russian[5]
- **Third Foreign Language:** Spanish[5]

**Profile Selection:** Students choose from four profile areas in grade 7[2]:
- Nature
- Technology
- Responsibility
- Creativity

**Graduation Pathways:** The school awards all Berlin graduation certificates[6]:
- **BBR** (Berufsreife – basic vocational qualification)
- **MSA** (Mittlerer Schulabschluss – intermediate school certificate)
- **Abitur** (university entrance qualification)[2][6]

**Special Academic Programs:**

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Christburg Campus operates as a free carrier of state-recognized Christian schools and kindergartens in Berlin, with **Jesus at the center** of its work. Its mission is to enrich Berlin's educational offerings professionally and competently on a biblical foundation, helping children and youth grow into strong personalities aligned with the divine image of humanity and discover their callings.[3][7] Core values derive from Christian faith, emphasizing trust in God, parental responsibility, value transmission beyond competencies and knowledge, individual support without writing off students, and creating safe learning environments where Jesus is experiential, guided by the Holy Spirit.[1][3] The approach integrates state curricula with a Christian perspective, fostering purposeful living, societal engagement, and partnerships.[3][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1998 with the Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule starting as a Realschule with 18 students (state-recognized since 2002), the campus expanded to its current site in 1999—a protected Wilhelminian school ensemble at Christburger Str. 14-14B, Prenzlauer Berg, owned since 2005.[2][6] It now serves ~900 students across three kindergartens (e.g., Bunte Burg, Helles Nest, Immanuel-Kita), three primary schools (Elisabeth-Abegg-Grundschule, Immanuel-Grundschule), and the Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule (integrated secondary school).[1][2][3] Locations include Prenzlauer Berg (Pankow district), Spandau, and Hellersdorf; the Prenzlauer Berg site houses ~300 secondary students in bright rooms, with a familial atmosphere praised by parents for personal greetings, events, and small size where everyone knows each other.[2][7] Facilities feature historical brick buildings, school workshops for crafts, digital blackboards, and open all-day care (OGB) at primary level; no homework supervision is offered.[1][4][5][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The campus delivers state-recognized alternative education fulfilling all state learning goals, supplemented by Christian perspectives, from kindergarten through grade 13.[1][3][6] Primary schools (Grundschulen like Elisabeth-Abegg and Immanuel) offer English as first foreign language, open all-day care, year-group learning groups, and school entry phases.[5] The Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule is an integrated secondary school (ISS, grades 7-13, ~25 students/class) with English (1st foreign language) and Spanish (2nd); Sekundarstufe I leads to **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** after grade 10, Sekundarstufe II to **Abitur** in 3 years (gymnasial upper level since 2017).[1][4][6] Supports include free remedial classes, individual/group tutoring, learning plans, training, presentation/prüfung prep, peer tutoring, counseling, talent development, and BerlinPass partnerships; second educational pathway and vocational prep (application training, internships, job agency cooperation).[1][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize community and creativity: school festivals, performances, charity runs (Spendenlauf), summer festivals, Advent markets, Christmas services, baking, art projects (e.g., Seerosen like Monet, wood fish crafting), self-defense courses, garden trips, smartphone awareness, 48-hour prayer events, and student bands (e.g., Christmas song).[7] Sports/practical skills via workshops and internships; arts in regular classes (e.g., grade 6b creativity); excursions (e.g., August-Heyn garden school, neighborhood cleanups); home learning workshops; AG groups (project weeks).[1][4][7] Parental testimonials highlight patience, family atmosphere, and events fostering belonging.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Students achieve **above-average results** vs. other Berlin schools: nearly all pass MSA after grade 10, with high gymnasial recommendations; first Abitur cohort planned for 2020.[1][6] Awarded **Fair-Family-Gütesiegel** in 2014 for family engagement.[2] Strong individual support yields high success rates; ~900 students across sites with state oversight.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
No specific enrollment process details (e.g., applications, requirements) are provided in the content. **No tuition, fees, or cost details** (monthly/annual, registration, materials, meals, after-school, scholarships, income-based options) appear on scraped pages or listed PDFs (none found). As a private state-recognized school, ~2/3 costs are state-funded; remainder from parents/donors.[2] Contact Sekretariat@Christburg-Campus.de or visit www.christburg-campus.de for inquiries.[4][5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
SIS Swiss International School Berlin aims to develop students as **global citizens** with strong awareness of their roots through a bilingual, stimulating learning environment that fosters academic excellence, enjoyment in learning, and pride in achievements.[1][2] Core values include **lived bilingualism** (equal use of German and English in instruction and daily life), independence, value-orientation (honesty, respect, responsibility), a full-day school community with structured programs, and performance-oriented diversity.[1][2] The philosophy centers students' learning needs, emphasizes international orientation with local roots, continuous education from kindergarten to university entrance, technology-enhanced learning (e.g., digital tools, programming from early stages), and a vibrant, diverse community.[1][2][5]

SIS Berlin originated in **2016** when SIS took over Internationale Schule Villa Amalienhof in Germany.[5] It is part of the SIS Group, a pioneer in bilingual education since 1999, operating 20 schools worldwide (Switzerland, Brazil, Germany, Italy) with 4,844 students from 102 nationalities and 1,070 staff; SIS is a joint venture of Klett Group (Germany) and Kalaidos Education Group (Switzerland).[1][2][6] The school is privately financed by fees and state subsidies, politically/ideologically independent, and serves a diverse international community.[1][3] Located at **Heerstr. 463 (entrance at Reimerweg 11), 13593 Berlin (Spandau district)**, with office hours Monday-Friday 8 a.m.-3 p.m. and phone +49 30 3643 9820.[1] No specific facilities details are provided beyond a full-day setup with meals, homework supervision, and flexible care.[1][2]

SIS Berlin offers continuous bilingual (German/English) education from **kindergarten through primary school, secondary school (Years 7-10, state-recognized), and upper secondary (Years 11-13, state-approved)**, culminating in **German Abitur** and **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**.[1][2][3] Specific offerings include **Kindergarten, Grundschule (primary), Sekundarschule, Gymnasiale Oberstufe, and IBDP**; technology integration starts early (programming, media, e-safety).[1][2][5] IBDP authorized since **March 9, 2016** (IB code 051071, English instruction, coeducational day school).[3] Primary/secondary (Years 7-10) meet German compulsory attendance; equals state schools.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school provides a **ganztägige Schulgemeinschaft** (full-day program) with block lessons, midday meals, supervised homework, flexible morning/afternoon care, courses, and holiday programs.[1][2] A **Student Exchange Programme** is available group-wide.[2] Technology enriches learning across subjects.[2][5] Community features a "lebendige Schulgemeinschaft" (vibrant school community) with international diversity, alumni networks, and events like open days.[1][2] No specific sports, arts, or extracurricular details extracted beyond general full-day offerings and positive parent reviews on respectful environment and language clubs.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
SIS Berlin is an **IB World School** for Diploma Programme since 2016.[3][5] Part of SIS network accredited for IB at multiple sites (e.g., Basel, Zürich); participates in international benchmarking like ISA.[5] State-recognized private primary/secondary (Years 7-10), state-approved upper secondary.[1] Group serves 4,844 students from 102 nationalities; positive parent feedback on motivation, bilingual progress, and community.[2][4] No specific awards, rankings, or outcome stats (e.g., Abitur/IB scores) mentioned.
