description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Sophie-Scholl-Schule embodies the ideals of its namesake, Sophie Scholl, a resistance fighter against National Socialism, emphasizing **zivilcourage** (civil courage), **Gewaltlosigkeit** (non-violence), **Toleranz** (tolerance), **solidarisches Verhalten** (solidarity), and living **Demokratie** (democracy).[1][Curriculum Page] As a UNESCO project school, it commits to peace, global openness, and sustainable development, viewing student diversity as a strength and promoting inclusive education where all learners cooperate on shared content.[1][Curriculum Page] The school fosters social learning, self-responsible work, team collaboration, and individual support for strengths and weaknesses in a trust-based community.[1][Curriculum Page]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Operating for over 35 years, initially as a Gesamtschule since 1979 (merging prior schools like Sophie-Scholl-Oberschule, Georg-Graf-von-Arco-Oberschule, and Isergebirgs-Oberschule), it transitioned to an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with gymnasialer Oberstufe in 2010/11.[1][2] Located in Berlin-Schöneberg (Tempelhof-Schöneberg district) at Elßholzstraße 34–37, 10781 Berlin, the site is a listed architectural monument and part of the Schulzentrum Elßholzstraße.[1][2] Facilities include a **Ganztagsschule** (all-day school) with a Mensa for warm meals, a large Freizeitbereich (recreation area) supervised by social-pedagogical staff, and a new grand piano ("Victor") in the Lefèvre hall from a successful fundraising campaign.[1][Main Page] Contact: Tel. 030 90277 7171, Fax 030 90277 4465, kontakt@sophie-scholl-schule.eu.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serving grades 7–13 (Sekundarstufe I and II with gymnasialer Oberstufe), the ISS offers no grade repetition or probation year; students achieve Berlin qualifications after 10th grade: **BOA** (berufsorientierender Abschluss), **BBR**/eBBR (berufsbildender Realschulabschluss), **MSA** (mittlerer Schulabschluss).[1][3][9] Profile classes from 7th grade (chosen for 4 years) focus on **Kunst** (art), **Musik** (music), **MINT** (math, IT, natural sciences, technology; interdisciplinary Biologie, Chemie, Physik, Informatik), **WAT** (Wirtschaft, Arbeit, Technik), or **SESB** (Staatliche Europaschule Berlin for French).[1][4][7] As a SESB, it enables simultaneous **Abitur** and **Baccalauréat**; Abitur possible after 12 or 13 years.[1][4] Fremdsprachen: Englisch (required), Französisch, Spanisch, Latein. Leistungskurse include Bildende Kunst, Biologie, Chemie, Deutsch, Englisch, Französisch, Geschichte, Mathematik, Musik, Physik, Politische Weltkunde.[4] DELF French exams offered (June 2026).[Main Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars expand offerings via voluntary **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** in sports, languages, and arts/music.[1] All-day structure provides supervised recreation and social-pedagogical support.[1] Arts experiences include student exhibitions (7th–12th grades) and music performances (e.g., ensembles with choirs, bands, orchestras using the new grand piano).[Main Page] MINT activities feature competitions like Informatik-Biber (top prizes in 2025).[Main Page] School trips, partnerships, and projects promote engagement; inclusive differentiation supports diverse needs.[Curriculum Page]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**MINT-freundliche Schule** award for the third consecutive year (2025), a top national title by Kultusministerkonferenz.[Main Page] Staatliche Europaschule for French.[1][4] First German school named after Sophie Scholl; highly sought-after with profile-based selection over 10 years, competing with Gymnasien while reserving 25% spots for lower-prognosis students (incl. special needs).[2][5] Strong MINT competition results (e.g., 1st places in Informatik-Biber 2025).[Main Page] Music students performed at "musikalische Stolpersteine" conference (Nov 2025).[Main Page]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Tuition-free public **ISS** open to Berlin students with gymnasial/Sekundarschulprognose and English (Regelzweig) or French (SESB) as first foreign language; limited special needs spots without prognosis.[1][5][10] Profile-based selection via **Eignungsnachweis** (aptitude test) for Kunst, Musik, MINT, WAT (not grades); 25% reserved for prognosis ≤2.8.[5][7][10] Open Day: 23 Jan 2026, 16–19 Uhr (for 6th graders/parents); 7th grade enrollment Spring 2026.[1][Main Page]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is **"Be a champion in life"**, emphasizing success beyond sports careers, combining elite athletic training with academic achievement.[6] It honors Harald and Dorothee Poelchau, who resisted the Nazi regime, racism, and discrimination, promoting values of anti-racism and courage through sport at its historic Olympiapark location.[1][5] The philosophy integrates high-performance sports with education, fostering elite athletes who pursue Abitur while managing intensive training.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1973 as Poelchau-Oberschule (Gesamtschule) in Halemweg, Charlottenburg, designed by architects Lothar and Ingrid Juckel.[1] It developed a sports profile in 1997, relocated to Olympiapark in August 2015, and renamed **Sportschule im Olympiapark - Poelchau-Schule**.[1][2] The Olympiapark site, originally built in the 1920s-1930s by architects Werner and Walter March for the 1936 Olympics, offers optimal training facilities including sportstätten and a renovated historic building (Haus des Deutschen Sports).[1][2][3] Located in Berlin-Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district at Olympischer Platz 3 (also noted as Prinz-Friedrich-Karl-Weg 1), 14053 Berlin.[1][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
**Integrated Secondary School (ISS)** with a sports emphasis and **gymnasiale Oberstufe** leading to **Abitur**.[2][4] Focuses on elite athletes (Kaderathleten) balancing school with training; upper secondary schedule includes morning training (8-12) followed by regular classes.[2] Supports pathways for sports-talented students, though specific lower grades (e.g., MSA, BBR) not detailed.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Elite sports programs in multiple disciplines, including **Eliteschule des Sports** (since 2006, first in west Berlin), **Eliteschule des Fußballs** (since 2008), and Olympiastützpunkt Berlin.[1][2][4] Students engage in high-volume training, club/verband sessions, and competitions alongside academics; examples include athletes in athletics, tennis, modern pentathlon, and water polo.[2][5] Anti-racism initiatives like "Kein Platz für Rassismus im Sport" with events and patrons such as IOC President Thomas Bach.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- **Eliteschule des Sports** awarded by Deutscher Olympischer Sportbund (DOSB) on 11.12.2006.[1][2][4]
- **Eliteschule des Fußballs** by Deutscher Fußballverband on 20.11.2008.[1]
- Produces national/international athletes (e.g., Annika Schleu, Maurice Jüngling).[5]
- Recognized for combining elite sports with Abitur success.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school (Öffentlich)**, enrollment prioritizes sports-talented students (e.g., ranked athletes) for its elite programs; good performance in national rankings opens doors.[2] Specific processes not detailed, but focuses on Kaderathleten compatible with ISS structure; contact sport coordinator Carsten Richter.[4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission focuses on providing professional training in **Bühnentanz (stage dance)** and **Artistik (artistry/acrobatics)** combined with general education, enabling students to achieve state-recognized qualifications as **Staatlich geprüfte Bühnentänzerin/Tänzer** or **Staatlich geprüfte Artistin/Artist** while preparing them for careers in theaters, opera houses, variety shows, and companies worldwide.[7][2][1] Its educational philosophy emphasizes high artistic quality, student health preservation, ongoing artistic development in dance, and balancing professional demands with child welfare, including monitoring for eating disorders and supporting diverse body types.[2][6] As a state institution, it offers tuition-free education to national and international students starting from age 10 (5th grade).[7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1951 as the **Staatliche Ballettschule der DDR** on Gret Palucca's initiative, the ballet school merged in 1991 with the **Staatliche Schule für Artistik** (established 1956) to form the **Staatliche Ballett- und Artistikschule Berlin**.[1][2][3][7] Post-1990 reunification, Berlin consolidated the schools, with a new building completed in 2010 at a unified campus location.[1] The school is situated in Berlin's **Pankow** district at Erich-Weinert-Str. 103, 10409 Berlin, described as a modern campus supporting combined vocational and academic training.[2] It serves a community of aspiring professional dancers and artists, hosting events like the 10th Biennale Tanzausbildung in February 2026.[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Professional training begins in 5th grade (age 10) and combines with general education, culminating in **Abitur** (higher education entrance qualification) or **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** alongside state vocational certifications in stage dance or artistry; lateral entry is possible under certain conditions.[7][1][2] The ballet program evolved from a 3-year course (1951) to 4 years (1954) and 5 years (1958), while artistry follows a 2-year foundational phase (9th-10th grade) plus 2 years specialization, oriented toward the Moscow Circus School.[1][3] No **BBR** (vocational training qualification) is explicitly mentioned; focus is on state-recognized performing arts diplomas.[4][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in intensive daily training in ballet or acrobatics, integrated with general schooling on a modern campus, including probation rehearsals and public performances like the annual Absolventen-Gala at Wintergarten Varieté.[1][4][7] The school prepares for professional engagements, with historical ties to DDR state circuses, and hosts international events like the 2026 Biennale for young dancers.[7][3] Extracurriculars emphasize artistic development, health monitoring, and competitions, though specific sports or exchanges are not detailed.[6][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school has nearly 70 years of tradition as Germany's only institution awarding state-recognized artistry diplomas, producing professionals for theaters and circuses.[4][1] Alumni include world stars like Gregor Seyffert and winners of international competitions under past leaders Martin Puttke and Ursula Leesch.[6] It continues to train classical ballet professionals despite past controversies.[6] No current rankings or certifications are specified, but it hosts prestigious events like the Biennale Tanzausbildung.[7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public state school, enrollment is open to national and international students via **Eignungstests (aptitude tests)** or **Aufnahmeprüfungen (entrance exams)**, starting from 5th grade (age 10), with possible lateral entry under conditions; contact the school for applications.[7] No tuition, registration, or material fees apply.[7]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Theodor-Haubach-Schule (THS) emphasizes a close-knit community where respect, friendliness, reliability, creativity, and team spirit shape daily school life, fostering a respectful coexistence among students and staff.[3] The school's approach prioritizes practical, dual learning that connects classroom theory with real-world practice through workshops, external partnerships, and subjects like Wirtschaft-Arbeit-Technik (WAT), preparing students for vocational transitions via collaborations with businesses and the employment agency.[1][3] Acting principal Frau Köhnen envisions a "school as a place to live, not just learn," advocating project-based, interdisciplinary teaching, student involvement, and external support inspired by Montessori principles.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Grimmstr. 9-11, 12305 Berlin in the Tempelhof-Schöneberg district, THS is a public, tuition-free Integrated Secondary School (ISS) serving as a welcoming community for grades typically after primary school (e.g., up to 10th grade referenced).[1][2][3] No specific founding date is provided, but the acting principal joined via her teaching training and stayed, highlighting long-term staff commitment.[3] Facilities include modern classrooms, professional workshops for wood, metal, electronics, textiles, a state-of-the-art school kitchen promoting nutrition education and teamwork, and labs for chemistry, physics, biology experiments, plus IT and informatics rooms.[1][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an ISS, THS offers all-day classes with small class sizes (25-26 students) and pathways to Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA), (erweiterte) Berufsbildungsreife (BBR), and Berufsorientierender Abschluss (BOA), with potential for Abitur in Model B.[1][2][3][4] Elective courses span Arbeitslehre to informatics, art, and others; Spanish is available as a second foreign language, alongside a bilingual class per yeargroup teaching subjects in German and English (more English hours than standard).[3] Dual learning integrates theory and practice via WAT in workshops, external company visits, and a Berufswahlpass tracking vocational experiences.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERISES
Practical hands-on learning occurs in workshops (metal, wood, textiles), school kitchen (cooking, time management, social skills), and labs; bilingual students engage in projects enhancing language skills.[1][3] Vocational preparation includes business partnerships for internships and career exploration; values like team spirit are reinforced through communal activities.[3] No specific sports, arts, or exchanges are detailed beyond general extracurriculars in sports, art, and culture typical for ISS.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Student Noel achieved 3rd place in an international NEFTI competition in Albania (among 8 finalists from 17 countries), presenting a solo business plan in English across Real Market, Ideenmarket, and Constructive Market categories after national selection.[3] The school highlights modern facilities like the new kitchen as key to practical success; dual learning supports strong vocational transitions, though no broader awards or rankings are mentioned.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Parents are invited to an open house (Tag der offenen Tür) on January 22, 2026 (16:00-19:00), and children to a trial day (Schnuppertag) on January 14 or 15, 2026—registration required by December 17 (pre-current date context).[3] Formal enrollments occur in March 2026 for the upcoming school year; as a public ISS, it is tuition-free with no fees noted.[3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **TING-Schule** operates as a democratic school emphasizing "Ich entscheide!" (I decide!), with core principles of **independence, personal responsibility, self-directed learning, and genuine participation**. The weekly school assembly serves as the central pillar of the school community, inspired by the historical meaning of "TING" as a people's and court assembly. It is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) with Grundstufe** (integrated secondary school including primary level) in private sponsorship by Freie Demokratische Schule Berlin e.V., promoting self-determined daily school life.[1][3][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding date or history is mentioned in the content. The school community includes **approximately 50 students aged 5 to 17** who shape their school day democratically, supported by **seven permanent staff members**. Leadership includes Svenja Weiß and others like Dr. Eckhard Krug and Anne Viezens. It is located at **Berliner Str. 21, 13189 Berlin (Pankow district)**, with some sources noting variations like Berliner Straße 100 or Grosse Seestrasse 16, and alternative addresses like Berliner Allee 128 or Berliner Allee 128.[1][3][6][8] No facilities details (e.g., classrooms, playgrounds) are provided.[3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school follows a **self-determined learning model** in an **open-form all-day school (Ganztagsschule in offener Form)** with **mixed-age learning groups (jahrgangsübergreifende Lerngruppen)** across grades, including Grundstufe (primary) and secondary levels up to potentially 7-10. Foreign languages offered are **English (1st), French (2nd), and Japanese (3rd)**. It aligns with Sudbury school concepts and is a **state-approved replacement school (genehmigte Ersatzschule)**. No specific graduation pathways (e.g., Abitur, MSA, BBR) or curriculum details are explicitly stated; enrollment targets ages **5-13** year-round, serving students up to 17.[1][3][4][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **self-determined daily routines** through democratic participation and weekly assemblies. **Open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung)** is available. No mentions of extracurriculars, sports, arts, or exchanges.[1][3][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, certifications, test results, or outcome data (e.g., graduation rates) are mentioned in the content.[3][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Admissions**: Contact via email (ting@ting-schule.de) with phone number for prompt response; year-round enrollment for ages **5-13**. First-graders' process starts no earlier than **January of the school obligation year**. Phone: 030-53215656 or 0171-8198389.[3][9]  
**Fees**: No tuition amounts, registration fees, material fees, meal costs, after-school care costs, scholarships, or income-based options are mentioned on any pages. No PDFs with fee information found.[3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission draws inspiration from Vincent van Gogh, emphasizing **zukunftsorientierte (future-oriented) education** that prioritizes holistic development over purely functional skills, integrating a general curriculum with additional offerings.[3] It promotes identification with the school through joint involvement of students, parents, and staff, fostering practical competencies via hands-on experiences focused on career and study orientation.[1][3] Key values include inclusive education for students with special needs, media literacy, and collaborative school improvement, as seen in initiatives like the "Zukunftskonferenz" (Future Conference) where stakeholders identify strengths, weaknesses, and changes.[2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1985** as part of the Hohenschönhausen III development, the school evolved from a POS (polytechnische Oberschule) to a Realschule in 1992 (named after Vincent van Gogh in 1995), merged with other schools in 2003 and 2010 to become an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)**, incorporating Hauptschule elements.[1][3] Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Neu-Hohenschönhausen), at Wustrower Str. 26, it serves a central community position near S-Bahnhof Hohenschönhausen, with a branch at Rüdickenstr. 22.[1][3][4] Facilities include a three-story Plattenbau (panel building) from 1985 with a new elevator for accessibility, four ground-level natural science rooms shared with a music school, modern workshops (e.g., teaching kitchen, ceramics), sports facilities with three gymnasiums (one fully renovated), an annex since 2018 with interactive smartboards and computers, and recent renovations like hellgrün (light green) painted doors/walls, palms in the foyer, and student art.[2][3][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **ISS (Integrierte Sekundarschule)**, it serves grades 7-10 (secondary level), integrating Realschule and former Hauptschule tracks, with all classes accepting students with special educational needs.[1][3][4] Offers include teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb (part-time all-day program), zweiter Bildungsweg (second educational pathway), grundschulteil (primary extension), and gymnasiale Oberstufe (upper secondary gymnasium level).[4] Focuses on holistic general subjects plus practical, hands-on learning for berufs- und studienorientierung (career/study orientation); no specific languages, Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways detailed, but supports practical skills for vocationally inclined students.[2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Emphasizes practical, hands-on activities where vocationally oriented students "blühen auf" (thrive) through creating with their hands.[2][3] Extracurriculars include modern workshops (Lehrküche, Keramikwerkstatt), sports in three gymnasiums and an outdoor sports area with classroom, music school integration, and audiovisual equipment (whiteboards, beamers, laptops, smartboards).[3][4] Community events like the Zukunftskonferenz involve students in democratic processes (e.g., room budgeting); student art (e.g., van Gogh portrait) decorates spaces; plans for a "Wir für Vincent" parent association.[2][3] No specific sports teams, arts exchanges, or other extracurriculars mentioned.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, or certifications are explicitly mentioned in the content.[1][2][3][4] Recent progress includes securing external teachers (14 in 2021), building renovations ahead of 2028 schedule, and stakeholder-driven improvements via Zukunftskonferenz.[2] Prüfungsergebnisse (exam results) are referenced but not detailed.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (öffentlich), enrollment is open without fees; accepts students into all classes, including those with sonderpädagogischem Förderbedarf (special needs).[3][4] No specific process detailed; contact via phone (+49 30 92403020), email (11K07@11K07.schule.berlin.de), or website.[4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
**Waldseeschule** is a public aftercare school (Nachsorgeschule) for students in psychiatric treatment, serving grades 1–10 with a focus on pupils from Reinickendorf who cannot temporarily attend regular schools due to psychiatric illness or extended stays in child and youth psychiatry.[1][2][3] Its philosophy emphasizes a holistic approach prioritizing emotional, social, and cognitive development, individualized support for psychosocial stabilization, reintegration into regular schools, and preparation for societal participation through transparency, parental involvement, and multiprofessional teamwork including teachers, educators, social workers, and a psychologist.[1][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No founding date is mentioned; it operates as a public integrated secondary school (Integrierte Sekundarschule, ISS) in Berlin-Reinickendorf (Hermsdorf neighborhood) under the Berlin Senate Department, previously known as Wiesengrund-Schule.[2][6][7] The main site is at Olafstr. 32-34, 13467 Berlin, with small learning groups; additional locations include Dorfschule Lübars, SSF (Sozialpädagogisches Angebot mit schulischer Förderung for grades 7–10, max 12 students, in cooperation with der Steg gGmbH), and KMV (Krankenhaus des Maßregelvollzugs).[1][5][7] Facilities support accessibility (e.g., ramps, elevators for wheelchairs).[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves grades 1–10 (primary and Sekundarstufe I), following Berlin's framework curricula, Grundschulverordnung, Sekundarschulverordnung, and Sonderpädagogikverordnung.[1][3][5] All qualifications from grades 9 and 10 are available: **BOA, BBR, eBBR, MSA**.[1] Career orientation is emphasized in grades 8–10 with internships starting in grade 8, collaboration with Agentur für Arbeit, and support for transitions to work or further education.[3][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Focuses on small-group instruction for stabilization and reintegration; career preparation includes company internships from grade 8 and close guidance for grade 10 students needing extra support.[3][5] Multiprofessional team supports daily school life; opportunities for parental involvement in transitions.[3][4] No specific extracurriculars, sports, arts, or exchanges are detailed.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Positions itself as a transitional school aiming for reintegration into regular schools, school completion, and vocational perspectives; no awards, rankings, or specific outcome statistics (e.g., graduation rates) are mentioned.[3][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admission for students with severe psychiatric illnesses follows a fixed procedure in cooperation with SIBUZ Reinickendorf; all school place requests must be directed exclusively to SIBUZ Reinickendorf, not the school.[3] As a **public tuition-free school**, no fees apply.[1][7]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Wangari-Maathai-Internationale-Schule (WMIS) provides an excellent multilingual education primarily in German and English, oriented towards a globally interconnected world with the motto "Earth, Arts, Action."[2][3] It aims to encourage students to work for sustainable development, democracy, and peace, inspired by Wangari Maathai, the Kenyan Nobel Peace Prize laureate, environmental activist, and founder of the Green Belt Movement.[1][3] As a bilingual state-run international school, it emphasizes future-oriented work in all subjects, with expansions in artistic-musical areas (theatre, art, movement, language) as a Bega school since 2021 and strengthening of STEM (MINT) programs.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
WMIS, a public spin-off of the Nelson-Mandela-School (founded in 2000), opened in 2017 with only the 1st grade, primarily for children from highly mobile families (permanent Berlin residents admitted only in first grade).[1][3] It is categorized as an integrated secondary school with primary and upper secondary levels in the Berlin system, located at Babelsberger Str. 24, 10715 Berlin (Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district), which will remain the site for the next few years while a final location is sought.[1][2] The name honors Wangari Maathai, chosen by founding families, with an official ceremony on March 22, 2019, attended by Berlin officials and the Kenyan ambassador.[1] It serves highly mobile families and native-level German/English-speaking Berlin children, with classes balanced between mother-tongue German and English speakers.[3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
WMIS offers bilingual lessons in German and English from grade 1 upward, using flexible entrance classes (e.g., Flex groups A-D for grades 1-2 by 2019/20).[1][3] It is a compulsory all-day primary school ("gebundene Ganztagsschule") providing support in German for subject lessons.[3] The program includes development projects coordinated via school committees, working groups, and study days, with a focus on STEM and artistic-musical enhancements.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
As a Bega school since 2021, WMIS offers special programs in theatre, art, movement, and language.[2] It operates as an all-day school with balanced bilingual class compositions to support integration.[3] No specific sports, arts beyond Bega offerings, or exchanges are detailed.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
WMIS is Berlin's second state-run international school.[2][3] No awards, rankings, or specific outcome data (e.g., Abitur, MSA, BBR pathways) are mentioned.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Primarily for children from highly mobile families; only first-grade children permanently living in Berlin are admitted (native-level German or English speakers).[1][3] As a public tuition-free school, no fees apply; contact administration for details (e.g., secretary@wmis-berlin.de for applications).[2]

description:
# Wilhelm-Bölsche-Schule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Wilhelm-Bölsche-Schule operates under the motto **"Schule mit Herz und Verstand leben – für Jetzt und die Zukunft"** (Living school with heart and mind – for now and the future).[9] The school's core values center on **human dignity and democratic principles**, explicitly grounding its mission in Article 1 of the German Constitution: "The dignity of man is inviolable."[9]

The school embraces **diversity as enrichment rather than threat** and positions democracy as the highest good of society.[9] As a **"Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage"** (School Without Racism – School with Courage) since November 9, 2015, the school actively distances itself from hatred and right-wing ideology through projects and participatory practices.[9] The school emphasizes that it remains a democratic space where the entire school community celebrates pluralism daily.[9]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The school building has significant historical roots. The main structure was constructed in 1904–1905 by architects Peter Jürgensen and Jürgen Bachmann and was inaugurated on January 6, 1906.[5] Originally built as the König-Friedrich-Schule zu Friedrichshagen (a boys' progymnasium founded in 1900), the building later served as the Bölsche-Gymnasium from 1994 until its closure in 2000.[4][5] Following the gymnasium's dissolution, the building was converted into a Realschule and subsequently transformed into an Integrated Secondary School (Integrierte Sekundarschule) in 2010, adopting its current name.[4][5]

**Location and Community**

The school is located in **Berlin-Friedrichshagen**, in the **Treptow-Köpenick district** (Bezirk).[1][2] The address is Aßmannstraße 11, 12587 Berlin.[1] The school serves approximately **550 students**, with about **10 percent having special educational needs** (autism, learning disabilities, socio-emotional development, and language support) and approximately **15 percent with migration backgrounds.[2]** Many students do not live in the immediate vicinity and come from socioeconomically challenged families.[2]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school features a **historic main building** with distinctive architectural elements, including a six-axis central risalit with a large arched portal as the main entrance.[5] A significant renovation project converted the old gymnasium into a **multipurpose space serving as a cafeteria, kitchen, and leisure area**, completed between 2010–2012.[7] The school currently has an **expansion building under construction** on the school grounds, with a recent topping-out ceremony celebrating progress.[9] The campus includes **three computer rooms** and various specialized spaces.[6]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Type and Grades**

The Wilhelm-Bölsche-Schule is an **Integrated Secondary School (Integrierte Sekundarschule) without a gymnasium upper level**, serving grades 7–10.[1] The school has **fixed cooperation agreements for upper secondary education (Sekundarstufe 2)** that guarantee eligible students a legally secure place to earn an Abitur (university entrance qualification).[1]

**Languages**

Students learn **English as the first foreign language and French as the second foreign language.**[3][6]

**Academic Profiles and Programs**

The school offers a **career-oriented profile** (berufsorientiertes Profil) and a **health-focused profile** (gesundheitsbetontes Profil).[3] The curriculum includes the **Economics-Work-Technology (WAT) profile**, emphasizing practical and vocational preparation.[3][6]

**Dual Learning and Vocational Preparation**

The school provides **special forms of dual learning**, including:
- **Praxislerngruppen** (practical learning groups)[6]
- **Produktives Lernen** (productive learning)[6]
- **Betriebspraktika** (workplace internships)[6]
- **Betriebserkundungen** (business explorations)[6]
- **Bewerbungstraining** (application training)[6]

**Special Education and Support**

The school offers **special education support (sonderpädagogische Förderung)** and **comprehensive social-pedagogical support.**[2] **Remedial instruction** is available in mathematics, English, and German.[6] The school employs **social workers** (since school year 2025/26) who focus on social learning, democratic education, and community building through classroom councils, team training, and afternoon project offerings.[2] Support includes **media and experiential pedagogical approaches** designed to re-engage students in learning and help them overcome barriers.[2]

**School Organization**

The school operates on a **partially bound all-day schedule (Teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb)** and provides **meal service.**[6]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities and Working Groups**

The school offers a **broad range of school working groups (SchulAGs)** including:[6]
- Economics-Work-Technology (WAT)
- Visual Arts
- Sports
- Media and Hardware Technology
- Cooking
- Dramatic Arts
- Support and Challenge (Fördern und Fordern)
- Homework Support

**Student Support Services**

The school operates a **student club (Schülerclub)** and a **school station (Schulstation)** providing additional support services.[6]

**External Partnerships**

The school collaborates with external partners including:
- **Bölscheclub** – Jugendzentrum Friedrichshagen (youth center)[6]
- **METEUM** – Technical Children's and Youth Academy of the Technical Youth Education Association in Practice, in collaboration with the Treptow-Köpenick District Office and the Wuhlheide Recreation and Leisure Center (FEZ)[6]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Recognition and Certification**

The school holds the **"Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage"** (School Without Racism – School with Courage) certification, awarded on November 9, 2015.[9]

**Student Enrollment Trends**

Enrollment data shows growth over recent years:[3]
- 2022/23: 506 students
- 2023/24: 528 students

The school is classified at **stress level 3** (Belastungsstufe 3) according to the school typification system (STYPS).[3]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment for 2026/27**

For the school year 2026/27, the Wilhelm-Bölsche-Schule will open **six 7th-grade classes** with capacity for **156 students** (as of October 25, 2025).[9]

**Admission Process**

In cases of overdemand, admission follows § 6 of the Regulation on School Types and Educational Pathways of Secondary Level I (Sek I - VO):[9]

- **60% of places (93 spots)** are allocated based on the **support prognosis** (Förderprognose)
- **30% of places (47 spots)** are allocated through **lottery** (Losverfahren)
- **10% of places (16 spots)** are reserved in the lottery for **hardship cases** (besonderer Härtefall), which must be documented and justified at registration

**Registration Period**

The registration period for the 2026/27 school year runs from **March 5, 2026 to March 12, 2026.**[9] Students register at their **first-choice school** (Erstwunschschule).[9]

**Tuition**

As a **public school (öffentlich), the Wilhelm-Bölsche-Schule is tuition-free.**[6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Wilhelmstadt Schulen, operated by IBEB gGmbH, emphasizes individualized student development, comprehensive general education, and values-based personality growth, with a focus on fostering each student's unique strengths and viewing weaknesses as opportunities.[1][3][4][5] Core principles include respectful interaction, mutual appreciation, collective learning, openness, friendliness, and helpfulness among students, teachers, and parents; the Gymnasium holds the "Friedensschule" title for student engagement in peace initiatives.[1][5] The campus promotes high-level education for children from diverse backgrounds and languages, living by the motto "Wir leben Bildung" (We live education).[4][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on a former military barracks site in Spandau's Wilhelmstadt, the Gymnasium opened in 2004, followed by the Realschule (now Oberschule) in 2006, Grundschule in 2014, and two Kitas; a new Kita for over 200 children is under construction.[1][3][4][7][9] The campus serves ~290-352 culturally diverse students across schools (Gymnasium: grades 7-12 with ~290-322 students; Oberschule: ~130-352 students), taught by ~28 international teachers per school, with small classes (~24 students) enabling personalized support.[1][2][4][5][8] Facilities include a green campus, school cafeteria offering breakfast and fresh lunches, upgraded science labs for experiments and robotics, and spaces for projects.[4][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an ISS-Gymnasium (integrated secondary school with upper secondary level), it offers continuous grades 7-12 leading to Abitur, alongside Oberschule pathways to MSA and potentially BBR; Grundschule starts English from grade 1.[1][2][3][4][5][6] Curriculum follows Berlin's state frameworks, with multilingual support (English, plus Turkish, Spanish, Russian, French); electives include robotics and "Jugend forscht"; weekend exam prep and holiday courses boost outcomes.[2][4][5] Admission considers grades (5th/6th grade), primary school recommendation, social behavior, and child interviews.[2][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include diverse AGs (working groups) like silk painting, sports, graffiti, chess, community trips, Red Cross first aid training, and "Schüler mit Verantwortung" projects.[2] Events feature neighborhood festivals, World Cleanup Day, open monument day, summer festivals, Tanzania school projects, career fairs, and sports like "Jugend trainiert."[Main Page] International achievements include award-winning participation in VILIPO – Vilnius International Project Olympiad.[Main Page] Open-door policy allows anytime access for students/parents.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Abitur success rate is 99.3-100%, with a recent average grade of 2.2 and nine students scoring 1.0-1.2; MSA pass rate nearly/fully 100% via Saturday prep.[4][5][Main Page] Recognized as "Friedensschule"; high individual support yields strong preparation for university, apprenticeships, and careers.[1][2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admission to Gymnasium relies on 5th/6th grade grades, Grundschule recommendation, social behavior assessment, and child interviews; Oberschule considers grades and personal suitability beyond notes.[2][5] As a private school (staatlich anerkannte Ersatzschule), no fee details are provided in the content; no PDFs with fees found, and dedicated fees/admissions pages are empty.[Main Page][School Information]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **Willkommensschule TXL** provides temporary welcome classes for children and youth (ages 6-15) housed in the arrival center on the former Tegel Airport grounds, aiming to offer quick school placement upon arrival in Berlin, foster integration, and ensure a safe, reliable learning environment.[1][2] Its educational approach emphasizes holistic learning that respects students' individual personalities and experiences, prioritizing social-emotional needs alongside German language skills and subject content to build openness, curiosity, and readiness for new challenges.[1] Core values include respect, inclusivity, diversity, peer support through shared experiences, play-based relief, and preparation for transitions to regular or welcome classes at primary/integrated secondary schools or upper secondary centers (OSZ).[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **January 2024** with 35 children in a container building near the Tegel arrival center (P10 site), the school has grown to 300-450 students based on arrivals and departures; it operates as a temporary public offer until local schools assign places.[1][2][3] It serves refugee families from Ukraine and asylum-seeking families in the Tegel center, primarily in **Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district** (secondary site in Charlottenburg-Nord).[1][2][4] Two sites: **Grundstufe (primary)** at P10, Flughafen Tegel 1, 13405 Berlin (container facility, school bus from TXL accommodation); **Sekundarstufe (Sek 1)** at Saatwinkler Damm 42, 13627 Berlin (integrated secondary school branch of Nelson-Mandela-Schule).[1][4][5] Public transport access via Bus 410 and 123; secretariat open specific days (primary: Mon/Wed/Fri; secondary: Tue/Thu).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Offers places for ages **6-15** (up to 10th grade), with **Grundschule** (primary, 6-11 years) at P10 and **Sekundarstufe I** (secondary) at Saatwinkler Damm; focuses on German language, subject content, and integration preparation.[1][2][4] **Unterrichtszeiten (class times)**: Primary - 08:00-09:30 (1-2), 10:00-11:30 (3-4), 12:00-12:45 (5); Secondary - 08:30-09:15 (1), 09:30-11:00 (2), 11:15-12:45 (3).[Curriculum Page] Includes full-day program (Ganztagsangebot) with meals; literacy testing in mother tongue (Fridays) for class placement; uses BOLLE digital profiles; no specific mentions of Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways, as it is transitional.[1][6][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Emphasizes play, guided/free play in breaks and lessons for emotional processing, peer bonding, and skill-building; projects connect students with children from other schools and local community for German practice and social experiences.[1] Full-day care with meals secured; psychological and social worker support; sports via rented hall times (no on-site fields); regular contact with other schools and partners planned.[1][7] School bus for primary students from TXL accommodation.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, or certifications mentioned in the content; outcomes focus on successful transitions to regular schools or OSZ, with all compulsory school-age children guaranteed a place.[1][2][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Digital registration available in **German, Ukrainian, and English**; primary (6-11 years) placement tests on-site at TXL Container 12 (Mondays 8:30-10:00), P10 (Tuesdays 10:00-11:30, Wednesdays 8:00-9:30).[1] Secondary students tested Fridays for mother-tongue literacy and Latin script, assigned to classes, invited by email the following Wednesday; contact class teacher for BOLLE profile.[1][6] As a **public tuition-free school (Öffentlich)**, open to schulpflichtige (compulsory school-age) children/youth from the TXL center; email: sekretariat@wiko-txl.de; phones: 030 3480639941 (primary), 0151 67660239 (secondary).[1][4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Willy-Brandt-Schule emphasizes learning in a welcoming environment where students feel comfortable, stating "Lernen ist dort, wo wir uns wohlfühlen" (Learning happens where we feel good).[1] It promotes individualized education in small groups from grades 7 to 10, at each student's own pace and motivation, supported by over **72 educators**, learning coaches, and school social workers to guide students to successful school completion.[1] The school fosters a strong team culture across table groups, year-level teams, and leadership, viewing collective success as tied to shared effort between staff and students.[1] Key focuses include preparing students for digitalization across life and work domains and providing career orientation through interactive platforms with partners like IHK and Joblinge.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Grüntaler Str. 5, 13357 Berlin in the **Mitte** district (Wedding area), this public, tuition-free **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** became an official ISS with a gymnasiale Oberstufe starting in the 2025/26 school year, enabling all qualifications up to **Abitur** without changing schools.[1] No specific founding date is mentioned, but it positions itself centrally in Berlin's heart as a community-oriented "Teamschule."[1] Facilities and community details are limited; it offers a canteen (Mensa), multiprofessional support including special education, school social work, and a school psychologist, and maintains social media presence on Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube.[1] An open house was held on January 5, 2023, for students/teachers (9-12 Uhr) and parents/children (14-17 Uhr).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **ISS**, it serves grades 7-10 primarily in small groups, now extended with a gymnasiale Oberstufe for all qualifications including **Abitur**.[1] **MSA** (Mittlerer Schulabschluss) and **BBR** (Berufsbildungsreife) are implied through ISS structure and career transition support.[1][4] The curriculum emphasizes individualized learning, digitalization preparation, and career orientation with interactive tools and practical insights.[1] Downloads provide subject materials, school regulations (Hausordnung), and guidelines; multilingual support appears in registration info (German, English, Spanish, Turkish, Arabic).[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Learning occurs in small groups with personalized pacing, supported by coaches and social workers.[1] Career platforms offer interactive job info and teacher resources, partnering with IHK and Joblinge for practical exposure.[1] Digital tools prepare students for modern life challenges.[1] No specific extracurriculars like sports, arts, or exchanges are detailed beyond general team-based community and all-day ISS elements (e.g., sports, art, culture per model).[1][4] Social media and open houses facilitate community engagement.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school celebrates becoming an official **ISS with gymnasiale Oberstufe** in 2025/26 as a milestone, expanding education, support, and future opportunities.[1] No awards, rankings, or specific outcome statistics (e.g., graduation rates) are mentioned.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
New registration periods are open for future 7th-grade classes; parents must schedule a personal appointment with headmistress **Fr. Franke** by January 2026 via "Termin buchen."[1] As a **public tuition-free school**, no fees or tuition apply; process targets centralized Berlin enrollment for secondary entry.[1]

description:
# Wilma-Rudolph-Schule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Wilma-Rudolph-Schule is named after the Olympic athlete Wilma Rudolph, whose legacy serves as "inspiration and obligation" for the school's commitment to excellence[8]. The school operates as an **Integrated Secondary School (ISS) with a gymnasium upper level**, emphasizing **individual support for every student**[3]. A defining characteristic is that there is no grade retention at the school—all students progress through the system with individualized support[3].

The school's educational approach centers on **profile-based learning**, where students select specialized learning tracks from grades 7-10 that align with their interests and abilities[9]. The school integrates **social learning measures** and maintains a **cooperative school culture** with engaged faculty and a motivating, learning-conducive classroom climate[4].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

The school was established on August 25, 1994, when the former Berlin American High School was transformed into the Wilma-Rudolph-Oberschule[1]. It was **Zehlendorf's first integrated secondary school**[1].

**Location and Size**

- **Address:** Am Hegewinkel 2A, 14169 Berlin (Dahlem district)[2]
- **District:** Steglitz-Zehlendorf[1]
- **Student Population:** Approximately 980 students (2022/23)[1]; 1,004 students (2023/24)[2]
- **Faculty:** 103 teachers (2023/24)[2]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school offers comprehensive facilities including[5][6]:

- Library
- Dedicated building for 7th grade
- Computer labs (7 computer specialist rooms with approximately 150 computers)[4]
- Student kitchen
- Cafeteria and mensa
- Metal and woodworking workshops
- Art house
- Biology house
- Leisure spaces
- Sports field
- Solar installation for electricity generation[4]
- Student-operated radio studio[4]
- Wheelchair-accessible restrooms[6]

**Planned Improvements**

A major school renovation is planned through HOWOGE, budgeted at €93.5 million[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Structure and Graduation Pathways**

The school offers **all school-leaving certificates—from Berufsbildungsreife (vocational qualification) through Abitur (university entrance qualification)**[3]. Students progress through:
- Grades 7-10 (Secondary Level I)
- Grades 11-13 (Gymnasium upper level with Abitur pathway)

**Profile Classes (Grades 7-10)**

Since 2014/15, all 7th-grade classes are profile classes[4]. Students select one of six specialized profiles that shape their education through grade 10[1][9]:

| Profile | Focus Area | Details |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Sport** | Athletic development | Intensive training in Hockey, Rugby, Swimming, or Volleyball; co-taught with club trainers; established since 1999[8] |
| **Bilingual Englisch-Deutsch** | Language and international studies | English as a working language; established since 1999[1] |
| **Darstellen & Gestalten** | Creative arts | Theater and visual arts; small group work with large-scale presentations; new as of 2025/26[9] |
| **MINT** | Science and technology | Mathematics, Informatics, Natural Sciences, Technology; partnerships with external organizations[1] |
| **Musik (Instrumentalklasse)** | Music education | Systematic orchestra instrument instruction; students perform in class orchestra from the start[1] |
| **WAT (Wirtschaft-Arbeit-Technik)** | Practical skills | Production, technology, and career orientation; hands-on material work[1] |

**Elective Subjects (Wahlpflichtfächer)**

- **Grades 7-8:** Three-hour weekly instruction in French, Art, Natural Science, Spanish, Sport, or WAT[1]
- **Grades 9-10:** Four-hour weekly instruction in French, Art, Natural Science, Politics and Contemporary History, Spanish, Sport, or WAT[1]
- **Grade 11 and above:** Students select two elective courses that typically continue as advanced courses (Leistungskurse) through Abitur[1]

**Foreign Languages**

English, French, and Spanish[2][5]

**Upper Level (Gymnasium)**

The gymnasium upper level offers specialized courses with written Abitur examinations for students taking advanced courses[7].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities (AGs)**

The school offers a diverse range of student-led and interest-based activities[1][6]:

- School band
- Student company
- Billiards
- Rowing
- Bowling
- "School Without Racism" initiative
- School newspaper
- School radio
- Mediation
- Chess
- Textiles and crafts
- Event technology
- DELF (French language certification)
- School garden
- School health service
- Cooking
- Robotics
- Debate club
- Orchestra
- "Wilma Helps" (community service)
- Ninja Warrior (parkour course)[5][6]

**Academic Competitions**

The school has achieved notable success in **Jugend forscht** (Youth Research), with 7 students representing the school at the Berlin state competition in 2025[9]. Currently, 26 students across multiple grades are engaged in Jugend forscht projects[9].

**Sports Achievements**

The school's swimming team has defended its championship title[9].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Recognition and Certifications**

- **"Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage"** (School Without Racism – School with Courage) designation[6]
- **School Inspection Report (2017):** The school received positive evaluations for:
  - Engaged school leadership with cooperative management style
  - Broad range of profile classes in secondary level I
  - Constructive work culture with an open, engaged faculty
  - Motivating and learning-conducive classroom climate
  - Diverse measures for social learning[4]

**Areas for Development (per 2017 inspection)**

The inspection identified opportunities for growth in:
- Development of a part-time all-day program
- Targeted internal evaluation
- Individualized learning opportunities, particularly for independent and differentiated learning[4]

**Student Demand**

Demand for admission to grades 7 and 11 has consistently exceeded available spaces for years[4].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process for Grade 7 (School Year 2026/27)**

**Audition Period:** February 24 – March 13, 2026

All prospective students must participate in a **profile-based audition (Sichtung)** aligned with their interest or talent area[9]. Auditions last approximately 15-40 minutes per profile; the Sport profile requires approximately 90 minutes total across selected sports[9].

**Registration Period:** March 5-12, 2026

- **Regular hours:** 8:00 AM – 3:30 PM daily
- **Extended hours:** Tuesday, March 10: 8:00 AM – 6:00 PM[9]

**Required Documentation**

- Original registration form from primary school with hologram and average grade of educational prognosis
- Last two report cards and conduct/behavior report (copies)
- School internal data sheet with attachments 1 and 2 (printed, preferably completed digitally)[9]

**Admission Criteria**

In cases of oversubscription, selection is based on[6]:
- 50% educational prognosis grade
- 50% profile-based testing results

**Important Notes**

- Registration secures admission to a 7th-grade class but does **not guarantee placement at this specific school**[9]
- Final school placement is determined by the district school office by end of June[9]
- Counseling is provided during registration; families are encouraged to research the school beforehand[9]

**Special Needs Support**

Students with special educational needs (sonderpädagogischer Förderbedarf) should contact the special education coordinator, Herr Major, before registration[9]. Required documentation includes current support decree (valid through July 31, 2027) and special needs registration form[9].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's leitgedanke emphasizes enabling students to achieve the most suitable qualification for their abilities, including berufsorientierender Abschluss (BOA), Berufsbildungsreife (BBR), erweiterte Berufsbildungsreife (eBBR), Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA), or Übergangsberechtigung to gymnasiale Oberstufe for Abitur (MSA-GO), with the ultimate goal of securing apprenticeships or upper secondary progression.[1][2][4] Key focuses include **berufliche Orientierung** (vocational orientation), **MINT** (math, IT, natural sciences, technology), and **Sprachbildung** (language education), supported by innovative, real-life relevant programs and a collaborative community of over 900 members (students, parents, teachers, staff).[2][4][5] The approach prioritizes individualized pathways, digital learning, and social competencies for vocational and societal participation.[2][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding date is provided, but the school restructured into four buildings across two nearby sites (Blumenstr. 13 and Borchertweg 2) on January 14, 2022, following a successful 2010 fusion process praised for integration and development.[3][5][7] It serves ~850-900 students (861 as of Sep 2024) in Spandau district, with ~80 teachers across 16 subjects, led by Schulleiter Klaus Grünewald.[2][3][8] Facilities include three main buildings (A-C) at Blumenstr. (housing grades 7, 9, 11, science/art rooms, productive learning classes, admin, Schülerclub "downtown"), one at Borchertweg (grades 8/10), double sports hall, Helmut-Schleusener-Stadion (summer), other halls, SMART Boards in every room, iPads for upper secondary, Sprachwerkstatt (language workshop since 2016/17), modern library, LEGO Education Innovation Studio, and computer rooms.[2][5][6][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** with gymnasiale Oberstufe (in partnership with B.-Traven-Gemeinschaftsschule), it covers grades 7-13, including 29 regular classes (7-11), two upper grades (12-13), Turbo-Profilklassen (welcome classes in grade 7), Ukrainian welcome class, and productive learning in grades 9-10.[1][6][8] Programs feature Wahlpflichtunterricht (electives), Ganztagsprojekte (all-day projects), digitale Medienbildung (digital media), gemeinsamer Unterricht (joint teaching), Produktives Lernen (practical vocational prep), TLG Plus, Praxislerngruppe, Turboprofilklasse, berufliche Orientierung, MINT initiatives (e.g., Forschen, Entwickeln und Programmieren), and Sprachbildung.[1][2][3][6] Digital tools like SMART Boards and iPads enable IT-integrated learning across subjects.[2][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Experiences include all-day programs with sports modules (e.g., in Bruno-Gehrke-Halle), Schülerclub, Erasmus+ exchanges, seminar trips (e.g., Wannseeforum), Climate Breakfast events, Chemistree-Wettbewerb, and MINT competitions (robotics via LEGO studio).[1][4][5][7] Additional offerings: Projekt Verantwortung (responsibility project with Evangelischer Kirchenkreis Spandau), Kooperationen (partnerships), Förderverein (support association), Schulsozialarbeit (school social work).[1][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Qualitätssiegel für berufliche Orientierung** renewed for 2023-2027 (awarded Sep 20, 2023) for innovative, real-life vocational programs; **MINT-freundliche Schule** award (5th time by 2014, pilot in eEducation Masterplan Berlin); successes in MINT competitions (German/World championships in robotics).[2][4][7] High demand and positive inspection (2015) for fusion, leadership, differentiation, evaluations, social competencies, and broad vocational offerings.[7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public school, enrollment for grade 7 (starting 2026/27) occurs March 5-12, 2026, with appointments bookable from February via school link for Aufnahmegespräch (admission interview); general transition info at Berlin.de (Wohin nach der Grundschule?).[1]

# Zuckmayer-Schule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Zuckmayer-Schule defines itself as a **learning and living space, contact point, and meeting place** for its students[9]. The school's overarching educational goal is to develop **independently thinking students who act responsibly and enrich society through their performance and personality**[9].

Core educational values include fostering **elementary competencies, performance readiness, and moral principles grounded in universal human rights**[9]. The school explicitly commits to **tolerance, gender equality, democracy, and respectful, peaceful coexistence**[9]. Learning is designed to **awaken curiosity and bring joy**, with teachers serving as important **reference persons who motivate students to actively shape instruction**[9].

The school maintains zero tolerance for **hatred, violence, and racism**, emphasizing that these commitments require tolerance, willingness to dialogue, and proficiency in German[9].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Community Context**

The Zuckmayer-Schule is located in the **Rollbergviertel (Rollberg neighborhood) in North Neukölln**, Berlin[4][6]. The school is named after author **Carl Zuckmayer**[7]. It serves a **socially disadvantaged community**: 91% of students receive learning materials support (lernmittelbefreit), 97% are non-native German speakers, and 96% live in families receiving transfer payments[4].

The surrounding neighborhood is characterized by **high cultural diversity alongside socioeconomic challenges**, including family and individual stressors that create risk factors for school conflicts, violence, and student disengagement[4].

**Facilities**

The school offers diverse learning spaces including a **multimedia room, teaching kitchen, woodworking workshop, and sewing room**[7]. Extracurricular activities include **theater, school band, and media design including programming and web design**[7].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Type and Structure**

The Zuckmayer-Schule is an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS) without a gymnasium upper level** (grades 7-10)[3][7]. The school operates on a **block hour model** and offers **part-time all-day programming (teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb)**[7].

**Academic Priorities**

Two core academic emphases structure the curriculum:

1. **Language Development**: The school holds the **BISS Language Education Certificate** and emphasizes strengthened language competencies[7][9]. This includes **learning groups for newcomers without German language skills**[7].

2. **Career Orientation**: The school has developed a **career-oriented school profile** with particular support for students' pathways into vocational training[5][7][9]. This includes **job coaching, deepened career orientation, and apprenticeship placement support** through partnerships with organizations like AYEKOO e.V.[5]

**Practical Learning**

The curriculum incorporates **internships and practical workshops**: students in grade 8 participate in workshop days, while grades 9 and 10 complete internships[10].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Social and Emotional Development**

The school provides **school social work services** offering sociopedagogical programs including **social competency training, Fairplayer programs, and girls' groups**[1]. Daily interaction emphasizes training in **diligence, punctuality, sense of duty, reliability, and courtesy**[9].

**Extracurricular Activities and Events**

Student experiences include:
- **Field trips and class journeys** (e.g., 9th grade trips to nature sites with activities like forest school, hiking, swimming, and talent shows)[10]
- **Cross-country running events** (56 students participated in a 3.2 km run at Britzer Garten in October 2025)[10]
- **Art exhibitions** (9th grade students and art course participants exhibited work titled "The Human in the Ecosystem—An Artistic Journey" at Körnerpark Gallery in July 2025)[10]
- **Theater, school band, and media design projects**[7]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Certifications and Initiatives**

The school holds the **BISS Language Education Certificate** recognizing its language development focus[7][9]. The school participates in the **"Schule macht stark" (School Makes Strong) initiative** and the **"Berlin Challenge" program**, both aimed at creating optimal learning conditions and fostering community cohesion[4][6].

**Recognition**

The school actively works to counter negative stereotypes about its location, emphasizing that **Neukölln is diverse and colorful** and that cultural, ethnic, and social diversity should be a gain for school community life[6].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a **public school (öffentlich), the Zuckmayer-Schule is tuition-free**[8]. The school is part of the Berlin public education system and follows standard Berlin enrollment procedures for integrated secondary schools. Specific admissions requirements are not detailed in the available content, but as a public ISS without a gymnasium upper level, it serves students through grade 10 (ages 12-16 approximately).

The school actively welcomes students with diverse backgrounds and language needs, including **newcomers without prior German language skills**[7].

description:
# Gymnasium 09Y12 School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Gymnasium 09Y12 is committed to fostering **respectful and tolerant interaction** among all members of the school community.[1] The school's educational mission centers on equipping students with knowledge, skills, and values that enable them to make independent decisions, pursue lifelong learning, and actively shape their professional and personal development as well as society's future.[1]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Location**

Gymnasium 09Y12 was established in the 2023/24 school year as the 12th gymnasium in the Treptow-Köpenick district.[1][2] The school is located at Kiefholzstr. 274, 12437 Berlin (Baumschulenweg), with convenient access to the S-Bahnhof Baumschulenweg station to the east.[2]

**Facilities**

The school occupies a **historically protected building** (Denkmalschutz) that was originally constructed in 1900/1901 by architect Eugen Kühn for the Treptow municipality.[2] The building has been fully renovated and features modern facilities including specialized subject rooms, media centers, and a historic auditorium on the upper floor.[1]

**Historical Context**

The building previously served as a community school and later as a primary school. During the German Democratic Republic era, it housed the 5th Polytechnic Secondary School (POS) "Alexander Matrossow." Following German reunification in 1991, the building became home to Treptow-Kolleg until the gymnasium's establishment.[2]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Class Structure and Schedule**

The school operates as an **open all-day school** (offene Ganztagsschule) with a maximum of 28 students per class, with instruction beginning at 8:35 AM.[1]

**Language Program**

Students study **English as the first foreign language**, with **French or Spanish** available as the second foreign language.[1] The language program includes special initiatives such as the Francemobil program for French instruction.[7]

**Divided Instruction (Teilungsunterricht)**

**Natural sciences and information technology** (ITG) are taught in divided classes, allowing for smaller group instruction and hands-on learning.[1]

**Support and Remedial Programs**

The school provides **grade-level support instruction** in German and Mathematics, with additional support for reading and spelling difficulties (LRS-Förderung).[1] The gymnasium offers comprehensive individual support options, including gifted student programs and learning coaching.[8]

**Academic Rigor**

As a gymnasium, the school maintains elevated academic standards and requires students to demonstrate sustained achievement to progress to the next grade level.[5]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school organizes diverse learning experiences including:

- **Science activities**: Experimentation days (Experimentiertag)[7]
- **Arts programs**: Graffiti workshops and student artwork exhibitions[7]
- **Violence prevention**: Project days for 8th grade students focused on violence prevention and self-defense[7]
- **Academic competitions**: Participation in mathematics competitions (Känguru-Wettbewerb) and geography knowledge competitions (Diercke Wissen)[7]
- **Language enrichment**: Francemobil program for French language instruction[7]
- **Orientation activities**: School orientation rallies and welcome ceremonies for new students[7]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain specific information about awards, rankings, certifications, or Abitur success rates for Gymnasium 09Y12. As a newly established school (founded 2023/24), standardized achievement data may not yet be publicly available.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The school is a **public gymnasium** and therefore operates on a tuition-free basis.[3] As a public school in Berlin's secondary education system, admission follows the standard Berlin gymnasium selection process. The school received 125 first-choice applications for 84 available regular student places in recent enrollment cycles, indicating strong demand.[6]

An upcoming **open house day** (Tag der offenen Tür) is scheduled for January 29, 2026, from 4:00 PM to 6:30 PM, providing prospective families an opportunity to visit the school.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Albert-Einstein-Gymnasium aims to prepare students to become independent, resilient, and joyful young people capable of critically thinking, shaping their lives responsibly in society, and using democratic tools to influence school life.[Main Page][Curriculum Page] Core values include respect, tolerance, mutual consideration, joy in learning, performance capability, and social competence, fostered through praise, constructive criticism, interdisciplinary teaching, and lifelong learning.[Curriculum Page] The educational approach emphasizes a **musical profile** integrating music, art, and theater into daily life; **intercultural competence** via the Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin (SESB) for bilingual German-Italian education promoting European identity; and social cohesion through encounters with diverse cultures, partnerships in business, politics, and culture, and strong technical facilities.[Main Page][About Page][Curriculum Page]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding date is mentioned in the content. The school is located in Berlin-Neukölln's Britz district at **Parchimer Allee 109, 12359 Berlin**, easily accessible via U7 station Parchimer Allee, serving over 1,000 students (1,044 as of recent data) and 105 teachers.[Main Page][1] It shares a campus with the Alfred-Nobel-Schule (ISS) and connects to two SESB primary schools (Finow-Grundschule and Hermann-Nohl-Grundschule) for seamless bilingual progression.[About Page] Facilities include award-winning architecture with art displays, foyers and gardens for performances/exhibitions, two prop storage rooms, a portable stage, sports hall for events, well-equipped library, and good technical infrastructure.[Curriculum Page][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **six-track Gymnasium** (sechszügiges Gymnasium), it spans grades leading to **Abitur** after 12 school years, equivalent to the Italian Liceo level; no mentions of MSA or BBR.[About Page][1] Two tracks follow the **bilingual German-Italian SESB profile**, with native-speaker teachers delivering equal instruction in both languages from primary feeders or qualified transfers; remaining tracks are regular.[Main Page][About Page] Subjects include Bildende Kunst/Kunst, Biologie, Chemie, Deutsch, Englisch, Erdkunde, Französisch, Geschichte, Informatik, Italienisch, Mathematik, Musik, Physik, Politische Weltkunde.[1] **Musical emphasis** features extended art/music hours in grades 9-10, elective AGs, collaborations with Musikschule Paul Hindemith Neukölln, theater/opera visits, and interdisciplinary projects.[4][Curriculum Page] Intercultural focus includes Wahlpflichtfächer, AGs, and upper school integration across tracks.[About Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize arts (music trips, Christmas concerts, theater workshops, art excursions to Leipzig museums/Galerie/Baumwollspinnerei, Leistungskurse in Berlinische Galerie), languages/culture (Turin Erasmus+ exchange, Paris Lycée Sévigne visit with Arc de Triomphe ceremony, Latin educational trip to Naples/Pompeii), diplomacy (BERMUN 2025 representing Latvia/UAE), anti-racism (podcast from "Schule ohne Rassismus" project), environment (Stadtradeln cycling: 56 participants, 10,253 km), and chess (SES title defense).[Main Page] Guest speakers include Shoah survivor Ruth Winkelmann and Ravensbrück committee president Ambra Laurenzi. Sports/chess and joint events foster community.[Main Page]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Students won two prizes at the **72. Europäischer Wettbewerb 2025 "Europa - aber sicher"** and were received at the Bundeskanzleramt.[Main Page][1] Retained **SESB-Schulschachturnier** title with golden trophy.[Main Page] Recognized as **Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin (Deutsch-Italienisch)**, unique nationwide model, with award-winning architecture.[About Page][4] Enrollment stable around 1,000-1,073 students (2012-2024), teacher-student ratio ~2.3.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich), tuition-free** Gymnasium, enrollment prioritizes SESB track students from affiliated primaries (Finow- or Hermann-Nohl-Grundschule) with bilingual proficiency; quereinsteiger (transfers) accepted with proven oral/written skills in German and Italian.[About Page] Regular tracks open broadly; parents/elternschaft actively engage. Check Vertretungsplan, Moodle, WebUntis for illnesses/career info (Studiengänge, Praktika, open days).[Main Page] Newsletter subscription available.[About Page]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Albrecht-Dürer-Gymnasium emphasizes a culturally diverse environment fostering engaged education, creativity, and community.[3][5] It implements **Dalton-Pädagogik** since 2011/12, promoting free learning, personal responsibility, and collaborative growth, where students independently plan their schedules in 2-hour blocks.[1][4][5][10] Core values include a **musikbetonte Schule** (music-focused school) for experiencing and developing musical talents in daily routines, **Begabungsförderung** (gifted education) to identify strengths and unlock potentials via programs like the Sommerakademie since 2006, and an **offener Ganztag** (open all-day program) for extended learning, play, and exploration.[5][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1906 as an Oberrealschule for male students preparing for math-technical careers and scientific studies, the school—named after artist Albrecht Dürer with a rhinoceros mascot—lifted gender restrictions post-WWII and expanded facilities like the attic in the 1980s.[1] Located in Berlin-Neukölln (Bezirk Neukölln, Emser Str. 133-137, 12051 Berlin), the protected historic building serves a diverse community with ~648 students and 57 teachers (2023/24 data).[1][2][6] Facilities include an aula, cafeteria, three computer labs, laptops/beamers/smartboards, school garden, wheelchair accessibility, and parking for disabled persons.[6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (grades 5–12/13, offering G8 Abitur pathway), it features musisches (arts/music) and naturwissenschaftliches (STEM/MINT-friendly) profiles with languages: S1 English/French, S2 English/Latin, third languages French/Latin/Polish.[2][4][6] Upper-level Leistungskurse (advanced courses) include Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, French, History, IT, Latin, Math, Music, Physics, Political Science.[2] Special elements: Dalton scheduling, gifted tracks (Schnelllernerzug since 1999), MINT emphasis (e.g., Lego Robotics AG, Schulgarten AG, Math Olympiads, Känguru contest, TU Berlin cooperation), and societal sciences, creative subjects, sports.[1][4][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars feature music (instrumental classes, Musik-AGs, Weihnachtskonzerts, Zafraan Ensemble performances), arts/culture (Albrecht Dürer Lectures, Neukölln tanzt! festival), exchanges (Schüleraustausch, Comenius project), career orientation (Berufsorientierung), and all-day options.[5][7][8] Sports and creative subjects are integrated; partnerships include Rotary Club Berlin Spree and eEducation Berlin Masterplan.[4][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Abitur averages are strong: 2.45 (92% pass rate, 2024); 2.44 (96%, 2023); 2.25 (95%, 2022), improving from 2.61 (89%, 2013).[2] Recognized as MINT-freundlich, first Berlin Gymnasium with Dalton-Pädagogik, and supports gifted programs; participates in math competitions.[1][4][10]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public Gymnasium, enrollment requires documents (July 2025/Jan 2026 report cards, Schnelllernertest confirmation, primary school prognosis/form, photo) and an intake interview for new 5th graders.[7] New 7th graders need a music test (March 2026); trial days (Schnupperunterricht) offered Dec 2025/Jan 2026; info evenings Nov 2025; placement notification by mid-June 2026.[5][7]
