description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Alexander-von-Humboldt-Gymnasium (AvH) is a versatile and traditionally rooted Gymnasium emphasizing holistic education, with a strong focus on intensifying mathematical and natural sciences instruction to highlight their methodological diversity, everyday relevance, and role in addressing deficits in these areas.[1][2] It promotes problem-solving strategies, interdisciplinary aspects, student experiments, knowledge management, information evaluation, and comprehensive preparation for life through subjects like Studium und Beruf (career and study orientation, fostering self-reflection and life perspectives) and Sport (enhancing physical health, teamwork, and fairness).[1] The school values engagement in democracy, anti-discrimination, remembrance of historical events like the Holocaust, and multilingualism.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as the successor to the Dorotheenschule zu Köpenick, the school opened in 1929 in a Bauhaus-style building designed by architect Max Taut, a listed architectural monument featuring a winkelförmiger complex with a five-story classroom wing, four-story administrative and aula wing, and steel-concrete double gymnasium.[3] After German reunification in 1991, it became Köpenick's first Gymnasium; a new sports field was added in 2006 on former 1950s grounds.[3] Located in Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district (Köpenick Ortsteil, Spindlersfeld near the Spree) at Oberspreestr. 173-181, 12555 Berlin, it serves approximately 805-808 students with 67-71 teachers.[1][2] Facilities include an aula, two computer rooms (16 PCs each), two turnhallen, wheelchair accessibility (ground level or ramps), and a new sports field; it uses IServ school server and maintains a public calendar and newsletters.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a Gymnasium (Model C), it offers education from grade 7 (post-primary transition) leading to **Abitur** (university entrance qualification), with consistent 92-100% pass rates and average Abitur grades of 2.11-2.49 (2013-2024).[1][4] Core languages are S1 Englisch/Französisch, with 3rd foreign language **Latein** and 4th **Russisch**; Leistungskurse (advanced courses) include Biologie, Chemie, Deutsch, Englisch, Französisch, Geschichte, Mathematik, Physik, Politische Bildung.[1][2] Special program: **Profilklasse** (math-natural sciences profile since 2001/2002), intensifying Math, Physik, Chemie with advanced/early content, experiments, problem-solving, and interdisciplinary focus; leistungsorientierte (performance-based) admission criteria apply, with probationary period (no failures recorded).[1] Additional offerings: Studium und Beruf, Sport, preparation for DELF French diploma; no Schnelllernerklassen or grade 5 starts.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse extracurriculars, including Fremdsprachentag (foreign language day with games, acting, singing in Englisch, Französisch, Russisch, Latein led by upper grades), student exchanges (e.g., with Texas, featuring cultural/political/educational activities), AGs (working groups) like Courage-AG for Stolperstein cleaning against antisemitism, and projects on discrimination, democracy ("Wir machen Demokratie"), local Jewish history ("Gegen das Vergessen"), and Russisch Bundescup competitions.[1] Sports include Fußball, Volleyball, two turnhallen, new sports field; arts/cultural: Chor, Schulhofgestaltung, exhibitions; other AGs: Mathematik, Physik, Chinesisch.[1][2] Partners support kulturelle and politische Bildung, schulsozialarbeit, and berufsorientierung.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- **Abitur success**: 92-100% pass rates (2013-2024), average grades 2.11-2.49.[1]
- Class 9.4 won Sonderpreis at Berliner Schülerwettbewerb 2025 for "Wir machen Demokratie" anti-discrimination project with posters and video.[1]
- Students from 10.4 competed in Bundescup 2025 "Spielend Russisch lernen" in Gotha.[1]
- **Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage** award in 2017.[3]
- No recorded expulsions from Profilklasse probation.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, enrollment for grade 7 transition occurs via online forms (Anmeldebogen, Kontaktdaten, Datenschutz, Geschwisterkind preference, Profilklasse form) during specified periods in KW 10-11 (March 2026: March 5-6, 9-12), processed without priority by submission order.[1] Profilklasse requires leistungsorientierte criteria (details at open house or from Math/Physik/Chemie teachers); general over-demand uses Förderprognose average grade or other criteria.[1][2] In-person registration times listed; no disadvantages for later dates.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Andreas-Gymnasium aims to provide high general education on a broad basis, emphasizing performance orientation alongside development of media and methodological competence, teamwork, and communication skills.[6][7] It offers two pathways to Abitur: a **mathematisch-naturwissenschaftlichen Zweig** (MINT-focused, with intensified Math at 5 hours/week and Biology, Chemistry, Physics at 2 hours each) and a broad **allgemeinbildenden Zweig** prioritizing languages, social sciences, and arts.[1][2][6] As a school with special pedagogical profile, it is part of Berlin's network of mathematically-natural science profiled schools and promotes comprehensive studies and career orientation.[1][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in October 1833 as "Stralauer Schule" with 4 teachers and 54 students in Berlin's eastern working-class area, it evolved through Kaiserreich, Weimar Republic, National Socialism, and SED regime, merging with Georg-Friedrich-Händel-Oberschule in 1968, renamed EOS Friedrich-Engels in 1972, and back to Andreas-Gymnasium in 2016/17.[2][4][8] Located in Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg district (Friedrichshain neighborhood, Andreasviertel near Ostbahnhof, Karl-Marx-Allee; U-Bahn: Strausberger Platz, Weberwiese, Jannowitzbrücke), at Koppenstr. 76, 10243 Berlin; serves ~840 students (grades 5-12, four classes from grade 7) with ~80 teachers.[3][6][7] Facilities include two school buildings (historic complex from 1904-1906 by Ludwig Hoffmann, including protected Renaissance-style director's house and "Schulpalast" with modern science labs), sports hall, small sports field, and amphitheater (built 2014).[2][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves grades **5-12** (Gymnasium leading to Abitur); second foreign language: **Französisch or Latein**; first language English; third options French/Latin; performance courses in Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, French, History, IT, Math, Physics, Political Education.[3][5][6][10] Special programs: MINT-profile classes from grade 5 (since 2012/13, collaborative with HU Berlin, part of MINT-EC national network); modular studies/career prep (StArt in grade 9, Business@School); block schedule and learning materials fund since 2011; Cambridge/DELF prep.[1][2][5][6] Two Abitur pathways as noted; comprehensive berufsorientierung certified excellent.[6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Active extracurriculars via 87 Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs) including Courage AG (12), Klima AG (4), Schach (21), Kindernothilfe (9); categories cover excursions/trips (75), school projects (31), competitions (99), student council (23).[Scraped Content] Sports: chess tournaments, relays, high jump, basketball, frisbee, cross-country, skiing, surfing, judo, fencing courses.[9] Arts/social: choir, band, psychology, music, social sciences, economics; exchanges (e.g., Le Rainsy/Paris); Big Challenge, Jugend forscht, chemistry, mediators; religion and conflict counseling.[5][9] Recent events: Christmas chess 2025, Math Olympiad, media award.[Scraped Content]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Member of Berlin's MINT-network (one of five) and national MINT-EC excellence network; repeatedly awarded seal for **excellent berufliche Orientierung** (2014 certification).[1][2][6] Participation in Math Olympiad (65th, stage 2), Kindernothilfe media prize, chess, other competitions (99 posts).[1][Scraped Content] New school chronicle celebrates 180+ year history; modernized facilities (e.g., 2014 rooms).[2][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free** Öffentlich Gymnasium, open enrollment; current info on applications for 2026/27 school year posted (Dec 2025); MINT-classes from grade 5 via separate selection; general process via Berlin public school system (no fees mentioned).[Scraped Content][6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Anne-Frank-Gymnasium emphasizes comprehensive education inspired by Anne Frank's legacy, fostering joy in learning, critical thinking, and responsibility through demanding instruction supplemented by intellectual, athletic, cultural, and social activities.[3] Its school program defines pedagogical priorities, goals, and measures, serving as a self-commitment for all involved, with a language and social sciences profile promoting communication, participation, democracy, and community spirit.[2][3] Quotes from Anne Frank on the homepage highlight values of gradual world change and appreciating beauty for happiness.[Main Page]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1991, the school proudly bears Anne Frank's name as a commitment to its values.[3] Located in Berlin-Altglienicke (Treptow-Köpenick district) at Uranusstr. 15-17, 12524 Berlin, near the Berlin-Brandenburg border and S-Bahn station Grünbergallee, it serves ~892-907 students from Treptow-Köpenick (60%), Dahme-Spree district, and Neukölln (40%), in a mixed residential area of single-family homes and prefab buildings.[1][2][9] Facilities include two school buildings, two courtyards, two gymnasiums with outdoor sports area, computer rooms, print shop, and cafeteria.[2][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (grades 7-12/13), it offers Abitur preparation with strong language focus: S1 English/French, S4 English/Spanish, third languages (French, Latin, Spanish); bilingual Geography (year-round from grade 9) and Biology (modules in grades 9-10); performance courses in Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, History, Math, Physics, Spanish.[1][4] English Profile Class (grades 7-8) adds extra English hour, prepares for Cambridge exams (mandatory internal PET grade 8 free; optional external FCE grade 10 ~€140; optional CAE later).[4][5] Covers grades 7-12 with subject lists (e.g., German, languages, arts, social sciences, math/IT, sciences, sports); elective options like theater, psychology, social sciences, alpine skiing, surfing; career guidance, internships.[Main Page][6] Typically 4-6 classes per yeargang; updated house rules since 2025 include lesson/pause times.[Curriculum Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include Model United Nations (AFMUN/BERMUN, school-internal from grade 9, external at John F. Kennedy School), language trips (Puebla Mexico, Greece), project-based learning, method training, November 9 events, school trips.[Main Page][4] Sports via facilities; arts and cultural activities; school social work since 2021 supports personal development, conflict resolution, prevention, social learning for students, parents, teachers.[2] Working groups (AGs) and career/study counseling available.[6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Abitur averages 2.24-2.48 (2013-2017) with 96-100% pass rates.[1] Prepares for international certifications: Cambridge FCE/PET/CAE (English), DELE (Spanish), DELF (French).[6] No recent awards or rankings in provided content.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; enrollment for grade 7 via foster prognosis (60%), hardship clause (10%), lottery (30%) among applicants; 4-6 classes/year, 1-2 English Profile Classes requiring grade 2 prognosis in German/Math/English plus entrance test (listening/reading/writing); non-passing enters regular class.[5][8] Info days/open houses (e.g., Dec 8, 2025 for grade 6); contact sekretariat@afg.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 51653360.[Main Page][10]

description:
# Archenhold-Gymnasium Berlin: Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **school motto is "Fit for the university,"**[1] reflecting its commitment to preparing students for higher education. The school emphasizes three core pillars for prospective students: "What can I do at Archenhold-Gymnasium?", "How am I supported?", and "How and what do I learn in instruction?" This framework demonstrates a focus on student agency, support systems, and pedagogical quality.

The school's curriculum incorporates **cross-curricular themes including media literacy, language development, and career and university orientation.**[3] The institution positions itself as a comprehensive gymnasium with multiple specialized profiles designed to meet diverse student interests and strengths.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Archenhold-Gymnasium was renamed on October 14, 1992, in honor of Friedrich Simon Archenhold, a famous German astronomer,**[1] and the nearby Archenhold Observatory located in the same borough of Treptow-Köpenick.

The school is located at **Rudower Straße 7, 12439 Berlin, in the Treptow-Köpenick district, specifically in the Niederschöneweide neighborhood.**[2][3] The school celebrated its 20th anniversary in 2013, marking two decades since its establishment as Archenhold-Gymnasium in 1993.

**The campus comprises three distinct buildings:**[1]
- The main building with four floors (excluding cellar and attic)
- A three-story extension housing informatics and natural sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, and astronomy)
- A dual-gymnasium structure with a raised stand, weight room, and a two-story library

The school provides **a cafeteria and computer rooms**[3] to support student learning and daily operations.

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**The gymnasium teaches grades 7–12**[1] and offers the **Abitur (German university entrance qualification) as its primary graduation pathway.**[2]

### Language Programs
The school offers **three language tracks:**[2][3]
- S1: English/French
- S2: English/Latin
- Third foreign language: Italian

### Specialized Academic Profiles
**The school features five distinct academic profiles:**[2][3]
- Mathematics and natural sciences (mathematisch-naturwissenschaftliches Profil)
- Art (Kunstprofilierung)
- Media emphasis (medienbetontes Profil)
- Information technology (Profil Informationstechnik)
- Language emphasis (sprachbetontes Profil)

### Advanced Courses (Leistungskurse)
**Students can pursue advanced coursework in:**[2][3] Bildende Kunst/Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, French, History, Informatics/Information Processing, Latin, Mathematics, and Physics.

### Special Programs
- **DELF scolaire certification:**[4] Since 2006/07, students have successfully completed French language certification exams, with recent graduates achieving certifications in June 2025.
- **MINT-EC membership:**[3] The school is designated as a MINT-Excellence-Center, emphasizing science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.
- **Media classes (Medienklassen):**[3] Learning with notebook computers is available.
- **Partial full-day program (Teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb):**[3] Extended school day options are offered.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

### Extracurricular Activities and Clubs
**The school offers diverse working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) including:**[3]
- Jugend forscht (Youth Research)
- Robocup and Lego-League robotics competitions
- Mathematics club
- Creative writing
- "School Without Racism" initiative
- Event planning
- Sports: Badminton, volleyball, and football
- Performing arts: Choir and dance
- Language certification preparation

### Support Systems
**The school provides:**[3]
- Peer tutoring ("Schüler helfen Schüler" - Students Help Students)
- Social work services
- Career and university counseling (Laufbahnberatung)
- Student newspaper (Schülerzeitung)

### International Connections
**The school maintains partnerships with:**[1]
- Lycée Aux Lazaristes in Lyon, France
- Frederiksborg Gymnasium in Hillerød, Denmark

The school also facilitates **information sessions on studying abroad and international university options.**[3]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

### Academic Performance
**Abitur results demonstrate consistent excellence:**[2]
- Average Abitur grade: 2.09–2.32 (on a scale where lower is better)
- Success rate: 96–100% across recent years (2013–2024)
- 2024: Average grade 2.32 with 100% success rate

### Recognition and Awards
- **Physics Excellence:**[4] In November 2025, physics teacher Herr Ogrisek received the Heinrich-Gustav-Magnus Prize from the Berlin Physical Society for outstanding teaching. Students Lea Schmidt and Lennard Habermann received the Physics Student Prize for exceptional performance and interest.
- **MINT Talent Recognition:**[4] In September 2025, students Alina Pesch and Erik Löchter were honored for acquiring the MINT certificate and winning STEM competitions.
- **DELF Certification Success:**[4] Multiple students achieved DELF scolaire certifications in 2025, with performance results derived primarily from classroom instruction.
- **"School Without Racism" designation:**[3]
- **Jugend forscht School 2012:**[3]

### Community Engagement
**The school collaborates with external organizations:**[7] A STEM experience day was jointly hosted by the Federal Employment Agency and Archenhold Gymnasium.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

### Enrollment Information
**Current enrollment:**[2] Approximately 732–741 students with 65–72 teachers.

### Admission Process
The school offers **an open house (Tag der offenen Tür) where prospective students and families receive:**[4]
- Guided tours by current students
- School presentations by leadership at 30-minute intervals
- Information on the three core pillars of the school's approach

**Admission criteria for oversubscription are based on average grades from the Förderprognose (promotion forecast).**[3] As a public gymnasium in Berlin, admission follows standard state procedures.

### Key Contact Information
- **Phone:** +49 30 6360195[3]
- **Fax:** +49 30 6360185[3]
- **Email:** sekretariat@archenhold.de[3]
- **Website:** archenhold.de[3]
- **Principal:** Michael Uhlig[3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Arndt-Gymnasium Dahlem emphasizes a **humanist-aesthetic profile** rooted in individual development, community, and holistic education. Core maxims include prioritizing the individual's promotion in line with humanism, providing solid and broad academic training, and fostering the "Arndt Spirit" through community values.[7][1] It promotes ganzheitliche Persönlichkeitsentwicklung (holistic personality development), self-motivated and social learning, and "Lernen, wie man lernt" (learning how to learn) in reform-pedagogical tradition with strong musical, artistic, and scientific orientations.[1][7] The school positions itself as a place for classical education (Homo universale), integrating intellectual, artistic, technical, and athletic growth via project-oriented, interdisciplinary approaches.[1][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1909** as a Landschulheim primarily for Prussian nobility, the school carries a tradition of humanist and reform-pedagogical education through major historical periods, including both World Wars; it celebrated its 100th anniversary in 2008.[1][2][3] Located in Berlin's **Steglitz-Zehlendorf** district at **Königin-Luise-Straße 80-84, 14195 Berlin**, it spans multiple buildings with specialized classrooms for science, arts, languages, and MINT education, plus an extensive aula for presentations and performances.[2][7] Partnerships exist with Freie Universität Berlin and local music schools; it is part of the "Schule mit Courage" network.[7][2] The surrounding Dahlem area features cultural sites like museums and parks.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it offers education leading to **Abitur** (university entrance qualification), with altsprachliche Ausbildung (ancient languages: Latin, Ancient Greek) for all students alongside modern languages (English, French); English is the first foreign language.[7][1] **Profilklassen** include altsprachlicher Bildungsgang from grade 5 and Werkstatt Ästhetische Bildung for artistic deepening.[7] MINT subjects (Mathematics, Informatics, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Technik) are networked via project-oriented Wahlpflichtfach Natur und Technik and 40 Leistungskurs combinations.[7][1] Gesellschaftswissenschaften (History, Politics, Geography, Ethics, Philosophy) emphasize civic heritage; grades span typical Gymnasium levels (e.g., from grade 5 profiles).[7][1] A 2004 Schulprogramm outlines the framework.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in rich extracurriculars, including **Science Slams** (3rd event in 2026), archaeology trips, debate competitions ("Jugend debattiert"), Weihnachtskonzerte, and visits to CERN, BESSY II lab, and Etruscan sites.[7] Arts thrive via Musik (Kammerorchester, United Big Band, opera collaborations), Bildende Kunst, Theater (darstellendes Spiel), and Werkstatt Ästhetische Bildung.[7][2] Sports include Ski trips (grade 8), Hockey, Fußball, Basketball, Rugby.[7] Other activities: AGs in MINT, society sciences, Wettbewerbe; partnerships enable exchanges and professional performances.[7][2][1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Bundesweite and international successes in MINT AGs and Leistungskurse; strong performances in debates, Science Slams, and cultural events like orchestra opera participations.[7][1] Part of "Schule mit Courage" network (announced Sept 2025).[7] No specific rankings or awards detailed, but noted as traditionsreich (tradition-rich) and popular in Dahlem.[1][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Information on **Anmeldung** (enrollment) is highlighted on the main page, with a dedicated section; as a **public, tuition-free** Öffentlich school, access follows Berlin public Gymnasium processes (e.g., via district, often with waiting lists for profiles), prioritizing local residents.[7] Terminkalender provides dates; Profilklassen may have selection.[7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission emphasizes early empowerment of students for self-organized, responsible action to actively shape school life and future using acquired competencies.[1][2] It focuses on fostering individual interests and strengths from grade 7, with flexible offerings in grade 8 onward, including third foreign languages and interdisciplinary fields, alongside concepts for internships, career orientation, media education, and competency days.[2] Originally founded as an elite classical school, it now prioritizes social competencies, climate and environmental awareness.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1875 as an altsprachliche (classical languages) elite Gymnasium in Berlin's Friedrichvorstadt (Hallesche Straße 24–26), named after the nearby Askanischen Platz, with 15 teachers and 600 students initially.[1][3][4][7] It merged with Tempelhofer Gymnasium in 1929, moved to Tempelhof, and was renamed multiple times, including Askanische Oberschule post-1945, reverting to Askanisches Gymnasium in 2012.[3][7] Located in Berlin-Tempelhof-Schöneberg district (Tempelhof locality) at Kaiserin-Augusta-Str. 19/20, 12103 Berlin, serving ~739 students with 62 teachers.[5][6] Facilities include computer rooms, foreign language library, internet café.[5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a Gymnasium (grades 7–12/13), it offers Abitur as the main graduation pathway, with gymnasiale Oberstufe and no mention of MSA or BBR.[1][5] Core languages: S1 English/French or English/Chinese, S4 English/Spanish; third foreign languages from grade 8: French, Latin, Spanish, Chinese.[5][6] Leistungskurse (advanced courses) in Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, French, Geography, History, Informatics, Math, Music, Physics, Political Education, Spanish.[5][6] Additional programs: business internship in grade 9, study/career orientation, media education; support for new students without German proficiency via Lerngruppen.[2][5] Music includes theory, history, bands, big band, choir, Abitur exam option; art emphasizes practical creativity with theater AG.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include diverse Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs) in sports, informatics, languages, music, art, theater, technology, sciences, mediation.[5] Arts: chess tournament, art competitions ("Unterwegs in Berlin"), calligraphy (Chinese course success), workshops, exhibitions, calendar contest.[10] Sports: rowing AG since school founding (grades 7–12, Abitur credit in 11–12), basketball via ALBA Berlin partnership/Oberschulliga.[1][8] Exchanges: Erasmus+ (e.g., Helsinki job-shadowing), partners in Spain, France, China; virtual/real exchanges, interkultural projects; French film week.[1][2][5][9][10] Other: French Filmwoche visit.[10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certifications: "Exzellente Digitale Schule 2024-2028", Erasmus+ partner school, "Umweltschule in Europa/Internationale Nachhaltigkeitsschule 2024", "Schule:Global" for intercultural work.[2][9] Student successes: art competition wins, Chinese calligraphy prizes, MAX-Artists exhibition.[10] Ranks 14th in some listings; MINT partner.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; admits ~128 students (4 classes) to grade 7 for 2026/2027, with enrollment March 5–12, 2026; open house January 16, 2026; trial day for grade 6 January 14, 2026.[2][10] At overdemand, uses average grade from primary school recommendation; special needs require valid decree (<2 years old) or hardship application original at enrollment.[2] Flexible profile development from grade 7.[2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Barnim-Gymnasium is an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school) emphasizing **Inklusion and Integration** for every student, guided by the maxim "Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar!" (Human dignity is inviolable).[1][6] It promotes **excellence** in subjects, particularly MINT (math, IT, natural sciences, technology), alongside diverse support like gifted education, democracy education, career orientation, and international programs, while investing in future-oriented education.[1][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on August 5, 1993, as a branch of Pestalozzi-Gymnasium (now Manfred-von-Ardenne-Gymnasium) in Hohenschönhausen with ~300 students; new building in Falkenberg occupied in 1998, designed barrier-free.[1][2] Largest Gymnasium in Lichtenberg with ~1200 students (recently ~1181) and 109 teachers; ~35% non-German background, including large Vietnamese and Russian communities.[1][2][3] Located at Ahrensfelder Chaussee 41, 13057 Berlin (Falkenberg, Lichtenberg district), near Ahrensfelde S-Bahn and Tierheim Berlin.[2] Facilities include Mensa for warm meals, Barnim-Café, Lernstudio, lockers (rentable online), and sports halls; partners with Kietz für Kids Freizeitsport e.V. and Schlaufuchs Berlin for all-day programs.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
**Gymnasium** (grades 5-13) offering Abitur; admits grade 5 (two classes of 32, ancient languages with Latin profile, no special admission) and grade 7 (five classes of 32, no special admission); plans third grade 5 class from 2027/28.[1][6] MINT Excellence Center (member since 2001, national network); subjects include Politikwissenschaft, Psychologie, Astronomie, Informatik, Biologie (with Gläsernes Labor), Chemie, Mathematik, Physik; languages: Deutsch, Englisch, Französisch, Latein (from grade 5, Latinum by grade 10), Spanisch; other: Ethik, Religionsunterricht.[1][3][5] Special profiles: Bläserklasse (brass class, grades 7/8), Basketballklasse; gifted programs like BAUM (math-music); Ergänzungskurs for career/study prep with portfolios and modules on self-competence, job training, communication.[1][5][6] All-day includes homework help, prep courses for MSA/Abitur.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Extracurriculars**: Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs) like Badminton, Barnim Makers, Jugend forscht, Bienen AG, Stolpersteine (Holocaust remembrance); Sommercamp, Hausaufgabenhilfe, Förderkurse.[1][6] **Sports**: Basketball (teams train pre/post-school), Skikurs, Volleyball, Segelkurs.[3][6] **Arts/Music**: Bläserklasse (concerts, Philharmonie), Musikunterricht, Kunstfest, Darstellendes Spiel, Literarisches Schülerquartett, Theater.[3][5] **Exchanges/Trips**: Barnim-International (Vietnam, Dänemark), Kursfahrt Rom, Wittenberg, Amsterdam, POL&IS-Fahrt, SIMEP; Barnim Talk podcast on politics (e.g., Rechtsruck, Europawahl).[1][3][6] Social: Sozialpädagogische Arbeit, Willkommensklassen.[7][10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
MINT-EC certified (since 2001, national excellence network); BegaSchule title (2019/20) for gifted education by Berlin Senat; principal Sebastian Koven (since 2017) awarded for inclusion/integration, cultural openness, school partnerships, Willkommensklassen.[1][3][6][7] Successful basketball teams; student podcast productions; Stolperstein projects with local partners.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; no special procedures for grade 5 (ancient languages, Latin) or 7 intakes.[6] Open Day January 15, 2026 (17:00-20:00); trial courses for incoming grade 5 (Jan 23, 2026: Math, German, English, Latin, pre-registration from Dec 1).[6] Info-brochure and orientation plan available; prior info events (e.g., 2013).[4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's **Leitbild** (mission statement or guiding principles), class profiles, and school history define its core identity, emphasizing collaborative development involving teachers, students, and parents. The **Schulprogramm** (school program), established in October 2010 through intensive cooperation among teachers, students, and parents, is actively implemented in daily school life and continuously updated.[1] This reflects a philosophy of lived, evolving education tailored to student needs.

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded around 1908 as the Beethoven-Oberschule, it is now the **Beethoven-Gymnasium** in Berlin's Steglitz-Zehlendorf district, located at Barbarastr. 9, 12249 Berlin.[2] No specific facilities are detailed in the content, though references to a digital substitute plan, auditorium (Aula) for events, and music groups imply standard gymnasium infrastructure. Community engagement is evident in historical-political education projects and parental involvement in the school program.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (academic secondary school, grades 7–12/13 leading to **Abitur**), it admits 4–5 classes annually into grade 7, each with specialized **Profilklassen** (profiles): Deutsch-musisch-ästhetisch (German arts-aesthetic, offered every 2 years per Senate capacity), Musik (music, requiring instrumental proficiency; enhanced music lessons, choirs, orchestras, bands), Englisch (English with bilingual geography from grade 8, exchange in grade 10), and Mathematik (math with extra hours and integrated natural sciences).[1] Subject areas include musisch-ästhetische Fächer (art, music, performing arts), Deutsch, Sprachen (English, French, Spanish, Italian, Latin), Gesellschaftswissenschaften (geography, history, politics, social sciences, philosophy, ethics), MINT-Fächer (math, informatics, ITG, biology, chemistry, physics), Sport, and Religion. No mentions of MSA or BBR pathways.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize arts and music: choirs, orchestras, smallband/big band, BOSTAG (tech/lighting/sound group), and rehearsals for Christmas concerts. Sports include athletics hall meetings with team successes. Exchanges feature eTwinning projects in French (e.g., "Joyeux Noël nach Italien!") and grade 10 London trip with Westminster School. Educational trips include grade 7a to Exilmuseum Berlin for historical-political learning.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Both school teams earned **bronze medals** at the Leichtathletik-Hallenmeeting through teamwork. No additional awards, rankings, or certifications are mentioned.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (Öffentlich), admission to grade 7 profiles relies on criteria like instrumental proof for music or grades for others, with 4–5 classes admitted yearly per Senate capacities. No specific enrollment process details provided.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Berlin Cosmopolitan School's mission is **empathy, equity, excellence in education**, striving for innovation by challenging the traditional education system while fostering individual support, bilingual education, and preparation for university or apprenticeships.[Main Page][4] Core values include diversity, social justice, equal opportunities, and inclusion regardless of background, with a focus on nurturing talents in arts, sciences, sports, and social engagement.[Admissions Page][2] The philosophy emphasizes modern pedagogy, outdoor learning in nature (integral for over six years to promote independent thinking and sustainability), multilingualism, and drawing from Montessori, Reggio Emilia, and IB approaches.[Main Page][Curriculum Page][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **2004** as Berlin's first bilingual English-German kindergarten with 18 children, it expanded to primary school in 2007 and secondary in 2009, becoming an IB World School in 2013.[3][5] Now a non-profit with ~700 students from over 45 countries, it promotes a diverse, international community as active world citizens through strong academics, social skills, and intercultural development.[1][2] Facilities include **City Campus** (Rückerstr. 9, 10119 Berlin-Mitte, a 1912 listed brick building near Alexanderplatz, with SMARTboards, dance hall, outdoor basketball/football pitch, and additional rooms in Rückerstraße/Mulackstraße)[Main Page][6][5]; **Nature Campus** at Mügelsee (with new digital weather station for real-time data on temperature, pressure, humidity, rainfall, wind; data accessible via app/website for hands-on learning in math/science/geography).[Main Page] Other features: Cosmovatorium (music/dance school, first in Berlin for IB Dance), alumni site, and hiring for international team.[Main Page][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Offers **Kindergarten/Preschool** (from age 1), **Primary**, and **Secondary (Gymnasium)** through grade 12, as a recognized **IB World School** with Primary Years Programme (PYP) and Diploma Programme (DP) on Berlin's framework; primary language English, bilingual English-German.[1][3][4] Focus on sciences, music, dance, nature, digitalization, core subjects; Bilingual Abitur (German qualification for German/international universities, with 3 written/oral exams + presentation) and Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA) in grade 10 (Berlin state exam; English Science option for recent arrivals, plus English oral/presentation; prerequisite for upper school).[Main Page][4] Individualized support via Child Support Team for academic/social-emotional needs, including classroom collaboration, morning support (reading/math/science), referral processes, and external specialist links.[Curriculum Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Emphasizes outdoor/nature-based learning, workshops (e.g., Emotions for grade 4), language trips (grade 9), internships (grades 7/8/10), trial weeks (Schnupper Woche for grade 10 Abitur/DP), poetry readings on identity/multilingualism, democracy discussions (e.g., Venezuela at Berlin Freedom Week).[Main Page] Extracurriculars include music/dance (Cosmovatorium, IB Dance), sports (outdoor pitches), after-school programs, winter vacation programs, life skills workshops (stress management, conflict resolution, self-acceptance), and real-world projects like weather station data analysis.[1][Curriculum Page][Main Page] Multilingual events, alumni networking, and menu plans support daily life.[Main Page]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Authorized **IB World School** (PYP/DP since 2013); first Abitur class (2016) achieved Berlin's best average grade of **1.4**; first IB Diploma graduates in 2015; first MSA exams passed in 2013; state recognition for secondary school in 2014.[3][5] Students regularly graduate with top Abitur grades; publicly available weather data highlights practical science engagement.[Main Page][2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Enrollment process**: Online application form (complete for login/status; non-editable post-submission); requires photo (passport-style JPEG/PNG), sole custody proof if applicable; **50 EUR non-refundable fee** for primary/secondary (not kindergarten); documents for "APPLIED" status; open to diverse talents/backgrounds, with scholarships for need.[Admissions Page][Fees Page] School uniforms compulsory (via Dress for School or Cosmo Swap second-hand; includes sportswear). **Tuition**: Income-based (detailed schedule in PDF: https://www.cosmopolitanschool.de/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Gebuehrenuebersicht-Schulgebuehren-2024-25-.pdf); sibling discounts; scholarships for financially needy.[Fees Page] No exact EUR amounts listed on pages (e.g., no monthly/annual/material/meal/after-school specifics); refer to PDF for 2024/2025 details.[Fees Page]

description:
# Phorms Berlin Mitte - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Mission Statement**
Phorms Berlin Mitte operates as a **bilingual German-English immersion school** that combines personal development with academic excellence[1]. The school's founding concept emphasizes "Persönlichkeitsentwicklung mit Leistungsorientierung" (personality development with performance orientation).

**Core Educational Values**
The school's philosophy rests on five pillars[1]:

- **Bilingual immersion**: Continuous German-English education from age two through Abitur using the immersion method with native-speaking teachers
- **Future competencies**: Project-based learning, critical thinking, creative problem-solving, and responsible digital media use
- **Child-centered approach**: Pedagogically appropriate class sizes with optimal teacher-student ratios enabling individual support
- **Performance and personality**: Applied skills development alongside personal growth
- **Global perspective**: "Local Schools – Global Education" motto emphasizing international and open-minded worldview

The immersion method allows children to acquire language naturally through daily use across all subjects rather than through traditional language lessons[2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Growth**
Phorms Berlin Mitte was **founded in 2006** and celebrated its **20th birthday in summer 2025**, making it the oldest and largest of all Phorms schools[7]. The school serves approximately **950 students** across all levels (kindergarten through secondary school)[1], with the primary school alone housing **450 children**[3].

**Location and Accessibility**
The campus is located in **Berlin-Mitte district at Ackerstr. 76, 13355 Berlin**, in an attractive red-brick building in central Berlin[7]. The location offers optimal positioning near the Berlin Wall Memorial and Humboldthain park, with excellent public transportation access[1].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**
The modern campus includes[1]:

- Three modern sports halls
- High-quality science laboratories
- Specialized music rooms for practical music-making
- Spacious library
- Theater hall with fully equipped stage, lighting, and sound system
- Smartboards in all classrooms
- Lockers with USB connections in the secondary school
- Green spaces including Gartenplatz nearby

**Community Character**
The school emphasizes a **familial, international atmosphere** with students, teachers, and parents from diverse backgrounds[1]. The campus operates as a full-day community from early morning through 6 PM, integrating learning and living harmoniously[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Structure**
The school offers continuous education across three divisions[1]:

- **Kindergarten (Kita)**: From age two
- **Primary School (Grundschule)**: Grades 1-4
- **Secondary School (Gymnasium)**: Grades 5-13, leading to Abitur

**Bilingual Curriculum**
All instruction occurs at native-speaker level in both German and English[7]. The curriculum follows the Berlin state curriculum while incorporating extended content in English-language subjects[5].

**Primary School Subject Distribution**
The primary school integrates bilingual instruction across all subjects, with language learning embedded naturally throughout the day rather than confined to dedicated language lessons[2].

**Secondary School Subject Distribution**
In middle school (Grades 5-6), subjects are divided as follows[4]:

- **Taught in German**: German, Art, History, Sports
- **Taught in English**: English, Mathematics, Science, Geography
- **Additional offerings**: Extra lessons in Social Studies and Science

In upper school (Grades 11-13), students specialize in subjects of interest with advanced courses available in[4]:

- English, History, Biology, Mathematics, Art, Geography, German
- Music ensemble course for interested students

**Language Programs**
Starting in Grade 7, students choose an additional foreign language: **Spanish or French**[4]. Students develop subject-specific vocabulary in both German and English with ease through the immersion method[4].

**Graduation Pathways**
Students earn the **state-recognized Berlin Abitur** supplemented by a bilingual component in applicable subjects[5]. The school is state-recognized, ensuring qualifications are recognized both in Germany and internationally[5].

**Special Programs**
From Grade 5, the school offers a **bilingual science-focused secondary school** for students already enrolled at Phorms who demonstrate strong academic drive[4]. From Grade 7, a bilingual secondary school pathway is available for both Phorms students and external applicants[5].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities**
From Grade 3 in primary school, children can select different **elective subjects**[6]. The secondary school (from Grade 7) offers diverse elective choices in language and science areas[6].

**Afternoon and Enrichment Programs**
The school provides an **extensive and varied afternoon program** with working groups, free play, and situational offerings that extend classroom learning[6]. A **varied vacation program** offers opportunities to deepen personal interests beyond regular instruction[1].

**Individual Support and Advancement**
In the secondary school, the school provides[1]:

- Targeted support during "Studytime"
- Additional remedial instruction
- **Rotation model (Drehtürmodell)** for highly motivated learners
- Active participation in competitions

**Language Support**
For multilingual students needing additional support, the school provides **tailored remedial programs** with individual lessons in German (DaZ - Deutsch als Zweitsprache) and English (ESL - English as a Second Language) matched to each child's skill level[3].

**Special Needs Support**
The school supports children with special educational needs who study in regular classes alongside other students[6].

**Cognitive Benefits**
Research cited by the school indicates that bilingual learners develop[3]:

- Better language and foreign language skills
- Enhanced creativity
- Improved language awareness
- Greater cognitive flexibility

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Academic Performance**
The secondary school has **consistently achieved results well above the state average** for over 14 years with highly qualified teachers[5].

**Certifications and Recognition**
- **State-recognized private school** (staatlich anerkannt)
- Qualifications recognized both in Germany and internationally[5]
- Bilingual Berlin Abitur with extended content in English subjects[5]

**Curriculum Alignment**
The school follows the Berlin curriculum while offering extended content in English-language subjects to provide deeper and broader education[5].

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

**Admissions Process**
The enrollment process consists of three steps[1]:

1. **Online registration**: Complete the application form online without obligation
2. **Personal meeting**: School contacts families to discuss next steps, answer questions, and provide insight into daily operations
3. **Placement offer**: School notifies families via email of acceptance and sends contract documents for digital signature

Admissions can occur mid-year if spaces become available[1].

**Fee Structure**

The school implements **income-dependent parental contributions** to enable access for families across different economic backgrounds. Fees are calculated based on total family annual income.

**Kindergarten/Reception (Kita) Fees**

Monthly parental contributions (Förderbeitrag) are income-based:

- Up to €20,000 annual income: €135/month
- €50,000+: €212/month
- €75,000+: €246/month
- €100,000+: €275/month
- €130,000+: €349/month

These amounts are rounded and do not include meal costs and other fees[1].

**Additional Costs**
- **Meal costs**: Separate meal allowance (Verpflegungspauschale) applies
- **Berlin Kita voucher**: Covers part of basic costs; families must apply at their district office (Bezirksamt) at least two months before enrollment
- **Förderverein membership**: Monthly contribution required to support the bilingual concept and enhanced teacher-student ratios

**Sibling Discounts**
Families with multiple children receive reductions[1]:

- 1st child: Full monthly contribution
- 2nd child: 25% discount
- 3rd child: 50% discount
- 4th and subsequent children: 75% discount

**Tax Deductibility**
Parental contributions and Förderverein fees may be deductible as childcare expenses (Betreuungsaufwendungen) in tax returns under §10 Abs. 1 Nr. 5 EStG, up to two-thirds of expenses (maximum €4,800 per child annually), excluding instruction and extracurricular activities[1].

**Primary and Secondary School Fees**

The website content provided does not include specific tuition amounts for primary school (Grundschule) or secondary school (Gymnasium). A fee schedule document (Beitragsordnung) is referenced but the specific amounts are not displayed in the accessible content.

**Contact Information**
- **Phone**: +49 30 318 737 010 (Monday-Friday, 10:00-16:00)
- **Email**: admissions.berlin@phorms.de
- **Admissions Coordinator**: Hannah Vollmer (Kita)

# School Profile: Phorms Berlin Süd

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Phorms Berlin Süd emphasizes **bilingual education as an integral part of everyday school life**, with the goal of developing students who speak German and English fluently at near-native level[4]. The school's educational philosophy centers on fostering **independent learning, problem-solving, and reflective thinking** through creative learning methods and interdisciplinary team and project work[4].

The school promotes **intercultural skills and global awareness**, guiding students to understand cultural and linguistic diversity while recognizing global connections[4]. A key principle is supporting individual strengths through early identification of abilities and targeted fostering through remedial lessons and enrichment initiatives[6]. The school describes itself as providing "Local Schools – Global Education," combining rigorous academics with personal development[6].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

Phorms Berlin Süd was **founded in 2008** and opened its primary school that year[5]. The secondary school opened in 2010[3]. The campus celebrated its 10th anniversary in 2018[3].

**Location and Facilities**

The school is located in **Steglitz-Zehlendorf**, Berlin's green southwestern district[5][6]. The campus occupies a historic building with significant heritage: it was formerly the Telefunken works, where tens of thousands of patents were developed in the 1930s, and later served as headquarters for the American Berlin Brigade after 1945[3]. The building was extensively renovated in 2009 before the school moved in[3].

The campus includes **sports facilities with two sports fields with running tracks, as well as throwing and long jump facilities**[5]. The location combines urban cultural access with suburban green spaces and parks[5][6].

**Community Size**

The campus serves approximately **700 children and students** across nursery, primary, and secondary levels, with over 120 educators[5].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Structure**

The school serves students from **age one through secondary school graduation**, with programs in nursery, primary school, and secondary school[6].

**Bilingual Immersion Approach**

Teaching takes place using the **immersion method** in both German and English, with native-speaking teachers in each language[5][6]. The bilingual concept is maintained from nursery through Abitur graduation[5].

**Academic Pathways**

Students are guided to the **Abitur in 12 years**[4]. The school offers an **advanced English course in upper secondary** where students can earn the **Cambridge Advanced English Certificate**, an internationally recognized qualification[4].

**STEM Focus**

The school has a **strong STEM profile** and is recognized as a "House of Little Researchers"[6]. The curriculum emphasizes applied skills, responsible media use, and development of critical thinking and problem-solving abilities[6].

**Curriculum Foundation**

The curriculum is based on the outline curriculum of the federal state of Berlin[2].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Learning Methods**

The school implements **"rhythmized lessons"** in primary school, where longer teaching units alternate with time for projects and shorter learning units, with flexible lesson endings rather than fixed bell schedules[5].

**Enrichment and Support**

The school identifies and fosters individual talents through remedial lessons and the **"Digitale Drehtür" (Digital Revolving Door) model**[6]. An **engaging afternoon program and various competitions** enhance the learning experience[6].

**Extracurricular Activities**

While specific extracurricular programs are not detailed in the available content, the school emphasizes sports facilities and cultural offerings available through Berlin's location[5][6].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Certifications and Recognition**

- **Official recognition as "Ersatzschulen"** (state-recognized private schools) for both primary and secondary school[3]
- **"Schule mit Courage, Schule ohne Rassismus"** (School with Courage, School Without Racism) award in 2016[3]
- **Cambridge Advanced English Certificate** program established, with first exams in 2017[3]
- **AP (Advanced Placement) exams** offered, with first exams in 2016[3]
- **Abitur graduation** program established, with first graduates in 2016[3]
- **Vocatium partner school** designation in 2018[3]

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

description:
# Carl-Friedrich-von-Siemens-Gymnasium School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The school's motto is **"Lernen im Grünen"** (Learning in the Green), reflecting its commitment to education within a natural, green environment.[1][6] The Carl-Friedrich-von-Siemens-Gymnasium emphasizes a holistic educational approach that integrates academic excellence with practical learning experiences. The school cooperates with diverse organizations, companies, and sports associations to provide students with varied opportunities and improve school climate and satisfaction among all stakeholders.[6]

The institution demonstrates a commitment to innovative educational partnerships, as evidenced by its participation in the "Museum at School" educational ecosystem project with the Museum für Naturkunde Berlin, which enables research-based learning and authentic educational experiences.[3]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Location**

The Carl-Friedrich-von-Siemens-Gymnasium was **founded in 1951** and is located in Berlin-Siemensstadt, a district in Spandau at the border with Charlottenburg.[1] The school is situated within the Volkspark Jungfernheide, a green space designed by Erwin Albert Barth between 1920 and 1926, providing students with a distinctive learning environment surrounded by nature.[1] The school is one of six gymnasiums in Spandau.[1]

**School Size and Staffing**

The school serves approximately **750 students** organized in 4 to 5 classes per grade level.[6] The pedagogical staff consists of **90 people**, including **80 teachers** described as a young and active faculty, plus a dedicated school social work team comprising full-time social workers, dual-study students, volunteers from the Voluntary Ecological Year (FSJ), and a media educator.[6]

**Facilities**

The school features modern infrastructure including internet access in all rooms and contemporary teaching equipment such as whiteboards, smartboards, and data projectors. Science department rooms are particularly well-equipped.[2] The school shares a modern dining hall and canteen with Tews Primary School, offering a variety of hot meal options for lunch.[2]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Structure and Entry Points**

The school offers a traditional gymnasium pathway. Beginning in the 2026/27 school year, the school is introducing a **new foundational class 5 (NaWi)** with a focus on natural sciences.[6]

**Interdisciplinary Profiles**

Students can choose from four interdisciplinary profiles:[6]

- **Profil Kultur mit Bläserklasse** (Culture Profile with Wind Band Class)
- **Profil Englisch** (English Profile)
- **Profil MINT** (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics Profile)
- **Profil Sport** (Sports Profile)

**Languages**

The curriculum offers comprehensive language instruction:[2]

- **First foreign language:** English
- **Second foreign languages:** Spanish and French
- **Third foreign languages:** French, Spanish, Latin, and Chinese

Students in gifted and talented classes (available since 1993, starting in year 5) study English as the first language and French as the second foreign language, with opportunities to pursue additional projects.[2]

**Subject Selection and Specialization**

In **year 8**, students select their first compulsory option, choosing between science and languages (Latin, Spanish, and Chinese), which can be continued to A-level.[2]

In **year 10**, students select a second subject from options including Russian, information technology, mathematics, astronomy, economics, art, music, and geography.[2]

**Sixth Form (Upper Secondary)**

The school offers a wide range of subjects at both elementary and advanced levels for sixth-form students.[2]

**Special Academic Initiatives**

The school has established a multi-year partnership with the Free University and the University of Technology Berlin, enabling students to undertake individual research projects in science.[2]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities and Sports**

The school maintains an active extracurricular program, including:

- **Sports:** Girls' futsal teams competing in Berlin-wide competitions; ski trips for grade 8 students
- **Theater:** Student-written and performed theatrical productions (e.g., "MISSION (IM)POSSIBLE – In the end lies the beginning")
- **Academic competitions:** "Jugend präsentiert" (Youth Presents) competition, with school winners advancing to regional finals

**Cultural and Educational Partnerships**

The school participates in the "Museum at School" educational ecosystem with the Museum für Naturkunde Berlin, enabling students to engage in research-based learning on topics including dinosaurs, inclusion, behind-the-scenes museum operations, and the solar system.[3] The school has also hosted international educational exchanges, including visits by Native American tribal representatives facilitated by the U.S. Embassy.[5]

**Digital Learning**

The school has integrated digital learning into its educational framework, utilizing itslearning as its primary learning management system to blend in-person and digital instruction.[4]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school has received **multiple certifications and awards** over the years, reflecting its vibrant daily school life and commitment to continuous development.[6] Specific achievements include:

- Recognition as a school with gifted and talented programs since 1993[2]
- Successful integration of the Berlin curriculum into digital learning platforms[4]
- Active participation in educational innovation projects with major Berlin cultural institutions[3]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Process**

The school accepts applications for the 2026/27 school year. Prospective families can attend school tours (scheduled for January 20 and January 27, 2026) and information presentations at the school's main office.[6] The school website provides comprehensive information about the application process and available programs.

**Public School Status**

As a **public school (Öffentlich)**, the Carl-Friedrich-von-Siemens-Gymnasium is **tuition-free**. There are no enrollment fees or tuition charges for students.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission emphasizes transforming knowledge into comprehensive education through an equitable integration of all subjects, fostering a broad educational framework.[4][1] It aims to equip graduates with the ability to participate independently and responsibly in societal life and shape it.[4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1909-1910 as a higher girls' school (Oberlyzeum Görschstrasse), it evolved through names like Richard-Wagner Lyzeum (1920-1951) and Erweiterte Oberschule Carl-von-Ossietzky (1951-1991) before becoming the current Carl-von-Ossietzky-Gymnasium in 1991; the building was designed by Carl Fenten and opened in 1910.[2][3][5] Located in Berlin-Pankow district at Görschstraße 42/44, 13187 Berlin, it serves over 1,000 students (1,123 students and 104 teachers recently reported) with about 100 staff.[1][4] Facilities include lockers available for rent from 7th to 12th grade via Mietra.[Scraped Website Content]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it offers education leading to the Abitur (upper secondary qualification); grades span at least 5th to 12th, with two new 5th-grade classes planned for 2026/27 featuring a French bilingual profile in Geography from 7th grade.[1][2][6] Languages include S1 English/French, S5 French/English, S6 English/Italian, with 3rd foreign languages Latin or Chinese; advanced courses (Leistungskurse) in Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, French, History, Italian, Math, Philosophy, Physics, Political Science.[1][2] Basic courses expand to Performing Arts, Career Studies, IT/Digital Worlds; it participates in Ethics/Philosophy pilot.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school supports environmental engagement, earning Umweltschule in Europa certification four times (latest 2016).[2] Lockers aid daily student life from 7th-12th grade.[Scraped Website Content] Open doors and info events allow families to explore, including with children.[6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent Abitur averages: 1.93 (2024), 1.98 (2023), showing strong performance (German grading: 1.0 best).[1] Awarded Umweltschule in Europa four times, most recently 2016.[2] Student numbers grew steadily to 1,083 by 2022/23 with 97 teachers.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, admission to new 5th classes (2026/27, two classes of 30) targets 4th graders via grade sum in German, Foreign Language, Math, General Studies: priority to sums 4-6, then 7-9, then higher; open to motivated, gifted students including French bilingual track.[6] Info events and open house (e.g., Jan 24, 2026) for families.[6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
No explicit mission statement is provided on the website. The school's educational approach emphasizes hands-on, interdisciplinary learning through its **Biologie-Station**, which houses over 1,000 animals from around 50 species and promotes goals like understanding scientific concepts, animal observation, nature conservation, responsibility for living beings, teamwork, and hygiene in animal care.[1] It fosters a positive attitude toward nature, reduces animal phobias, and enables cross-grade social interactions without grading pressure, integrating subjects like geography for habitat studies.[1] The school supports environmental initiatives, such as participation in "4000 Tonnen Kartoffeln für Berlin" against food waste, and is certified as an **Umweltschule 2023**.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
**Dathe-Gymnasium** was built in 1908-1909 at a cost of 750,000 Reichsmark, initially as the 98th (boys') and 87th (girls') Volksschulen, with stone figures by sculptor Joseph Rauch still visible.[1] Post-1945, it became the 6th and 14th Oberschulen Friedrichshain; in 1991, half was converted to a Gymnasium (grades 7-9), with the other half used by Hoffmann-Grundschule until 1994. The Biology Station began in 1957 under Herr Vogler, expanding to modern standards in 1996 (now threatened by budget cuts) and featuring animals like a Königspython.[1] Named after zoologist Heinrich Dathe, despite controversy over his NSDAP membership.[5]

Located in **Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg** district at **Helsingforser Str. 11-13, 10243 Berlin**, it serves a community-oriented environment with student-led projects like wooden seating for the courtyard and renovated facilities (e.g., boys' toilet).[1] Facilities include: cafeteria lounges, outdoor courtyard and volleyball area, art rooms, 4th-floor canteen (Mensa), multi-purpose hall (Mehrzweckhalle), music rooms, biology/physics/chemistry/informatics labs (modernized 2007), five computer rooms (15-17 PCs each, upgraded 2021-23), and the Biology Station.[1][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (academic secondary school, grades 5-12/13 leading to **Abitur**), it offers a broad **Wahlpflichtunterricht (WPU)** elective program for grades 9-10 to deepen interests, with detailed PDFs available.[1] French is taught from grades 6/7.[2] Career and study orientation for grades 10-11, informatics upper-level projects, and qualification for Alba-Oberschulliga (likely math/science competition).[1] Modernized STEM rooms support sciences; no specific mention of MSA or BBR pathways, typical for Gymnasien focusing on Abitur.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include artist workshops, **Murder Mystery Dinner**, Christmas concert, project days (e.g., "Staatsoper" for grades 5-6), "Dathe liest" reading event, Make@thon "Wasser & Gesellschaft", and Oberschulmesse.[1][3] Sports: Volleyball field, Alba-Oberschulliga qualification.[1] Arts: Art rooms, music rooms, kunsträume.[3] Biology Station offers animal care, exhibitions, and trial pet-taking home.[1] Community involvement: Homepage-AG for website maintenance (email: homepage@dathe-gymnasium.eu), alumni reunions, psychological support.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as **Umweltschule 2023** for environmental efforts.[3] Participation in food waste action "4000 Tonnen Kartoffeln für Berlin" and successes in Biology Station breeding.[1] Math Olympiad (2nd stage), informatics projects, sports qualifications; no formal rankings or awards listed.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
**Tuition-free public school**. Admissions for 2026/27 school year: Schnelllernertest (Jan 17, 2026, past as of current date); grade 5 enrollment Feb 10-13, 2026 (post-test, in-person at secretariat); grade 7 trial day via info@dathe-gymnasium.eu, enrollment Mar 5-12, 2026 (in-person).[1] Open house Jan 9, 2026 (16-19 Uhr); info events for grades 5/7 (in-person and digital replays); digital handbook, FAQ, and presentation available.[1][3]

description:
# Diesterweg-Gymnasium Berlin: Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Diesterweg-Gymnasium** is committed to **interculturality as a core educational principle**[1]. The school's mission centers on equality, integration, and cross-cultural learning for all students in its diverse community[1]. 

Key philosophical pillars include:

- **Intercultural competence**: Learning about cultures is embedded as a fixed component of school work, with cross-curricular project-based instruction occurring for all grade levels annually, always connected to interculturality[1]
- **Inclusive excellence**: The school actively and successfully pursues equal treatment and integration of all student groups represented in the school community[1]
- **Multilingual development**: A unique language sequence (French–Latin–English) supports multilingual competence for linguistically gifted students[2]
- **Artistic and creative expression**: The school emphasizes artistic profiling alongside academic rigor[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Evolution**

The Diesterweg-Gymnasium was founded in **1905**[5] and is named after the German pedagogue Friedrich Adolph Wilhelm Diesterweg[1]. The school relocated to its current location at the beginning of the 2011/12 school year[1].

**Location and Accessibility**

- **Address**: Böttgerstraße 2, 13357 Berlin[2][3]
- **District**: Mitte, Ortsteil (neighborhood) Gesundbrunnen[1][3]
- **Setting**: Located on a property between Böttgerstraße and Pankstraße, opposite the Wedding District Court (Amtsgerichts Wedding) at Brunnenplatz[3]
- **Public Transportation**: Excellent accessibility via U-Bahnhof Pankstraße (U8 line) to the northeast and Bahnhof Gesundbrunnen (regional rail/S-Bahn/U8) to the east[3]

**Facilities**

- **Renovated building**: The school moved into a fully renovated facility in 2011[1]
- **Accessibility**: The school building is equipped with disability access features including an elevator[2]
- **Capacity**: The school serves approximately **675 students** with **63 teachers** (as of 2023/24)[3]

**Student Demographics**

The school serves a highly diverse student population: **698 students come from 30 nations, with all world religions represented**[1]. This diversity directly informs the school's educational approach and intercultural mission.

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Structure and Organization**

The Diesterweg-Gymnasium encompasses grades 5–12[1]:
- **Lower level (Unterstufe)**: Grades 5–6 (single-track)
- **Middle level (Mittelstufe)**: Grades 7–10 (four-track)
- **Upper level (Oberstufe)**: Grade 11–12 (course-based system)[1]

**Language Programs**

The school offers a distinctive **unique language sequence: French–Latin–English**[2], beginning in grade 5. This sequence is designed for linguistically gifted students and provides foundational knowledge and skills through the intermediate school leaving certificate (mittlerer Schulabschluss)[2].

Standard language offerings include:
- **French** (primary language, starting grade 5)
- **Latin** (starting grade 5)
- **English** (starting grade 7)[2]

**Special Programs**

- **Bilingual French program**: A francophone bilingual class (Frankophone Bilinguale Klasse) is available[4]
- **Academically advanced track**: A "Grundständige Klasse" (academically advanced class) begins in grade 5[4]
- **International certification**: **AbiBac (Abitur-Baccalauréat)** in French, providing dual German-French certification[2]

**Subject Areas**

The curriculum is organized into five main subject areas[4]:

| Subject Area | Subjects |
|---|---|
| **Languages** | Deutsch, Englisch, Französisch, Latein |
| **Social Sciences** | Geschichte, Politikwissenschaft, Geografie, Ethik |
| **Natural Sciences** | Mathematik, Chemie, Biologie, Physik, Informatik |
| **Arts and Music** | Musik, Kunst, Theater |
| **Physical Education** | Sport |

**Upper Level (Oberstufe) Courses**

Advanced placement courses (Leistungskurse) include: Bildende Kunst/Kunst, Biologie, Chemie, Deutsch, Englisch, Erdkunde, Französisch, Geschichte, Mathematik, Physik, and Politische Weltkunde[3]

**All-Day School Model**

Since 2011/12, the Diesterweg-Gymnasium operates as a **bound all-day school (Gebundene Ganztagsschule)**, mandatory through grade 10[1][2]. The school developed its own all-day concept featuring:
- School begins at 8:00 AM with two single periods[2]
- 30-minute morning break after the first two periods[2]
- Double-period subject instruction in the late morning[2]
- **60-minute lunch break** after the 6th period with extensive extracurricular offerings[2]
- Comprehensive extracurricular program during the midday band[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities and Support**

The school offers extensive support and enrichment programs:

- **Working groups (AGs)**: Comprehensive extracurricular offerings during the midday band[1][2]
- **Student library**: Dedicated school library for student use[4]
- **Sports programs**: "Ganztag Bewegt" (all-day movement/sports program)[4]
- **Social work services**: School social work (Schulsozialarbeit) available to students[4]
- **Counseling**: Trust teachers (Vertrauenslehrkräfte) for student support[4]

**Academic Support and Enrichment**

Support offerings include[2]:
- Individual learning plans (Förderpläne)
- Learning development conversations (Lernentwicklungsgespräche)
- Method days and method training (Methodentage, Methodentraining)
- Tutoring and support courses in groups (Nachhilfe/Förderkurs Gruppe)
- Exam preparation (Prüfungsvorbereitung)
- Language support and language training (Sprachförderung, Sprachtraining)

**International and Cultural Experiences**

- **Erasmus+ program**: Student exchanges and study experiences abroad, including month-long placements near Lyon, France[4]
- **Film festivals**: Participation in Cinéfête (French cinema festival) at the Institut français Berlin[4]
- **Language certifications**: DELF exam preparation and certification in French[4]
- **Career and study guidance**: Berufs- und Studienorientierung (career and university orientation)[4]

**Student Governance**

- **Class councils** (Klassenrat)[4]
- **Student representation** (Schülervertretung)[4]
- **Parent representation** (Elternvertretung)[4]
- **School association** (Schulverein)[4]
- **Diversity initiatives** (Diversität)[4]

**Cultural and Artistic Activities**

Recent activities documented on the school blog include[4]:
- Theater productions by grade 7 students
- Poetry slam participation ("Slam der Jugend")
- Museum excursions (e.g., to the Altes Museum for history courses)
- Science week activities including university visits (Kinderuni)

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is "Vom Wannsee aus die Welt entdecken" (Discover the world from Wannsee), emphasizing global exploration from its location in Berlin's green southwest.[1][3][4] Its **Leitbild** (educational philosophy) promotes individualized talent development, performance and joy in learning, team-oriented instruction, integration of students' life contexts and societal relevance, a learning organization with shared responsibility, external partnerships, social competencies, and community events for a positive school climate.[1] The school positions itself as competent guides in a complex world, with ongoing staff development.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established in the early 20th century as part of Berlin's educational expansion, the school at Dreilindenstr. 49, 14109 Berlin (Nikolassee, Steglitz-Zehlendorf district), serves as both an active Gymnasium and a protected heritage site, preserving its architectural legacy.[1] It enrolls about **750 students** supported by around **70 teachers** and **5 trainee teachers**.[1] Facilities include a historical entrance portal with modern interior, generous aula for concerts/theater/Abitur exams, modern subject labs, inner courtyard for relaxation/exercise, two sports halls, and a large sports field for breaks and events like sports/herbstfeste.[1] The green, nature-rich location near Wannsee offers good public transport access.[1][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it covers Sekundarstufe I and II (grades 7 to 13, leading to **Abitur**).[1][2][3] Core offerings include regular curriculum plus **bilingual (German-English) profile** from grade 7 to Abitur with a special certificate, strong performance in Vera 8, MSA, and Abitur exams; **mathematical-natural sciences profile**; "Natural Science" elective in English; **Certilingua accreditation** (since 2009, one of eight in Berlin) for advanced language skills; and **Staatliche Europaschulen Berlins (SESB)** program since 2021/22 with 50/50 German-English instruction.[2] No mentions of MSA or BBR pathways beyond standard Gymnasium structure.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Community-building includes **Sportfeste, Musik- und Theateraufführungen, Herbstfeste** in the aula and sports field.[1] Extracurriculars feature yearly reading competitions, circus activities, youth development/prevention programs.[1][6] Digital tools support learning (e.g., DSB, Nextcloud, Lernraum Berlin, Schulportal, YouTube).[1][4] A **Care-Team** provides support.[1] Virtual open days, magazine, and YouTube channel offer further engagement.[1][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Certilingua accreditation** since 2009 (held by only eight Berlin schools); overaverage bilingual results in Vera 8, MSA, Abitur; SESB participation since 2021/22.[2] Participation in reading competitions and projects noted, but no specific awards/rankings detailed.[1][6] Heritage site status highlights cultural significance.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich), tuition-free** school in Steglitz-Zehlendorf, enrollment follows standard Berlin public Gymnasium processes (e.g., via "Aufnahme" page mentioned, likely local priority/waitlisting).[1][4] Specific requirements not detailed in content; digital tools and organization page provide forms/contacts.[1][4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Droste-Hülshoff-Gymnasium is a general education school with focuses on music and French, aiming to foster students' comprehensive development so they can creatively and responsibly contribute to shaping society.[2] Core values emphasize collaboration ("Miteinander"), democratic decision-making in school life, respect for differences, digital literacy ("Mündigkeit"), and courage in education, inspired by Martin Luther King's quote on taking the first step.[1][2] The philosophy promotes projects across year groups for designing, making music, researching, training, and discussing, alongside democracy education and self-determined action in societal and digital spaces.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as a private higher girls' school in 1885 by Flora Köppler, it grew rapidly; in 1902, the community built a larger facility at the current site, opened on October 12, 1903, for 225 students, and took over operations in 1904.[1] It became state-recognized in 1909, was named Lyzeum in 1911, renamed after Annette von Droste-Hülshoff in 1938, co-educational from 1948/49, and classes suspended until May 1945 due to WWII.[1] Located in Berlin's Zehlendorf (Steglitz-Zehlendorf district) at Schönower Str. 8, 14165 Berlin, the main building was designed in 1902 by architects Jakob Sedelmeier and Johann Hoeniger.[1] Community includes music-filled daily life with choirs, orchestras, and events; contact: Tel (030) 84 50 84 66, Fax (030) 84 50 84 68, schulbuero@droste-berlin.de.[2] No specific modern facilities detailed beyond music rehearsal spaces implied.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it serves grades 7–12/13, preparing students for **Abitur** in 12 years total (G8/G9 model).[1][3] Special profiles from grade 7: **Music** (extra lesson in 7/8, reduced sport for music in 9/10; mandatory choir/orchestra; entry via annual test; Leistungskurs in upper school) and **French** (profilklasse option).[1][2] Music builds theoretical/practical skills, ensemble work, and creativity; French offers bilingual elements.[1][2] All students access music programs; 26 AGs (working groups) support projects.[2] Curriculum includes standard subjects with music/French emphasis; digital literacy focus.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars feature **26 AGs** for collaborative projects across grades (music, research, training, discussion).[2] **Arts**: Extensive music with choirs, school orchestras, band, soloists, concerts (Christmas/summer), opera/musicals, choir/orchestra trips; open to all.[2] French profile enhances language immersion.[2] Sports: Reduced in music profile for music time.[2] Community events: Open Day (e.g., Jan 15, 2026), choir trips with support services.[2] Democratic school governance and digital mündigkeit projects promote responsibility.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, or certifications mentioned in content.[1][2] Notable alumni include journalist Andreas Austilat, politician Cerstin Richter-Kotowski, hockey player Floris Völkner; former teacher Helmut Wolff (painter/sculptor).[1] Music events like Leistungskurs finales and concerts are annual highlights.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; enrollment for grade 7 via website info, including annual music profile test during application period.[2] Open Day (e.g., Jan 15, 2026, from 4 PM) for visits; details for 2026/27 school year available online.[2] No fees or further requirements specified beyond profiles.[1][2]
