description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's philosophy emphasizes holistic student development: "Eine gute Schule sollte vor allem jedem Schüler helfen, sich selbst zu erkennen, und alle seine Seiten ausbilden und vervollkommnen. So wird sich der junge Mensch seiner Stärken bewusst und er kann sein Leben danach einrichten." (A good school should above all help each student recognize themselves and develop and perfect all their aspects, becoming aware of their strengths to shape their life accordingly.)[3][5] This approach fosters self-awareness, strength-building, and active engagement with historical events and current societal issues through modern teaching methods and a friendly atmosphere.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in the scenic Treptow-Köpenick district (Friedrichshagen neighborhood) near Müggelsee, at Bruno-Wille-Str. 37-39, 12587 Berlin, the school serves around 650-791 students with 50-66 teachers.[1][2][5] It has a long tradition as a key part of Friedrichshagen; originally linked to the König-Friedrich-Realgymnasium, it was re-established in 1946 as Gerhart-Hauptmann-Schule, merged in 1951 as Gerhart-Hauptmann-Oberschule, and evolved into the current Gymnasium.[4] Facilities include a new Mensa for warm meals, Aula for performances, and resources like biotops and student labs for MINT subjects.[1][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves grades 7-12/13 (gymnasiale Oberstufe). Classes 7-8 are taught in full class groups: Deutsch, Mathematik, first/second foreign languages, Musik, Bildende Kunst, Geschichte, Erdkunde, Sport, Biologie, Chemie, Physik, Medien, optional Religion.[5] Classes 9-10 add 4 hours of Wahlpflichtfach (third foreign language like Latein for Latinum, or other subjects), ending with mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA) exams for Oberstufe entry.[5] Oberstufe uses a course system with two Leistungsfächer from options like modern languages, Deutsch, Mathematik, Musik, Kunst, Politikwissenschaft, Geschichte, Erdkunde, Naturwissenschaften, Informatik.[1][5] Languages: S1 Englisch; S2/S4 Französisch/Spanisch; third: Latein/Französisch/Spanisch.[1][2][5] New 7th graders get method training, 8th communication training, and extra support in Deutsch, Mathematik, languages.[5] Leads to Abitur; historical Abitur averages 2.25-2.38 with 98-100% pass rates (2013-2017).[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include choirs, Big Band, Aula performances, Freizeitsport, Arbeitsgemeinschaften (e.g., Englisch AG, Technik AG, Theater AG, Schulsanitäter), Wandertage, Sportfeste, Klassenreisen, Betriebspraktikum (grade 9), projects/excursions, Wettbewerbe (e.g., Känguru Mathematik, Physik MNU, Mathe-Olympiade, Geographie Wissen).[1][5][7] Sports: Handball/Volleyball teams succeed in Jugend trainiert für Olympia; historical sports excellence.[3][4][5] Exchanges: Partnerships with France/Spain (mutual 2-week homestays, Voltaire-Programm), individual long-term stays.[5] Other: Duke of Edinburgh Award (Bronze), WPU projects (e.g., Kriegsgräberfürsorge), experimental archaeology ("Lebendige Antike"), MINT labs.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTcomes
28 students from grade 10 earned DELF-B1 certificates (best quota yet).[3] Strong sports starts in JtfO tournaments (U16 Handball, Volleyball).[3] Duke of Edinburgh Bronze awards for personal development.[3] Historical: Ehrenbanner as best Berliner Erweiterte Oberschule in Jugendsport; top Abitur results (e.g., 2.25 average, 100% pass).[2][4] MINT competitions: Bundesweiter Physik MNU, Känguru, Mathe-Olympiade.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free Gymnasium. Enrollment for new 7th classes via Aufnahmeantrag process; information for 2026/2027 school year shared with surrounding Grundschulen (as of Dec 2025 news).[3] No fees or tuition; standard Berlin public school admission based on qualifications for Gymnasium track.[6]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Goethe-Gymnasium is a humanistic gymnasium emphasizing comprehensive general and human education in the spirit of its namesake, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, fostering personal responsibility, critical engagement with cultural heritage, and independent work in a respectful social environment.[1][3] It aims to prepare students to shape the world responsibly, discover beauty in subjects, and find joy in learning, as reflected in quotes like "Behandle die Menschen so, als wären sie, wie sie sein sollten..." from Goethe and Carl Friedrich von Weizsäcker, an alumnus.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Gasteiner Str. 23, 10717 Berlin in the Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district (Wilmersdorf area), the school is a public, tuition-free institution named after Goethe, with a main building constructed from basalt (socle), limestone (ground floor), and Warthau sandstone, designed by architect Otto (specific last name not detailed).[1][2][3][4][5] It is a cultural monument (Kulturdenkmal) and has been operational since at least 1954, with active blogging since 2008; no exact founding date is specified, but it serves a diverse student body highlighted in events and projects.[1][3][4] Facilities support open all-day operations (offener Ganztag), choirs, orchestras, and project days, with an upcoming Open Day on January 17, 2026.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This four-track (vierzügig) humanistic gymnasium with ancient languages track starts from Grade 5, offering an open all-day program leading to **Abitur** (university entrance qualification).[1] Foreign language sequence: English (1st, continued from Grade 1/3), Latin (2nd from Grade 5), Ancient Greek (3rd in Grade 7 for all; Grade 8 choice of Greek or French), optional French (4th from Grade 10).[1] Leistungskurse (advanced courses) include Ancient Greek, Biology, Chemistry, Deutsch, English, History, Art, Latin, Math, Music, Philosophy, Physics; Latin/Greek or French required as exam subject or 5th Abitur component.[1] Special offerings: gifted education (Begabtenschule des Landes Berlin), digital education (Lernraum Berlin platform, robotics/IT AGs, ITG in Grade 7, Informatics elective in Grade 10, GK Informatics/Digital Worlds), choir class.[1] No mentions of MSA or BBR pathways.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include working groups (AGs) like Denkmal-AG (monuments), Kalliope, Robotik; choir class, Jazzkooperative, Orchesterakademie, student firm, student mediation, development projects, participation (SV), sociocultural initiatives, Omnibus all-day program.[1] Arts and culture: Christmas concerts (e.g., 2024 "Träume werden wahr", "Winterschimmer"), art exhibitions (Hamburger Bahnhof visit by LK Art), reading competition (national), Tag des offenen Denkmals, project days.[1] Exchanges and trips: Den Haag/Netherlands, Paris (French 4th semester), kennenzlernfahrten (getting-to-know trips), Studien- und Austauschfahrten (study/exchange trips); internships (9th grades Jan 19-30, 2026), LK trial (10th grades).[1] "Faire Schule" promotes fair practices.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Internal reading competition and Tag des offenen Denkmals 2025 described as "full successes" (voller Erfolg); no formal awards, rankings, or Abitur outcomes specified.[1] Recognized as Begabtenschule (gifted school) by Berlin state and cultural monument status.[1][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (Öffentlich), enrollment follows Berlin public gymnasium processes, starting from Grade 5 for the ancient languages track; Open Day on January 17, 2026 (10:00-14:00) for tours.[1] No specific requirements detailed beyond standard public intake; English continuation from primary.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The Goethe-Gymnasium Lichterfelde traces its origins to April 8, 1872, when it was founded as the Krahmersche höhere Mädchenschule by Adelheid Krahmer in Villa Hildesheim (Berliner Straße, now Ostpreußendamm), initially serving six students[1]. It relocated in 1878 to a custom-built facility at Berliner Straße 166 (now Willi-Graf-Oberschule), benefiting from proximity to the Hauptkadettenanstalt for shared male instructors[1]. Renamed Lyzeum und Oberlyzeum der Gemeinde Berlin-Lichterfelde in 1911, it suffered heavy damage in 1943, reopened in June 1945, and moved to the current Drakestr. 72-74 building (previously used by Realgymnasium Lichterfelde)[1]. It became the Goethe-Schule in 1951 after a interim name as Lichterfelder Oberschule für Mädchen, and was officially renamed Goethe-Gymnasium Lichterfelde in 2016[1]. The school celebrated its 150th anniversary in 2022[1]. The current building, a protected monument (Baudenkmal), was constructed from 1909-1912 by architects Julia Ammer and Richard Tietzen[1][3]. A Förderverein (support association), Verein der Freunde des Goethe Gymnasiums Lichterfelde e.V., was founded in 1922 to support school projects[1]. description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Gottfried-Keller-Gymnasium emphasizes a **lebendiges, friedliches Schulklima** (vibrant, peaceful school climate) that enables effective learning, creative work, and discovery of personal inclinations and abilities among students, parents, and teachers.[2] As the only **gebundenes Ganztagsgymnasium** (full-day gymnasium) in Berlin-Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf, it fosters **Selbstverantwortung** (self-responsibility) for personality development and realization of individual learning paths in collaboration with others, detailed in its Ganztagskonzept.[2] The school's Leitbild promotes an atmosphere of mutual understanding, respect, consideration, and helpfulness, shaping the school as a **Lern- und Lebensraum** (learning and living space).[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1919**, the nearly 100-year-old building is currently undergoing major renovations while accommodating around **800-847 students** from **25 nations** and **83 teachers**.[1][2][4] Located at **Olbersstr. 38, 10589 Berlin** in the **Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf** district, it serves a diverse, international community.[1][2] Facilities include **Mediothek** (media library for independent project work), **three Schulstationen** (lounges with social pedagogues), **Lernlandschaften** (study areas), **Jugendbücherei & Leseinsel** (youth library and reading nook), **two on-site Sporthallen** plus one nearby, **Sportplatz & Fitnessraum**, **Bläser-Trakt** (brass band area), **Holzwerkstatt** (wood workshop with student firm "JustHolz"), **Kreativraum** (crafts space), **Jugendclub "straks"** (gaming and recreation), **Schulhof & Schulgarten**, and **Schulmensa & Cafeteria**.[2] Students actively shape these spaces, e.g., via Schulhof redesign and Tape-Art.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This Gymnasium serves grades **7-12/13** (Sekundarstufe I and II), leading to **Abitur** (gymnasium diploma); no mentions of MSA or BBR pathways typical of lower tracks.[1][2] It operates as a **gebundenes Ganztagsgymnasium** with extended hours: Monday-Thursday 8:00-15:55, Friday 8:00-14:15 (Oberstufe sports until 16:00).[2] Key features include **5 hours weekly Studienzeit** (supervised study replacing much homework), **Wahlpflichtbereich** from grade 8 (preparing for Oberstufe Leistungskurse), **Profilkurse** in grade 10, and integrationsfächer like **Naturwissenschaften**, **Literarisch-musikalisches Gestalten**, and **ITG** (informationstechnische Grundbildung) for non-third-language choosers.[2] Languages: **Englisch** (3 hrs from grade 7), optional third foreign language **Französisch** or **Latein** from grade 8 (enabling Latinum).[2] Enhanced **Kunst & Musik** in grade 9; **digital Lernwerkzeuge** integrated.[2] **Sport-Profilklassen** in handball, basketball, football.[1][4] Abitur outcomes: average grades 2.46-2.77 (2013-2024), success rates 90-100%.[1]

| Grade | Key Hours (Examples) |
|-------|----------------------|
| 7     | Deutsch 4, Englisch 3[2] |
| 8-10  | Wahlprofile/Profilkurse, 3rd language option[2] |
| 11-12 | Leistungskurse prep, extended sports[2] |

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize Ganztagsbetrieb with AGs (e.g., Holzwerkstatt, "JustHolz" firm), **Bläserklassen** (brass classes with performances), sports in **Profilklassen** (handball/basketball/football; e.g., basketballers played before 9000 at Uber Arena), **Fitnessraum** activities, and events like Adventsbasar, Mitsingkonzert, Nikolaus-Buffet.[1][2][4] Exchanges include **Frankreich-Austausch** with Courbevoie (grades 8-10).[2] Academic competitions: **Matheolympiade-Bezirksrunde** participation.[2] Physics Leistungskurse explore topics like sandcastles, hot Jupiters, bread tones.[2] Daily life includes project work, self-study in Mediothek, social spaces, and student-led designs.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Abitur** results show strong performance: averages **2.46-2.77** (2021 best at 2.46), success quotas **90-100%** (100% in 2021-2022).[1] Student enrollment grew to **847** (2023/24) with **83 teachers**.[1] Recent successes: **Matheolympiade** district round, France exchange, basketball exhibition game, well-attended Adventsbasar.[2] Holds **Sprachdiplome** (language diplomas) and partnerships listed (details unspecified); **Auszeichnungen** (awards) noted but not detailed.[2] Sports profile with pro-level exposure.[1][2][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Öffentlich school**, enrollment for new **7th classes (2026/27)** occurs **March 5-12, 2026**, requiring **prior appointment booking** at Schulbüro (Room 110, 1st floor, Entrance D, weekdays from 8:00).[2][3] Mandatory: **Förderprognose** with combined grade sum ≤14 in Deutsch, Mathe, 1st foreign language (English) from 5th/6th grade halves; bring Anmeldebogen, Mitbringliste, data privacy info.[3] Open house (**Tag der offenen Tür**) on **January 17, 2026**; info events for Oberstufe.[2][3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Gymnasium Steglitz promotes the development of young people through dialogue with antiquity and a foundation of solid knowledge, fostering historical consciousness and deep understanding of liberal democracy.[1][3] It emphasizes an open, friendly, cooperative atmosphere spanning from antiquity to the present, with problem-oriented, interdisciplinary, and project-based teaching that strengthens teamwork and social skills among students and teachers.[1][3] The school cultivates curiosity, performance readiness, and responsibility, aiming to develop holistic, responsible, and discerning personalities with empathy, critical thinking, civil courage, and cultural openness, while rejecting racism and discrimination; it prioritizes respectful relationships, agreed rules, and conflict management.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1886, the school is located at Heesestraße 15, 12169 Berlin, in the Steglitz-Zehlendorf district.[1][3] Its student and parent community draws from diverse Berlin neighborhoods, predominantly the southwest and southern outskirts.[1] Since the 2010/11 school year, it operates as an open all-day school (Ganztagsschule), offering after-school care (Hortbetreuung) for grades 5-10 in partnership with Mittelhof e.V., particularly tailored for grades 5-6.[1] No specific facilities beyond the address and rowing boats for the rowing team are detailed.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school serves as a Gymnasium starting from grade 5, with classical language tracks (altsprachlichen Zügen) beginning Latin as the second foreign language in grade 5 and Ancient Greek as the third from grade 8; English continues as the first foreign language from primary school, with optional French from grade 9.[1] From grade 7, it hosts the Staatliche Europa-Schule Neugriechisch and offers a Latin class with a digital education focus.[1] Upper secondary (Oberstufe) provides every subject as an advanced course (Leistungskurs), covering nearly all combinations, with Abitur exams in subjects like Latin, Ancient Greek, Modern Greek, History, Geography, Music, Art, French, and others; French can substitute classical languages for exams if continued.[1] Graduation leads to Abitur; no mentions of MSA or BBR.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Languages and Exchanges**: Exchanges with England (Malvern/Sherborne) in grade 9 and France (Paris, Lycée Louis-le-Grand); bilingual elective "Politics" from grade 10, continuable as project course (PW) in upper secondary; options for Hebräisch (Hebrew) as a third ancient language.[1]  
**Projects and Workshops**: Researcher days (Forscher­tage) three times yearly for grades 5-6 in sciences, music, social sciences, or media; project days for grade 7, social internships for grade 8; workshops on cyberbullying, sustainability field days, and Antikenfahrt (ancient studies trip).[1][3]  
**Arts and Music**: 12 music working groups (AGs); theater performances; project weeks in arts-related areas.[1][3]  
**Sports**: Basketball and ball games AGs with TuSLi; prominent rowing team (Ruderriege) with own boats; sports exams in ball games, swimming, 12-minute run, athletics.[1][3]  
**Other**: Extensive AG offerings; all-day school with care; class trips (Klassenfahrten).[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Every course request in the upper secondary has been fulfilled in the last two years.[1] Certifications include DELF-Diploma (for French university access), Cambridge Certificate, Ellenomatheia-Diploma (after Modern Greek AG), Graecum, Latinum, and Hebraicum.[1] The rowing team is described as large and successful.[1] No awards, rankings, or broader outcome statistics are mentioned.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public (öffentlich) tuition-free school, enrollment follows Berlin public school processes, with application periods for grade 5: first-choice until February 13, second-choice until March 19, third-choice until April 15 (2026 dates).[3] Info days for grade 10 advanced courses on January 19, 2026; trial lessons on February 23.[3] Diverse catchment from southwest Berlin and surroundings.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Gymnasium Tiergarten emphasizes high-quality education through engaged, competent teachers who continuously professionalize and collaborate with students and parents to develop the school. Core values include **engagiert** (committed teachers fostering school improvement), **sozial** (social responsibility via mutual respect, appreciation, and conflict resolution through goal-oriented discussions), and **vielfältig** (actively promoting diversity to support individual student interests and potentials).[1] The philosophy aligns with turning "mirrors into windows" through education, as quoted from Sydney J. Harris, prioritizing a positive school climate, stimulating offers, projects, school festivals, and events to build student identification.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Formed in the 2012/13 school year from the merger of Menzel-Schule and Heinrich-von-Kleist-Schule, which previously cooperated extensively, the school builds on their traditions to offer high-level education.[1] A 2019 school inspection report confirmed a good learning environment, ongoing development with kollegium engagement, and strong pedagogical conditions.[1] Located in Berlin-Mitte district at Altonaer Str. 26, 10555 Berlin, near Tiergarten park, it serves a central urban community.[1][3] Facilities include a Maker Space for ideas, sustainability, and creativity; contact via sekretariat@gymnasium-tiergarten.de or +49 (0) 30 398 00 97 10; and events like Tiergarten-Gespräche hosting experts in politics, society, sports, science, arts, and culture, including a recent AI press conference.[1] Open Door Day is scheduled for December 5, 2025, 16-19 Uhr.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (grades 7-12/13 leading to Abitur), it offers Mittelstufe (4-7 classes, 1-2 bilingual) and Oberstufe (around 120 Abitur graduates annually).[1] Key programs include bilingual German-English education since 2000 (Modell A, meeting Berlin standards for study proficiency in English), with intensive English in grades 7-8, adding CLIL subjects (Geography, Biology, History/Political Education) from grade 8; extras like Debating, Business English, Social Media.[1] Second foreign languages from grade 7: French, Latin, or Spanish; Chinese also available.[1][2] Planned for 2026/27: 2 bilingual Deutsch-Englisch classes and 3-4 regular classes.[1] School program (2019) and rules available as PDFs.[1]

| Grade | Key Bilingual Elements |
|-------|-------------------------|
| 7     | Englisch 3+1           |
| 8     | Englisch 3+2, Geografie 1+1 |
| 9     | Englisch 3, Geografie 1, Biologie 2+1 |
| 10    | Englisch 3, Geografie 1, Biologie 2+1, Geschichte 2 / Politische Bildung 1; LK Englisch + GK PW bilingual[1] |

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse extracurriculars: strong chess program (Deutsche Schachschule Bronze award); theater (e.g., "Requiem for a Rotten Society" at Abgeordnetenhaus, "Krug – Zerbrochen!" at Theater Strahl, Grips Theater events); opera visits (e.g., "Salome" at Komische Oper); excursions (Bundestag visits by 10a/10e, Q1-PW radio at Humboldt Forum, benbi workshop on gender diversity); Erasmus+ exchange to France ("rising waters, shared solutions"); Maker Space and creative redesign projects; school festivals and projects.[1] Arts, culture, politics, and social initiatives foster holistic development.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- Designated **Deutsche Schachschule (Bronze)** by Deutsche Schachjugend in November 2025.[1]
- Positive 2019 Schulinspektion: good learning conditions, engaging climate, successful development.[1]
- ~120 Abitur graduates yearly; theater invitations (e.g., Abgeordnetenhaus); active in competitions (Jugend-Herbstopen chess).[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public Gymnasium, admission follows Berlin public school processes with specific criteria detailed on the Aufnahmekriterien page; all guardians must sign forms. For 2026/27, expects 2 bilingual Deutsch-Englisch and 3-4 regular classes in entry year. Contact sekretariat for details; Open Door Day December 5, 2025, for tours and info.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto, "Ich will verstehen" ("I want to understand"), from Hannah Arendt, its namesake, emphasizes achieving university readiness (Studierfähigkeit), learning second or third foreign languages, individualized subject deepening with diverse electives, preparation for the upper secondary level (gymnasiale Oberstufe), and introduction to scientific work.[3] It prioritizes conveying societal values, work and quality standards for collaboration and community spirit, and alignment with Arendt's principles across all subjects.[3] The goal is to inspire students to learn, pursue dreams, achieve more, and succeed in life.[3][1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1999 in Berlin's Rudow neighborhood (Neukölln district) as the sixth Gymnasium in Neukölln, it was previously the Hannah-Arendt-Oberschule; construction began October 1, 1998, with the building occupied September 4, 2000, and named after Hannah Arendt on May 4, 2001.[2][1] The 9,631 m² campus on former Rudow fields includes a rectangular site bounded by Waltersdorfer Chaussee (north), Lieselotte-Berger-Straße (south), and Elfriede-Kuhr-Straße (west), with an internal path (Benedicta-Teresia-Weg) and square (Helene-Jung-Platz), plus an outdoor pond with wooden bridge and seating.[2] Facilities feature two rooms each for music, art, biology, and physics; three for chemistry; five computer pools; an aula, library, and upper-grade student lounge; cafeteria; four computer specialist rooms; notebook pools; computer islands in departments; language lab; modern media lab for music and technology; accessible parking, elevator, and restrooms for wheelchair users.[2][4] Enrollment is 812 students with 62 teachers (2023/24), up from 745 in 2017/18.[1][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a Gymnasium, it offers grades leading to Abitur (university entrance qualification), with strong preparation for the gymnasiale Oberstufe.[3] Languages include S1 English/French, S4 English/Spanish, and third foreign language Latin.[1][4] Leistungskurse (advanced courses) in art, biology, chemistry, German, English, geography, informatics, mathematics, music, physics, and political education.[1][4] Additional offerings: bilingual German/English programs, philosophy instruction to Abitur, vocational preparation with qualification modules, and second educational pathway.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) include football, basketball, hip-hop/streetdance, golf, choir, instrumental groups, drums/percussions, keyboard, school band, Spanish, homepage maintenance, geology, chemistry, costumes, student newspaper, school radio, and theater.[4] Emphasis on individual learning, language education, cultural and political education, school quality support, career orientation, school social work, and prevention programs.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Abitur averages are consistently excellent: 2.28 (2024, 95% pass rate), 2.37 (2023, 94%), 2.25 (2022, 97%), with historical averages around 2.4 and pass rates 93-100%.[1] Ranked #1 among Neukölln Gymnasiums in 2024 Abitur average; #3 in pass rate.[5] High demand: 98% first-choice placements in recent admissions.[5] Social index of 55.3 indicates moderate structural burden.[5] Building praised as a "budget miracle" by Deutsche Bauzeitung for halving costs.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public (öffentlich) tuition-free Gymnasium in Berlin-Neukölln, enrollment follows standard Berlin public school processes via centralized Schulanmeldung, with high demand (e.g., 125 first-choice applications for 128 places recently).[5] No tuition, registration, or material fees specified.[School Information]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Hans-Carossa-Gymnasium is a modern, five-track Gymnasium operating in an open all-day program (Offenes Ganztagsbetrieb), emphasizing a **broad, solid gymnasiale Grundbildung** with individual elective options across all educational pathways.[2] It prioritizes comprehensive secondary education with special pedagogical tracks, including school development focuses on lesson development, inclusion experiments, child protection, social learning, reading competence, and addiction/drug prevention.[1][2] The school values performance-oriented yet life-relevant education ("Leistungsstark und lebensnah"), fostering individualized learning paths as articulated by principal Rußbült.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Am Landschaftspark Gatow 40, 14089 Berlin (Kladow, Spandau district), the school has approximately **1,120–1,155 students** and has been in its current site since 2000, previously in Hakenfelde.[1][3] It recently renamed to honor Margot Friedländer—the first school in Germany named after her—approved unanimously by the school conference, with national media coverage and events like Holocaust remembrance and a special postage stamp presentation.[2] Facilities include a 2014-built cafeteria (Mensa), new teaching kitchen, library, and ongoing energetic renovation of the south facade; it participates in community projects like street art on utility boxes.[1][2][6]

### 3. CURRICURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it offers grades leading to **Abitur** (university entrance qualification), with five tracks (züge) including four 7th-grade classes and two specialized foundational tracks from early grades.[1][2] Specialized programs include bilingual education (enhanced English/French from grade 5, Geography in English from grade 7, History in English from grade 9 in "a-classes"); strengthened natural sciences (more STEM hours, Biology in English in grade 8 for "b-classes"); electives like Spanish and Latin from grade 8.[1] Additional initiatives: MINT-friendly (STEM-recognized multiple times), Erasmus+ school, hybrid digital learning experiment with Humboldt University, private tablets in class, and phone regulations.[1][2] No specific MSA or BBR pathways mentioned, aligning with Gymnasium focus on Abitur.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse excursions (e.g., Messe Berlin with fire truck demos, Sachsenhausen memorial, Helmholtz Center for superconductors, Police Academy with training and dogs) and cultural events (Chanukah at Finance Ministry, multicultural dinners, Christmas cooking, Shakespeare "A Midsummer Night's Dream" performance).[2] Extracurriculars include sports (girls' volleyball Berlin champions, U18 bronze "Jugend trainiert für Olympia," state finals qualification, tennis), arts (choir, theater/acting, philosophy competition, art LK on emotions/body language/Lygia Clark), debating AG (successful since 2011), English Theater AG, internships (e.g., school janitor assistant for inclusion students), politics (meeting Berlin parliamentarians), and podcasts on school life/AI.[1][2] All-day operations support social learning and events like World Kindness Day, open house, and hiking days.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Renaming honor**: First German school named after Margot Friedländer, with national media (dpa, Tagesspiegel, radio/TV), foundation press release, and events tied to her 104th birthday (2025).[2] Sports: Girls' volleyball Berlin masters, U18 Olympia bronze.[2] Certifications: **Umweltschule in Europa** (European Eco-School) for environmental projects; multiple **MINT-freundliche Schule** (STEM-friendly school) awards; Erasmus+ designation.[1] Other: Successful debating, philosophy contests, art performances for open house, and evaluations showing progress in reading, argumentation, digitalization, social learning (2024/25 report).[1][2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich), tuition-free** Gymnasium in Berlin, enrollment follows standard public school processes via local district assignment, with no tuition, registration fees, or income-based requirements noted.[2] Recent info-brief mentions new staff, attestation rules for missed tests, and upper-grade dates, suggesting open enrollment tied to Berlin's school choice system; Tag der offene Tür (open house) showcases the school.[2]

description:
# Heinrich-Hertz-Gymnasium Berlin School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Heinrich-Hertz-Gymnasium** positions itself as a school for students who enjoy thinking, researching, and creative problem-solving, with a particular emphasis on mathematics and natural sciences[1]. The school's mission centers on empowering curious students to discover, understand, and critically reflect on the world of mathematics and natural sciences through scientifically-oriented instruction[1].

The school's core values emphasize:

- **Intellectual curiosity and competent discourse**: Students develop pleasure in thinking, are intellectually curious, discuss competently, and enjoy experimental work[1]
- **Excellence**: The school's top students achieve peak performance at national and international levels[1]
- **Social competence**: Students develop into socially competent, well-rounded individuals[1]
- **Responsibility**: Students use their special abilities to take responsibility for their lives and society[1]
- **Mutual support**: Students value their peers' achievements and support one another[1]

The school emphasizes that logical, structured thinking benefits not only MINT subjects (mathematics, computer science, natural sciences) but also supports comprehensive general education and prepares students for modern challenges[1]. Additionally, the school is committed to implementing child protection and youth safety concepts, ensuring a violence-free, secure, and respectful learning environment[1].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Heritage**: The Heinrich-Hertz-Gymnasium was **founded in 1961** and has enjoyed a reputation as a renowned mathematics and natural sciences gymnasium for over 50 years[1][3]. The school is named after the legendary German physicist Heinrich Hertz[3].

**Location**: The school is located in **Berlin-Friedrichshain** at Rigaer Str. 81-82, 10247 Berlin[4]. Originally located in Adlershof, the school has historical ties to the science park that date back to its founding[3].

**Community Size**: The school serves approximately **560 students**[3].

**Partnerships and Connections**: Since 1991, the school has maintained working relationships with the Adlershof technology park and its institutes, including the Leibniz Institute for Crystal Growth (Institut für Kristallzüchtung, IKZ) and the mathematics department of Humboldt-Universität Berlin[3]. Students regularly participate in internships, experiments, and lectures at these partner institutions[3].

**Facilities**: The school is currently undergoing a **new building construction project**. The winning architectural design has been selected, featuring a terraced structure that creates diverse learning landscapes with roof terraces for outdoor learning, experimentation, and exploration[5]. The design emphasizes flexibility between compartmentalized and home-base learning models[5].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grades Offered**: The school serves students from **grade 5 through grade 12** (Klassen 5–12), following the German gymnasium system[1].

**Academic Profile**: The school features an **expanded course load in MINT subjects** in the secondary level (Sekundarstufe I), including mathematics, physics, chemistry, and computer science[1]. This specialization continues in the upper gymnasium level (gymnasiale Oberstufe) through mandatory course selections[1].

**Educational Approach**: The curriculum is characterized by scientifically-oriented instruction that develops logical, structured thinking and supports comprehensive general education[1].

**Graduation Pathways**: As a gymnasium, students work toward the **Abitur** (German university entrance qualification). The school's curriculum is designed to prepare students for higher education, with approximately **60–70% of graduates pursuing careers in MINT professions**[3].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities and Competitions**: The school maintains a broad range of activities (AG-Angebot) and encourages student participation in academic competitions[1]:

- **Mathematics competitions**: Students participate in the Federal Mathematics Competition (Bundeswettbewerb Mathematik), with 11 students recognized in the 2025 second round[1]. The school also participates in the "Long Night of Mathematics" (Lange Nacht der Mathematik)[1]
- **Science competitions**: Students compete in the Physics Marathon, Chemistry Kids (Chemkids) competition, and other science-focused events[1]
- **Presentation skills**: Student Tamina Grunert won the national "Jugend präsentiert" (Youth Presents) competition in 2025[1]
- **Chess**: School teams compete in German school chess championships[1]
- **Theater**: The Theater AG produces classical plays with modern interpretations, with recent productions including "Best of Grimms" and "Romeo and Julia"[1]
- **Civic engagement**: Students participate in the "denkmal aktiv" (Monument Active) project, exploring Berlin's historical sites and cultural heritage[1]

**School Community Events**: The school hosts an annual summer festival featuring student performances, exhibitions, and project showcases[1].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school is **widely recognized across Berlin for outstanding mathematics and natural sciences performance**[1]. Recent achievements include:

- **11 students** recognized in the 2025 Federal Mathematics Competition (second round)[1]
- **Rudolf Shtraube** won the 2025 Physics Marathon with 19.85 points, and the school won the overall school trophy with a significant margin[1]
- Multiple students achieved strong placements in the Chemistry Kids competition[1]
- **Tamina Grunert** (grade 11) won the national "Jugend präsentiert" competition in 2025[1]
- School chess teams placed **9th and 13th** at the 2025 German School Chess Championships[1]
- Approximately **60–70% of graduates pursue MINT careers**, reflecting the school's success in fostering interest in these fields[3]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Enrollment Expansion**: Beginning in the 2025/26 school year, the school is accepting **two new seventh-grade classes for the first time**[1].

**Registration Process**: Online registration appointments became available starting **January 18, 2026 at 10:00 AM**[1].

**Registration Periods**:
- **Grade 5**: February 2026 (details to follow)[1]
- **Grade 7**: March 5–12, 2026[1]

**Public School Status**: As a public school (Öffentlich), the Heinrich-Hertz-Gymnasium is **tuition-free**. There are no enrollment fees or tuition costs for students[1].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Heinrich-Schliemann-Gymnasium positions itself as an institution that builds bridges, emphasizing its **humanistic and language-oriented profile** with a tradition in classical languages.[2][6] It offers a comprehensive education focused on **future-oriented learning**, including digital tools, media education concepts for grades 5-12, and strong language training starting with mandatory second foreign languages from grade 5.[1][7] Core values include fostering clear thinking, fair debate, literacy, health prevention, and community engagement through events and alumni contact.[1][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as the 309th and 310th Gemeindedoppelschule in 1914 in a landmark-protected building designed by architect Ludwig Hoffmann (1910-1915), it evolved post-1945 and became the Heinrich-Schliemann-Gymnasium, successor to the Luisenstädtisches Gymnasium, with an altsprachlicher Zweig established in 1993/94.[2] Located in **Prenzlauer Berg, Pankow district** at Dunckerstr. 64, 10439 Berlin, it serves ~1122 students with 97 teachers (2023/24 data).[3] Facilities include wheelchair-accessible design, two computer rooms, school library with internet stations, homework room, student club, cafeteria/mensa, bike racks, digital whiteboards in all classrooms, mobile laptops/iPads, robotics sets, and specialized labs (e.g., physics, math, biology, chemistry).[1][4][7] Public transport access is excellent (S-Bahn Prenzlauer Allee 4 min, U-Bahn Schönhauser Allee 8 min, tram 2 min).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium with gymnasiale Oberstufe**, it covers grades 5-12/13, leading to **Abitur** (no MSA or BBR mentioned).[1][3] **Altsprachlich** track starts grade 5 with Latin as 2nd language (English 1st), adding 3rd language (Ancient Greek or French) from grade 8; **neusprachlich** track from grade 7 with French/Spanish.[1][2][4][5][7] Additional languages: Italian, Russian (AGs or options).[3][4] Leistungskurse (advanced courses): Art, Biology, Chemistry, German, English, Geography, French, History, Math, Physics, Political Education, Spanish.[3][4] Bilingual offerings in Deutsch/Englisch (Sport, History, Geography, Biology from grade 8; Business English in Oberstufe); digital integration (WebUntis, IServ, GeoGebra, etc.).[4][5][7] Two 5th and two 7th grade classes admitted annually.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize languages, sports, arts, and community: **Jugend debattiert** debate finals, **Big Challenge** English contest, **Berliner Altsprachenfest**, **Mini-Marathon** (90+ students grades 5-12 through Brandenburg Gate), **Mathe im Advent**, **Weihnachts-Basketballturnier**, **Große Lesenacht** literacy event, **Präventionsradar** health study.[1][8] AGs (working groups): Italian, Spanish/Guitar, Drama, School Radio, IT/Network, Graffiti, Chess, Environment, Basketball, Volleyball; school social work, inclusion team.[4][5][7] Other: Schliemann-Tage projects, alumni events, student representation, mensa meals.[1][2][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Participation in competitions like **Big Challenge** (English), **Jugend debattiert**, **Mini-Marathon**, **Altsprachenfest**, **Präventionsradar** health study, **Mathe im Advent**, and others (e.g., Lebendige Antike, Jugend forscht, DLRZ aerospace).[1][5][8] Enrollment growth to 1122 students (2023/24); strong language/MINT ratings (top 9 in languages per schulen.de).[3][5] No specific awards or Abitur pass rates detailed in sources.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (Öffentlich), admission is open; annually takes two 5th grades (Latin start) and two 7th grades (French/Spanish).[1][4] Contact sekretariat@hsg-berlin.de (Mo-Fr 07:30-14:00); principal Hr. Pietraßyk-Kendziorra, secretaries Fr. Knospe/Fr. Pospieszny. Phone +49 30 446895-0.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Heinz-Berggruen-Gymnasium is an **altsprachliche Oberschule** (classical languages upper secondary school) with a strong emphasis on **artistic and musical profiles**, combining rigorous classical language education with creative and performative arts.[1][2][3][9] It promotes creativity, improvisation, playfulness, and discipline through theater and music, fostering self-reliance and responsibility in project-based learning.[5] The school's approach integrates altsprachlich (Latin from grade 5, optional Ancient Greek), kunstbetontes (art-focused), and musikbetontes (music-focused) tracks, originating from a pioneering "Neusprachlichen Zug musischer Richtung" in 1953/54.[2][3][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in September 1945 as "Charlottenburger Gymnasium" post-WWII (initially altsprachlich, housed at Heerstraße 12), it succeeded institutions like the Mommsen-Gymnasium and Kaiserin-Augusta-Gymnasium; renamed Heinz-Berggruen-Gymnasium in August 2008 after the art dealer.[3][4][6] The building, a protected monument (former Erich-Hoepner-Schule), was rebuilt 1954-1956 by architect Robert Tepez.[3] Located in Berlin's Westend (Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district, near Theodor-Heuss-Platz U-Bahn and fairgrounds), it serves **865 students** with **78 teachers**.[1][2] Facilities include a school library, modern specialized rooms, media-equipped classrooms, fiber optic connection, full networking, aula, concert hall access (e.g., UdK), schoolyard stage, and three table tennis tables.[2][10]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (grades 5-13, leading to **Abitur**), it offers classes starting from grade 5 with Latin (altsprachliche Züge), S2 English/Latin, third languages **Ancient Greek** or **French**.[1][2] **Leistungskurse** (advanced courses) include Art, German, English, Geography, French, History, Greek, Latin, Math, Music, Philosophy, Political Education.[1][2][4] Special profiles: artistic (e.g., theater as elective in Sek I Grundkurs in Sek II, art as LK), musical (Bläserklassen), from grade 5; no accelerated classes or Grundschulteil.[2][4][5] Gymnasiale Oberstufe supports Abitur pathway.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Rich extracurriculars emphasize arts and music: ensembles like Mucke-Truppe, choirs (Mittelstufenchor, Großer Chor), Combo, Bigband, Sinfonietta, Großes Orchester; AGs in Keramik, Fotografie, Manga, Trickfilm, Leseprofis, DELF, Theater, Tanz-AG, Garten-AG, student-led OHnE.[2][9][10] Events include Weihnachtskonzerte at UdK, Kammermusikabend, Weihnachtsbasteln, Tag der Offenen Tür.[9] International exchanges (e.g., EnGedi Boarding School Israel during Kunstwochen).[9] Sports: table tennis.[10] Partnerships: Deutsches Symphonie-Orchester Berlin (DSO), Freie Universität Berlin, Universität der Künste Berlin.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Pioneered Germany's first music-focused language track in 1953/54, influencing other schools.[3] Hosts high-profile events like UdK concerts and international exchanges.[9] No specific awards or rankings mentioned; admissions use average Förderprognose note and other criteria if oversubscribed.[2] Berufsorientierung includes internships, career fairs, competency assessments.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; enroll by reserving appointment via phone (902928702) for document submission at Sekretariat; required documents listed on site.[9] Opens two grade 5 classes annually with Latin; selection by average promotion prognosis note if oversubscribed.[1][2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Herder-Gymnasium is a **Gymnasium** with a mathematical and natural sciences profile and modern languages education, offering various individualized educational pathways.[1][4] It emphasizes a diverse educational offering committed to the spirit of the Enlightenment, promoting independent thinking ("Selber denken macht schlau!"), with special focus in profile classes on mathematics and natural sciences, and individual emphases set through elective subjects from grade 8.[1][4] Core values include reliability, respect, tolerance, solidarity, and responsible action, fostered through working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften, AGs) and cross-grade committees.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded around 1903 and named after Johann Gottfried Herder, the school originated as the Westend-Schule in Leistikowstraße with 800 students, moving to its current buildings in 1929; it served as a hospital during and after World War II until becoming the Herder-Oberschule in 1952 under Berlin's co-educational law.[4][6] Located in Berlin's **Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf** district at **Westendallee 45-46, 14052 Berlin**, about a 5-minute walk from S-Bahn Heerstraße station.[4][6] It serves a community of 838 students (2021/2022 data), with four classes per grade from grade 7; facilities support block scheduling, a regulated lunch period (Mittagsband) in a cafeteria (Mensa), and school events.[1][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium** (grades 5-12/13 leading to **Abitur**), it uses 90-minute block periods (since 2013/2014), starting at 8:00 AM and ending by 15:10 PM in Sekundarstufe I after the 4th block, with flexibility for project-based, student-paced learning and practical activities.[1][4] Profile programs include mathematically-natural sciences tracks from grade 5 (since 2005/2006 as a gifted program experiment, expanded to two 5th-grade profiles and one 7th-grade profile since 2013/2014, plus one standard 7th-grade class annually).[4] Modern languages (neusprachlich) feature Spanish as a second foreign language option alongside French (since 2016/2017); elective subjects from grade 8.[1][4] Participates in Berlin's deepened vocational orientation program (BvBO).[4] School year structured around committees, events, competitions, trips, and exchanges.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include working groups (AGs) and cross-grade governance for community building.[1] Sports achievements feature the swim team placing 5th at Berlin Oberschulen Championships (Sek 2) and basketball team in ALBA-Oberschulliga preliminary round.[1] International exchanges include Spanish language program to Sevilla (October 2025).[1] Guest speakers, such as journalist Konrad Schuller on Ukraine (December 2025), and events like Weihnachtsmarkt (December 4, 2025) and open house (January 16, 2026).[1] Year-round rhythm includes competitions, school trips, and student exchanges.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Sports successes: 5th place in Berlin Oberschulen swimming championships (Sek 2, pre-Christmas 2025) and participation in ALBA basketball league.[1] Ongoing success in competitions structures the school year; participates in BvBO for vocational preparation.[1][4] No specific academic rankings, awards, or Abitur pass rates mentioned in available content.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public, tuition-free** Öffentlich school, enrollment follows Berlin's standard Gymnasium process post-primary school, with profiled 5th and 7th classes and one standard 7th class annually; open house (Tag der offenen Tür) on January 16, 2026, includes program info and trial lessons (Schnupperunterricht).[1][4] No tuition, registration, or material fees specified.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Hermann-Hesse-Gymnasium emphasizes **diversity, democratic participation, and holistic digitalization** as core values, fostering a vibrant school environment in Berlin's Kreuzberg Graefekiez that mirrors the neighborhood's multiculturalism.[4][6] Its educational approach is project-based and interdisciplinary, promoting key competencies like teamwork, self-organization, and personal responsibility through a full-day schedule with intensive learning phases, relaxation periods, class councils, and balance discussions to build independence and democratic understanding.[1][7] The school views linguistic diversity as an opportunity, actively supporting multilingualism, and integrates media education and digital tools to enhance engaging, effective instruction.[1][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Formerly the Hermann-Hesse-Oberschule, the school transformed within a decade from a dilapidated problem school into a model institution through participatory practices and community engagement, earning strong identification from students, teachers, and parents.[1][2] Located at **Böckhstr. 16, 10967 Berlin** in Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg (Kreuzberg district, near U-Bahn Schönleinstraße), it serves a diverse community with about 400 students in Sekundarstufe I (grades 7-10) and 120 in Sekundarstufe II (gymnasiale Oberstufe, grades 11-13), totaling around 593 students and 60 teachers.[2][6] Facilities include a mediathek for self-study, an aula for events, and a gym under renovation (current narrow access via Böckhstr. 16; alternative wide entrance at Dieffenbachstr. 51).[1][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **gebundenes Ganztagsgymnasium** (bound full-day gymnasium), it offers grades 7-13 leading to **Abitur** (gymnasiale Oberstufe), with no mention of MSA or BBR pathways.[1][2][3] Languages include S1 English/French, S5 French/English, third foreign language Latin or Italian.[2][3] Advanced courses (Leistungskurse) cover Art, Biology, German, English, French, Mathematics, Physics, Political Education.[2][3] Profile courses (Profilkurse) for grades 7-10 include Robotik, Kunst, Tanz, Fußball, Tischtennis, Schach, 3D-Druck, Maker Space, Radio/Podcast, Foto, Achtsamkeit, Theater, Film, plus creative, music, and Spanish options.[1][5][7][8] Individual learning time is provided in the full-day program.[8]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in interdisciplinary projects like **Hesse for Future** (sustainability initiatives), **Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage** (anti-discrimination since 2010, with Courage AG since 2017), and **Kulturagenten für kreative Schulen** (cultural education reference school since 2021).[4] Extracurriculars and working groups (AGs) include theater visits (e.g., "Der zerbrochene Krug"), student newspaper (HERMI with interviews, LGBTQIA+ topics, recipes, quizzes), Stolperstein-AG, music (choir, guitar, keyboard), sports (basketball, football), art, chemistry experiments, nature, and language prep (DELF, Cambridge ESOL).[3][4] Full-day rhythm includes relaxation, midday breaks, and self-chosen profile courses fostering creativity and media production.[1][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school holds the **"Mehrsprachige Schule" seal** (awarded December after two-year pilot by Staatssekretärin Christina Henke) and renewed **"Exzellente Digitale Schule"** certification.[4] It participates in "Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage" (20-year milestone celebrated) and is a reference school for cultural agents.[4] Historical Abitur results show strong performance: averages 2.54-2.77, success rates 83-98% (2013-2017).[2] Vorbereitung for DELF and Cambridge-ESOL exams is offered.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, enrollment for 7th grade is open from **March 5-12, 2026**; application documents and details available via the school's Anmeldung section.[4] Open house (Tag der offenen Tür) is scheduled for **January 24, 2026, 10:00-13:00**.[4] Upon overdemand, admission uses average grade from primary school promotion prognosis.[3]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Hildegard-Wegscheider-Gymnasium (HWG) is a modern, open school emphasizing a **naturwissenschaftlich-mathematischen Profilzug** (STEM-focused profile) starting from grades 5 and 7, with alternatives like Medien und Kommunikation (MuK) or standard track from grade 7.[6][9] It cultivates a personal pedagogical relationship with students, fosters joy in achievement, rewards initiative and responsibility, promotes global openness, tolerance, and understanding, and maintains a positive school climate as an alternative in the "PISA valley."[4][5] The school actively involves students in shaping daily life, personal development through extracurriculars, and preparation for future life skills.[6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as a higher girls' school in 1896-1897 (main building by Carl Lange), it expanded in 1909 with a stylistically distinct building, creating architectural pluralism recognized as a listed monument; it evolved into a full Gymnasium offering Abitur by 1924, admitted boys in 1967, introduced social science tracks in 1965, and added fast-track programs in 1993.[2][3][8] Located in Berlin-Grunewald (Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district), a green villa colony near S-Bahn Grunewald, it serves ~580 students with 54 teachers in a "cool school in the green with great learning atmosphere."[1][7] Facilities include a naturwissenschaftlicher Trakt (science wing), aula stage, computer rooms, interactive whiteboards, and chalk-free classrooms; it hosts notable guests like Angela Merkel and Mikhail Gorbachev.[4][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a Gymnasium (grades 5-12/13 leading to **Abitur**), it offers **MINT (STEM) profiles** from grade 5 and from grade 7 (alternatives: MuK or standard track), with languages sequence: English (S1), French (2nd), optional Latin/Japanese/Italian (3rd).[1][6][7][9] Advanced courses (Leistungskurse) include Art, Chemistry, German, English, French, History, Informatics, Math, Music, Political Science, Biology, Physics, Geography.[1][7] Additional offerings: elective choices from grade 8, philosophy, science propaedeutic, Business English, Technoclub in upper grades; certified career orientation (excellent until 2023), support tutoring.[7] No explicit MSA/BBR pathways mentioned, as focus is Gymnasium/Abitur.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in projects, competitions, working groups (AGs), class trips, course trips, language exchanges, stage performances, and cultural events like those at Open Day.[6] Extracurriculars (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) include rowing, math, queer support, SMART, anti-racism, chess, environment, jazz-rock-pop, theater, finance, first aid, physics.[7] Sports/experiences: windsurfing, fast-track support (Schnellläufer/Superschnellläufer for young high achievers), school social work, psychology.[4][7] Open Day (Jan 16, 2026) showcases subjects, projects, performances, and catering.[6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Positive school inspections in 2007 and 2013; strong average grades (e.g., 2.11 in 2024); contacts with companies/universities; high gifted education (Hochbegabtenförderung); certified excellent career orientation until 2023; architectural landmark status.[1][4][7] No specific awards/rankings detailed beyond consistent performance stats and guest speakers (e.g., Angela Merkel).[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, enrollment prioritizes average grade from promotion prognosis at over-demand; direct entry to MINT profile from grade 5, with choices at grade 7.[1][7][10] Open Day on January 16, 2026 (15:30-18:30) for tours and program insights; contact via phone (+49 30 8974 5421 0), fax, or email (sekretariat@hwos.de).[6][7]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **Immanuel-Kant-Gymnasium** is a mathematically and scientifically oriented Gymnasium emphasizing a **MINT-Profil** (mathematics, informatics, natural sciences, technology), with profile classes starting in grades 5 and 7 featuring enriched curricula in mathematics and sciences, such as set theory, propositional logic, and sequences.[1][3][4][5] It positions itself as a school of special pedagogical focus, fostering mathematical giftedness through partnerships like the Berlin Network of Mathematically-Natural Science Profile Schools and Humboldt-Universität collaborations, including delegate teachers and projects like INNOMATH Erasmus+.[2][3][4] Core values include digital excellence ("Exzellente digitale Schule" certification), an inclusive community ("Schule ohne Rassismus"), and holistic development via ganztag offerings, prevention programs, and talent support.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1912** as the Kant-Schule (evolving from a 1905 private school led by Meta Horter, taken over by the Friedrichsfelde community), the school occupies a historic **1906 building** at Lückstr. 63 (also listed as 60-63), 10317 Berlin-Lichtenberg (Rummelsburg/Weitlingkiez), featuring a three-to-four-story brick structure with a clock tower, art stone facade, and a notable student-on-snail sculpture.[1][2][6] The community includes ~**870 students**, **82 teachers** (2023/24), a school psychologist, student council, peer mediators, and parent support via Förderverein; it promotes an "active school" with open all-day concepts, social work, and anti-racism initiatives.[1][2][5] Facilities highlight digital infrastructure: fiber-optic internet, modern PC rooms, maker lab, digital blackboards, school library, and tools like WebUntis, IServ, Kant-Hub.[5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serving **grades 5-12/13** (Gymnasium leading to **Abitur**), it offers a broad curriculum with **MINT focus**: core subjects include Biologie, Chemie, Informatik/ITG, Mathematik, Physik; languages (**S1: Englisch/Französisch, S2: Englisch/Latein**, third options Französisch/Latein); humanities like Deutsch, Geschichte & Politik, Ethik, Psychologie; arts/sports (Kunst, Musik, Sport, Darstellendes Spiel); electives (Wahlpflichtunterricht), Französisch, Geografie.[1][3][4][5] Profile classes (from grade 5/7) add weekly hours in enriched math/science; Leistungskurse (advanced) in Bildende Kunst, Biologie, Chemie, Deutsch, Englisch, Erdkunde, Französisch, Geschichte, Informatik, Latein, Mathematik, Physik; upper secondary (Sekundarstufe II) includes course selection, internships, career orientation (BSO).[1][4] No explicit MSA/BBR mentioned; public Gymnasium pathway to university-prep Abitur (historical averages ~2.3-2.4, 93-98% pass rate).[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars abound: **AGs** (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) like Imker (beekeeping), Astro, Mathematische Schülergesellschaft, Kantmade, Schülerreporter (KaosZeit), Digitale Darstellung (videos); events include Kantabende (themed evenings), Hoffest & Sponsorenlauf, Weihnachtsabend, Sleepover, Schulball, "Langer Nachmittag der Wissenschaften," competitions (Wettbewerbe), excursions (Exkursionen), student trips (Schülerfahrten), prevention days.[3][4][5] Sports: football successes (e.g., girls' tournament), "Sportlichste Klasse"; arts via Musik, Kunst, Darstellendes Spiel; ganztag all-day programs with team projects, talent promotion (Begabungsförderung), Girls'/Boys' Day, foreign stays (Auslandsaufenthalt); digital daily life and projects emphasized.[1][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as **"Exzellente digitale Schule"** (2014/award 25.09., recent reaffirmation); joined **MINT-EC Excellence Network** (November 2025) for advanced MINT programs; member of Berlin MINT profile schools network (since 2001) and INNOMATH Erasmus+ for gifted math students.[2][3][4][5] Strong Abitur outcomes: averages 2.30-2.46 (2013-2017), 93-98% success; participates in Humboldt-Universität projects (e.g., Lust auf Mathematik, ZE1); sports wins like girls' football tournament.[1][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public school**, enrollment is open via **Tag der offenen Tür** (open house), with specific **Anmeldezeiträume** for grades 5 and 7; **Aufnahmekriterien** (admission criteria) apply for profile classes/selection.[1][5] Processes cover Sekundarstufe II (upper secondary), with beratungstermine (consultations), no fees noted beyond standard public materials.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johann-Gottfried-Herder-Gymnasium is a language-oriented Gymnasium emphasizing modern foreign languages as **keys to the world**, including English, Spanish, Russian, and French, to prepare students for global political, economic, technical, ecological, and cultural engagement.[2][5] It promotes individual talents through challenging instruction across linguistic-artistic, societal, mathematical-scientific, and IT fields, fostering fairness, respect, tolerance, and social interaction based on democratic and pluralistic values.[2][4][5] The school's motto, *"Im Erkennen sollen alle Seelenkräfte zusammenwirken"* (In knowing, all soul forces should work together), underscores holistic development, creativity, maturity, discussion, and cultural respect.[5][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1975 as the unique DDR "Spezialschule für Fremdsprachen" with early English or French from grade 3 and intensified Russian from grade 5, it evolved into a modern Gymnasium.[4][6] Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Fennpfuhl district) at Franz-Jacob-Str. 8, 10369 Berlin, in a typical DDR-type building renovated 2014–2018 (including the gym in 2020) with a redesigned schoolyard featuring 100-meter tracks and basketball courts.[1][3][4] It serves ~750–999 students from a broad catchment area beyond Lichtenberg, including Hohenschönhausen, Pankow, and others, with ~87–93 teachers; the campus is a spacious, park-like area shaped by students, teachers, and parents.[3][5][6] It offers open all-day operations.[1][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
A **Gymnasium** (grades 5–12/13) focused on languages, with a foundational bilingual branch starting in **grade 5** (two classes/year, intensified English preparing for bilingual Geography from grade 8 and History from grade 9) and a modern languages branch from **grade 7** (two classes/year).[1][2][4][7] Languages include S1 English/French, S3 English/Russian, S4 English/Spanish, plus 3rd/4th options like French, Spanish, Latin; performance courses in Biology, Chemistry, German, English, French, History, Math, Physics.[3][7] Balanced with math-science-IT emphasis; leads to **bilingual Abitur** (no MSA/BBR mentioned as primary paths).[1][3][7] Current school program approved June 2018, under revision for agility.[scraped]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERISES
Bilingual instruction applies languages practically (e.g., discussions in English on politics/history/geography), supported by artistic projects via Kulturagenten program for creative experiences.[5][6][8] Sports facilities include renovated gym and outdoor tracks/basketball; students bring sports clothes for trial days.[scraped][4] Open all-day school fosters social development; alumni note strong foundations in languages and sciences like Biology.[1][5][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Strong Abitur results: averages 1.97–2.09 (2013–2017), 98–100% pass rates; enrollment grew from 665 (2012/13) to 989 (2023/24).[3] Participation in Kulturagenten for cultural education; resolution by faculty; no other awards specified.[scraped][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public tuition-free school; annual intake of two classes each for **grade 5** (from 4th graders) and **grade 7** (from 6th graders).[2][7] Trial day ("Schnuppertag") on Dec 2, 2025, for 80 4th graders and 100 6th graders to sample classes (register via forms on site, bring sports clothes); program details from Nov 17, 2025.[scraped][2] See school enrollment page for process.[scraped]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The John-Lennon-Gymnasium emphasizes a **leistungsorientiert** (performance-oriented), **engagiert** (engaged), and **weltoffen** (cosmopolitan/open to the world) approach to education, as highlighted on its main page[School Website Main Page]. It positions itself as a "Smart School" with a visionary concept for contemporary learning in the digital age, focusing on digital excellence and innovative teaching[School Website About Page][1]. The school avoids subject-specific emphases, instead prioritizing these core values to foster high achievement, commitment, and global openness[1].

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The school building, a four-story brick structure in Academic Historicism style designed by Hermann Blankenstein in 1884/85, originally housed the 153rd Boys' and 154th Girls' Schools; it was renamed after August Bebel in 1976 and became the John-Lennon-Gymnasium in 1994, with approval from Yoko Ono—a unique naming in Germany[1][2][7]. Located in Berlin-Mitte at Zehdenicker Str. 17, 10119 Berlin, the protected historic site was damaged in WWII, restored in 1951/52, and modernized in the 1990s[1]. As a digital pioneer, it benefits from partnerships (e.g., Dell, Epson, Deutsche Telekom) for infrastructure, workshops on coding, career education, and design thinking, as part of a national Smart School network of 21 schools[School Website About Page].

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Gymnasium**, it offers secondary education leading to the **Abitur** (university entrance qualification), typical for grades 5–12/13 in Berlin[School Website Information][1]. The school participates in the Berlin program for deepened vocational orientation (BvBO) and the school experiment "Hybride Formen des Lehrens und Lernens" since 2021/22, integrating hybrid teaching methods[1]. Digital devices are provided from grade 7 via a full-equipment concept[School Website Main Page]. No specific details on languages, MSA, or BBR pathways are extracted from content; the unreadable PDF appears to be curriculum-related but yields no interpretable data[Curriculum Page].

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include a vibrant **Basketball-AG** with NBA training participation, **jlgMUN** (Model United Nations conference, e.g., 2025 event on global challenges with 100 delegates, awarded Förderpreis Stiftung Bildung in 2024), JLG Bandnight, Weihnachtskonzert (Christmas concert), 20 Jahre Filmfest (20th Film Festival anniversary), ERBELEBEN, Berlin Freedom Week visits, and ethics workshops on integration and belonging[School Website Main Page][School Website About Page][1][3][4][5]. Additional offerings: Bücherlisten (book lists), Coach für dich! (coaching program), and parental meetings[School Website Main Page].

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- Awarded **"Exzellente digitale Schule"** by Berlin's Senatsverwaltung für Bildung, Jugend und Familie in April 2019[School Website About Page].
- Named **"Smart School"** by Bitkom in March 2018, as Berlin's first, with national network status and media coverage (Berliner Zeitung, Tagesspiegel, Deutschlandfunk Kultur)[School Website About Page].
- **Umweltschule in Europa – Internationale Agenda Schule** for environmental and climate efforts; UNESCO recognition as UN-Decade project for sustainable development education[1].
- **jlgMUN** received Förderpreis Stiftung Bildung in November 2024 for educational innovation[4].

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** (Öffentlich), enrollment follows Berlin public system processes, typically via local catchment or waiting lists; no tuition, registration fees, or specific requirements detailed[School Website Information]. Open Day (Tag der offenen Tür) held January 14, 2026 (registration closed); first school day for 2025/26 listed, but no current internships available[School Website Main Page]. External schools register for events like jlgMUN via dedicated forms[4].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto "Gemeinsam Schule wagen" (Daring school together) emphasizes collaborative learning and engagement.[2][4] It promotes a positive school climate with friendly, competent teachers and students who enjoy attending, fostering a welcoming atmosphere under principal M. Vehlow's passionate leadership.[1][4] The philosophy highlights continuous innovation in digital teaching, AI literacy, and media competence as central to learning.[4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on October 15, 1853, as a church-affiliated school near St. Nikolai Church, it is one of Berlin's oldest gymnasiums, originally a boys' Latin school; it relocated in 1915, 1972 to Bismarckstraße 54, and expanded in 1999 with a converted furniture store opposite the main building, plus 2003 courtyard containers.[3] Located in Berlin-Spandau district near Altstadt Spandau, it serves about 735 students with a supportive community, including alumni testimonials praising the atmosphere.[2][3][4] Facilities include a main building, new annex, beautiful aula for events, mensa, and virtual 3D tours and fly-throughs.[4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves grades 5-10 (ca. 500 students in 19 classes) and Sekundarstufe II (ca. 200 students, including 95 Abitur candidates), leading to **Abitur** as the primary graduation pathway.[2] Offers four foreign languages: **English** (1st), **Latin/French** (2nd), and **Latin/French/Spanish** (3rd); no explicit mentions of MSA or BBR pathways.[2] Integrates history field trips, digital education, and interdisciplinary projects.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Extracurriculars and exchanges**: France exchange with Collège Pierre Weczerka in Chelles (grades 8/9, Sept/Oct 2025); Democracy AG hosted politician Kassem Taher Saleh; music department's Adventskonzert; grade 11 Christmas market; cinéfête French film outing (7b).[4]  
**Sports**: Annual ski trip to Austrian Alps (30 grade 11 students); cross-country running with multiple podium finishes (e.g., 3x 1st place).[4]  
**Arts and culture**: History visits to Stasi memorial (10e); music and art performances like concerts and blacklight theater.[3][4] Community events build social bonds.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Awarded **fobizz Schule 2025/26** for excellence in digital education, AI literacy, and media competence, recognizing continuous staff engagement with innovative teaching.[4] Strong sports results in Spandau cross-country (e.g., 3x gold, 1x bronze); celebrated 150 years in 2003 and hosted Kant/Einstein lecture series.[3][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free** gymnasium, enrollment is open; prospective families can attend the digital info event on January 23 (18:00) via Webex, followed by self-guided open house on Saturday.[4] Upcoming events include Tag der offenen Tür (open day) with LK-Messe (advanced course fair) on Jan 21 and GEV meeting on Jan 20.[4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Käthe-Kollwitz-Gymnasium embodies the philosophy "Jede Gabe ist eine Aufgabe" (Every gift is a task), inspired by its namesake Käthe Kollwitz, emphasizing holistic development that considers students' personalities, social conditions, learning needs, abilities, and interests to foster personal, social, and academic growth.[2][3][5] It promotes a **mathematisch-naturwissenschaftlich-informationstechnisch** (MINT) profile with creative elements (MINKT), focusing on future-oriented, science-based instruction connected to students' real-world experiences, while recognizing achievement without stigma and providing broad opportunities beyond STEM.[2][5][9] Core values include systematic talent and giftedness promotion integrated into daily teaching and part-time all-day programs, social responsibility, digital education, and support for successful learning through systems like UFO-Team (covering psychology, social work, special education, media pedagogy, prevention, and pastoral care).[1][2][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Pankow district (Prenzlauer Berg) at Dunckerstr. 65-66, 10439 Berlin, the school honors artist Käthe Kollwitz, who lived and worked nearby; it originated from a 1946 girls' school and boys' Pasteur-Oberschule that merged in 1951 as EOS Käthe Kollwitz with a math-science focus, renamed Käthe-Kollwitz-Gymnasium in 2013.[3][6] It serves approximately 891-943 students with 78-85 teachers in a vibrant community emphasizing inclusivity and diversity (e.g., contact person for sexual and gender diversity).[1][4] Facilities include a **360° virtual tour**, library, cafeteria (Mensa), two informatics labs, laptop and computer cabinets, spaces for workshops/open offers, movement breaks, school club, and part-time all-day operations.[2][4][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a Gymnasium (grades 5-12/13 leading to **Abitur**), it offers a standard spectrum with a **MINT (STEM)** profile: increased hours and split classes in math/science from grade 5, continuous natural sciences to grade 7, physics/biology/chemistry from grade 8, mandatory chess and instrumental music in grades 5-6, and profile tracks in grades 9-10 requiring one MINT subject (math, informatics, physics, chemistry, biology).[2][3][5][8] Languages include S1 English/French, S4 English/Spanish, optional Latin; advanced courses (Leistungskurse) in art, biology, chemistry, German, English, geography, history, informatics, math, physics, political education.[1][4] Special programs: gifted promotion (e.g., early university studies, LernAkademien, MINT camps), hybrid learning trials, project-based learning, self-study times, inclusive education, and broad offerings in humanities, arts, sports, ethics/philosophy.[2][4][5][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **extracurriculars** like MINT-Schüler*innenschule (monthly experiments/programming with primary students), MädchenLab (weekly MINT for girls grades 4-6 in experimenting, math, programming, chess), theater (e.g., AI courtroom in English), music ensembles/orchestra/chorus, "Dirigieren lernen!" conducting program, "Schule mit Weltblick" global initiative, and Duke Award.[2][4] Sports and arts include theater, art, music, movement breaks; digital experiences via AI concepts, media pedagogy, robotics/digital worlds/aquaponics electives; social activities like Christmas market fundraisers; chess clubs; and all-day offerings with workshops, library, club.[2][4][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**Certifications and awards**: MINT-Excellence-Center School (one of five Humboldt University network schools), **Exzellente Digitale Schule**, **Deutsche Schachschule** (multiple Berlin/German championships, including 1st in German School Chess Championship), 3rd Prize for AI Overall Concept (national KI school prize), fobizz School 2025/26, MINT-EC School Prize, KlimaVisionen-Schule, quality seals in digital education and chess.[2][3][5] Recent successes: 1st in International Math Team Competition "Bolyai", Bundeswettbewerb Mathematik 1st prizes, International Chemistry Olympiad (top 16 nationally), Mathenacht 2025 wins.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Gymnasium**, enrollment prioritizes grades 5 and 7 via fixed-term applications and forms; oversubscription uses average grade from primary school promotion prognosis.[2][3][4] Open house: January 29, 2026 (15:30-19:00); regional girls' MINT groups for grades 4-6 preview the school.[2][4]
